> - **Strain ($\varepsilon$):** Ratio of change in length to original length ($\Delta L / L$). Dimensionless, often expressed in microstrain ($\mu\varepsilon$).
> - **Gauge Factor (GF):** Sensitivity measure relating fractional resistance change to applied strain.
> - **Bonded vs. Unbonded:** Classifies whether the resistive element is cemented to the flexure or stretched between supports.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Stretchy Rubber Band" Analogy:**
> Imagine stretching a thin rubber band while watching a fine wire glued along its surface. As you pull the rubber band, the wire stretches, becomes thinner, and longer — its electrical resistance increases. A strain gauge works on the **exact same principle** but uses a metallic foil (or semiconductor) instead of a rubber band, and a Wheatstone bridge instead of an ohmmeter to detect the tiny change.
>
> **For displacement:** Picture a diving board (cantilever) at a swimming pool. When a person stands on its free end, the board bends downward. The amount of bend (deflection/displacement) cannot be measured easily, but if you glue a tiny strain gauge near the fixed end (where bending is maximum), the gauge's resistance change tells you *exactly* how much the person caused the board to deflect — without ever touching the free end!

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus of Steel:** $E \approx 200 \text{ GPa} = 2 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$
- **Young's Modulus of Aluminum:** $E \approx 70 \text{ GPa}$
- **Gauge Factor (typical metallic foil):** $GF \approx 2.0$
- **Gauge Factor (typical semiconductor):** $GF \approx 100$ to $200$
- **Standard Gauge Resistance:** $R = 120\ \Omega,\ 350\ \Omega,\ 1000\ \Omega$
- **Standard Bridge Excitation:** $V_{ex} = 5\text{ V to }10\text{ V (DC)}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The strain gauge operates on the principle that **the electrical resistance of a conductor changes when it is mechanically deformed**.

1. **Initial Resistance ($R$):** A strain gauge is essentially a long, thin conductive wire or foil folded in a grid pattern. Its base resistance is given by the standard conductor formula:
   $$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$
   where $\rho$ is resistivity, $L$ is length, and $A$ is cross-sectional area.

2. **Application of Strain ($\varepsilon$):** When the parent structure deforms, the gauge deforms identically (being bonded to it). The length changes by $\Delta L$, area changes by $\Delta A$, and resistivity changes by $\Delta \rho$.

3. **Fractional Change in Resistance ($\Delta R / R$):** Differentiating the resistance equation and substituting the Poisson effect (transverse contraction):
   $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A} + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

4. **Gauge Factor Definition:** Combining all strain dependencies into a single constant:
   $$G_f = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$
   where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.

5. **Wheatstone Bridge Conversion:** The tiny $\Delta R$ is converted to a measurable voltage using a **Wheatstone bridge**:
   $$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} \right)$$

6. **Inference of Displacement:** For a cantilever beam, the strain at the surface is linked to free-end deflection by beam-bending theory:
   $$\varepsilon = \frac{6\, t\,\delta}{L^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = \frac{\varepsilon\, L^2}{6t}$$
   where $t$ is beam thickness, $L$ is beam length, and $\delta$ is free-end deflection (displacement).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Base resistance of conductor | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $G_f = (\Delta R/R) / \varepsilon$ | Gauge Factor (definition) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $G_f = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ | Gauge Factor (material physics) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Strain (definition) | dimensionless (or $\mu\varepsilon$) |
| 5 | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (stress-strain) | Pa |
| 6 | $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Output voltage (quarter bridge) | V |
| 7 | $V_o = (V_{ex}/2) \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Output voltage (half bridge) | V |
| 8 | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Output voltage (full bridge) | V |
| 9 | $\delta = 4PL^3 / (E b t^3)$ | Cantilever free-end deflection | m |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{max} = 6t\delta / L^2$ | Max surface strain (cantilever) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\delta = \varepsilon L^2 / (6t)$ | Displacement from strain (inverse) | m |
| 12 | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ | Temperature-induced resistance drift | $\Omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Boards:** *"Rho L over A, delta R over R, Gf times strain, Ve over four — and you've got the score!"*

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Wing and fuselage deformation monitoring on aircraft (both aluminum and composite structures).
- **Civil Engineering:** Load testing of bridges, dams, and skyscrapers; structural health monitoring (SHM).
- **Automotive:** Crash-test dummies, suspension travel sensors, torque measurement on drive shafts.
- **Biomedical:** Force plates in gait analysis, force feedback in surgical robots, prosthetic limb sensing.
- **Manufacturing:** Press-force monitoring in stamping, injection-mold cavity pressure feedback.
- **Weighing Systems:** Load cells in industrial scales (typically 4 strain gauges in a full bridge).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Gauge Factor from First Principles

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the Gauge Factor of a metallic strain gauge.

**Given:** A conductor of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ is subjected to an axial strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$.

### Step 1 — Express the initial resistance
We begin with the fundamental resistance of a uniform conductor:
$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$

### Step 2 — Take the total differential
Since $R$ is a function of three variables ($\rho, L, A$), we apply the chain rule for small changes:
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

> **Logic Note:** The negative sign on $dA/A$ arises because $A$ is in the denominator. As the wire stretches, $A$ decreases (Poisson contraction), but $R$ increases.

### Step 3 — Relate $dA/A$ to $dL/L$ using Poisson's ratio
For a cylindrical conductor of diameter $D$, the lateral strain is $\varepsilon_{lat} = dD/D = -\nu \cdot (dL/L) = -\nu\,\varepsilon$. The area $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, so:
$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \frac{dD}{D} = -2\nu\,\varepsilon = -2\nu \frac{dL}{L}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute back
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \left(-2\nu \frac{dL}{L}\right) = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} + 2\nu \frac{dL}{L}$$

### Step 5 — Group and divide by strain
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{dL}{L}\left(1 + 2\nu + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{dL/L}\right) = \varepsilon\left(1 + 2\nu + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Define Gauge Factor
$$\boxed{\,G_f = \frac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}\,}$$

> **Interpretation:**
> - The term $1$ arises from the **length change**.
> - The term $2\nu$ arises from the **area change** (Poisson effect).
> - The term $\Delta \rho / \rho \varepsilon$ is the **piezoresistive effect**. For metals it is small and roughly constant; for semiconductors it is dominant and gives very high $G_f$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Cantilever Beam Displacement from Strain

**Statement:** For a cantilever beam of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $t$, derive the relationship between free-end displacement $\delta$ and surface strain $\varepsilon$.

### Step 1 — Cantilever free-end deflection under end load $P$
From standard strength-of-materials (Euler-Bernoulli beam theory):
$$\delta = \frac{P L^3}{3 E I}$$

### Step 2 — Moment of inertia of a rectangular cross-section
$$I = \frac{b t^3}{12}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $I$
$$\delta = \frac{P L^3}{3 E \left(\dfrac{b t^3}{12}\right)} = \frac{4 P L^3}{E b t^3}$$

### Step 4 — Maximum bending moment at fixed end
$$M_{max} = P L$$

### Step 5 — Maximum bending stress at the surface
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{P L \cdot (t/2)}{b t^3 / 12} = \frac{6 P L}{b t^2}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Hooke's Law
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{E} = \frac{6 P L}{E b t^2}$$

### Step 7 — Eliminate $P$ between $\delta$ and $\varepsilon$ equations
From Step 3: $P = \dfrac{\delta E b t^3}{4 L^3}$.

Substitute into Step 6:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{6 L}{E b t^2} \cdot \frac{\delta E b t^3}{4 L^3} = \frac{6 t \,\delta}{4 L^2} = \frac{3 t \,\delta}{2 L^2}$$

> **Correction Note:** The conventional formula (when the strain gauge is placed at the top/bottom surface and we use the *full* gauge reading accounting for the bending) is often written as:
$$\boxed{\,\delta = \frac{2 L^2 \varepsilon}{3 t} = \frac{\varepsilon L^2}{1.5\,t}\,}$$
>
> A more common textbook form (with strain measured on the extreme fiber) is:
$$\boxed{\,\delta = \frac{2 L^2 \varepsilon}{3 t}\,}$$

### Step 8 — Numerical sanity check
For $L = 0.1$ m, $t = 0.001$ m, $\varepsilon = 1000\ \mu\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$:
$$\delta = \frac{2 (0.1)^2 (10^{-3})}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.67 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 6.67 \text{ mm}$$

This is a reasonable, easily measurable displacement — confirming the formula's validity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage

**Statement:** Derive the output voltage of a quarter-bridge strain gauge circuit.

### Step 1 — Bridge balance condition (initial state)
With $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$, the bridge is balanced and $V_o = 0$.

### Step 2 — Active gauge changes by $\Delta R$
Let $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ and $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$.

### Step 3 — Apply voltage divider to each branch
$$V_{left} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}$$

$$V_{right} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

### Step 4 — Output voltage
$$V_o = V_{left} - V_{right} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2} - V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} \right]$$

### Step 5 — Find common denominator
$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{(2R + \Delta R) - 2(R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{-\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

### Step 6 — Approximation: $\Delta R \ll R$
For typical strain gauges, $\Delta R$ is on the order of $0.1\%$ of $R$. So $2R + \Delta R \approx 2R$:
$$V_o \approx -V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$$

### Step 7 — Substitute $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$
$$\boxed{\,V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon\,}$$

> **Key Insight:** Doubling the bridge configurations (half bridge) doubles sensitivity to $V_{ex}/2 \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$; full bridge gives $V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$. This is why industrial load cells use full-bridge configurations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Strain Gauge Displacement Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unknown Displacement d] --> B[Elastic Element Cantilever Beam]
    B --> C[Mechanical Strain e induced on surface]
    C --> D[Bonded Foil Strain Gauge]
    D --> E[Resistance Change dR]
    E --> F[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    F --> G[Differential Output Voltage Vo]
    G --> H[Amplifier and Signal Conditioner]
    H --> I[ADC and Microcontroller]
    I --> J[Digital Display / Recorder]
    K[DC Excitation Vex] --> F
    L[Temperature Compensation dummy gauge] --> F
```

## 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q[Quarter Bridge]
        Q1[One active gauge]
    end
    subgraph H[Half Bridge]
        H1[Two active gauges: one in tension, one in compression]
    end
    subgraph F[Full Bridge]
        F1[Four active gauges: two tension, two compression]
    end
    Q --> H --> F
    Q1 -->|Sensitivity Gf*e*Vex/4| Qnote
    H1 -->|Sensitivity Gf*e*Vex/2| Hnote
    F1 -->|Sensitivity Gf*e*Vex| Fnote
```

## 4.3 Strain Gauge Foil Pattern (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Strain Gauge Foil Pattern] --> P1[Longitudinal current-carrying loops - aligned with strain axis]
    P1 --> P2[End loops - perpendicular turnarounds - minimize transverse sensitivity]
    P2 --> P3[Solder tabs at both ends - connection to lead wires]
    P3 --> P4[Backing carrier - polyimide or epoxy - transfers strain from host to foil]
```

## 4.4 Cantilever Beam with Strain Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Fixed Wall] -->|Clamped| B[Cantilever Beam of length L, thickness t]
    B -->|Free end displacement d| M[Movable Tip]
    G[Strain Gauge bonded at distance L from fixed end] -.Senses maximum surface strain.-> B
    B -->|Bending moment M = P x L| BM[Bending Stress]
    BM -->|Hooke law| E[Strain e]
    E -->|Bridge circuit| V[Output Vo proportional to d]
```

## 4.5 Bonded vs. Unbonded Strain Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BG[Bonded Type]
        BG1[Foil wire cemented directly to elastic element]
        BG2[Used in load cells, pressure sensors]
    end
    subgraph UG[Unbonded Type]
        UG1[Wire stretched between fixed and movable posts on a frame]
        UG2[Higher sensitivity, used in precision transducers]
    end
    BG --> UG
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** Always draw the Wheatstone bridge with the excitation $V_{ex}$ on the top and bottom nodes and the output $V_o$ across the left and right nodes. This convention matches textbook diagrams and saves examiner time.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define Gauge Factor of a strain gauge. What is its typical value for a metallic foil gauge?**

**Model Answer:**
The Gauge Factor ($G_f$) is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in resistance to the applied strain:
$$G_f = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$
For a metallic foil strain gauge, the typical value is **$G_f \approx 2.0$**. For semiconductor strain gauges, $G_f$ is much higher, ranging from **100 to 200**.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for typical values — examiner key)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Why is a Wheatstone bridge used with strain gauges? Mention the role of a dummy gauge.**

**Model Answer:**
A Wheatstone bridge is used because the fractional resistance change in a strain gauge is extremely small (of the order of $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$). The bridge converts this tiny $\Delta R$ into a measurable differential voltage. A **dummy gauge** (placed on an unstrained arm of the bridge) provides **temperature compensation**, since resistance changes due to temperature appear equally in both active and dummy arms and thus cancel out at the output.

*(1.5 marks for bridge justification, 1.5 marks for dummy gauge role)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Strain Gauge Physics Focus)*

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a strain gauge starting from $R = \rho L / A$. Explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Base resistance of conductor:
$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A} \quad \text{...(1 mark for writing this)}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiating logarithmically:
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3:** For cylindrical wire with diameter $D$, lateral strain is $-\nu (dL/L)$. Since $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, we get $dA/A = 2 dD/D = -2\nu (dL/L)$. Substituting:
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\frac{dL}{L} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** Dividing by strain $\varepsilon = dL/L$:
$$G_f = \frac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho/\rho}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5 — Significance of each term:** *(1 mark)*
- **1** → contribution from change in length.
- **$2\nu$** → contribution from Poisson contraction of area.
- **$\Delta\rho/\rho\varepsilon$** → piezoresistive effect (dominant in semiconductors).

#### **(b) [7 Marks] A strain gauge has $R = 350\ \Omega$ and $G_f = 2.1$. It is bonded to a steel cantilever ($E = 200$ GPa) of length $L = 0.15$ m and thickness $t = 2$ mm. Find the bridge output voltage for a free-end displacement of 0.5 mm. Use $V_{ex} = 10$ V in a quarter-bridge configuration.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute strain at gauge location:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{3 t\, \delta}{2 L^2} = \frac{3 \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})}{2 \times (0.15)^2}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{3 \times 10^{-6}}{0.045} = 6.67 \times 10^{-5} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Fractional change in resistance:**
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-5} = 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Quarter-bridge output voltage:**
$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{10}{4} \times 2.1 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$V_o = 2.5 \times 1.4 \times 10^{-4} = 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 0.35 \text{ mV} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Stress check (for credit):**
$$\sigma = E \varepsilon = 200 \times 10^9 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-5} = 13.33 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

This is well within the elastic limit of mild steel (~$250$ MPa), confirming linear behaviour.

**Step 5 — Comment:** *(1 mark)*
The output voltage is very small (sub-millivolt), demonstrating why strain gauge signals always require **high-gain instrumentation amplifiers** (e.g., INA128, AD620) in practical instrumentation.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Application & Cantilever Focus)*

#### **(a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat diagram, explain how a strain gauge can be used for displacement measurement using a cantilever beam arrangement. Derive the relevant formula.**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (reproduce in exam):**
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Fixed Support] --> B[Cantilever Beam: length L, thickness t]
    B --> M[Free End with probe - touches object being measured]
    G[Strain Gauge bonded near fixed end on top surface] -.measures surface strain.-> B
    B -->|deflection d| M
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- The unknown displacement is applied to the free end of the cantilever via a probe.
- The beam bends, producing surface strain that is maximum near the fixed end.
- The strain gauge bonded to the surface converts this strain into a resistance change.
- The Wheatstone bridge converts $\Delta R$ into a proportional voltage.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
- From beam theory: $\delta = 4 P L^3 / (E b t^3)$ ...(1 mark)
- Maximum bending stress: $\sigma = 6 P L / (b t^2)$ ...(1 mark)
- Strain: $\varepsilon = \sigma / E = 6 P L / (E b t^2)$ ...(1 mark)
- Eliminate $P$ to obtain: $\delta = (2 L^2 \varepsilon) / (3 t)$ ...(1 mark)

#### **(b) [7 Marks] A quarter-bridge strain gauge circuit uses a gauge of resistance 120 Ω and gauge factor 2.05. The bridge is excited by 5 V DC. The measured output voltage is 2.5 mV. Determine (i) the strain, (ii) the change in resistance, and (iii) the stress on the gauge if it is bonded to a steel beam ($E = 200$ GPa).**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Strain:** *(2.5 marks)*
$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon = \frac{4 V_o}{V_{ex} \cdot G_f}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 2.05} = \frac{0.01}{10.25} = 9.756 \times 10^{-4} = 975.6\ \mu\varepsilon$$

**(ii) Change in resistance:** *(2.5 marks)*
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 2.05 \times 9.756 \times 10^{-4} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = R \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} = 120 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.24\ \Omega$$

**(iii) Stress on the gauge:** *(2 marks)*
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = 200 \times 10^9 \times 9.756 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\sigma = 1.951 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 195.1 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit trap:** Always express strain in dimensionless form first, then convert to $\mu\varepsilon$ only at the final step. Many students write $\varepsilon = 975.6$ and lose a mark.
> 2. **Bridge factor confusion:** Students often use $V_{ex}/2$ (half-bridge formula) instead of $V_{ex}/4$ (quarter-bridge). Always read the problem carefully for "quarter", "half", or "full" bridge.
> 3. **Sign convention:** Strain may be tensile (positive) or compressive (negative). Mention the sign in your answer to earn full credit.
> 4. **Formula misapplication:** Many students incorrectly use $\delta = \varepsilon L^2 / (6t)$ instead of $\delta = 2 L^2 \varepsilon / (3t)$ (missing factor of 2 vs 6 in denominator). Verify by dimensionally checking!
> 5. **Forgetting the gauge factor:** A direct $\Delta R / R$ substitution without the $G_f$ step is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 (Strain Gauges):**
>
> ✅ **Gauge Factor** = $(\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$. Typical value: 2.0 (metal), 100–200 (semiconductor).
>
> ✅ **Quarter-bridge output:** $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) G_f \varepsilon$. Sensitivity is 4× lower than full bridge.
>
> ✅ **Full-bridge output:** $V_o = V_{ex} G_f \varepsilon$ — used in industrial load cells for maximum sensitivity.
>
> ✅ **Cantilever displacement formula:** $\delta = 2 L^2 \varepsilon / (3 t)$ (with gauge on extreme fiber).
>
> ✅ **Hooke's Law** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) is always valid *within the elastic limit*. Verify that computed stress is below yield strength of the material.
>
> ✅ **Dummy gauge** in the adjacent arm of the bridge provides **temperature compensation** and also **non-linearity cancellation**.
>
> ✅ **Poisson's ratio for steel** ≈ 0.30, for aluminum ≈ 0.33. This affects the $2\nu$ term of gauge factor.
>
> ✅ **Strain gauge types:** Bonded (foil, semiconductor), Unbonded (wire stretched between posts). Bonded types dominate modern instrumentation.
>
> ✅ **Common uses for displacement sensing:** Probes in coordinate measuring machines (CMM), dial indicators with electronic output, force-displacement testing rigs.
>
> ✅ **Always check the ratio $t/L$** — small thickness and long beam = higher displacement for a given strain → higher sensitivity displacement sensor.
>
> ✅ **Signal conditioning is mandatory:** Output is in mV; use **instrumentation amplifier** (INA128/AD620) with gain of 100–1000 before ADC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductance Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Inductance Type ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Inductance Type Transducers** are passive electrical transducers that convert a mechanical input quantity — such as linear displacement, angular displacement, force, or pressure — into a measurable change in **self-inductance** or **mutual inductance** of a coil circuit, using the principles of electromagnetic induction and variation of magnetic reluctance. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic is foundational for Module 2 (Displacement and Position Measurement). Mastery of **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is *mandatory*, as it is a guaranteed 14-mark question in End Semester Examinations. Mathematically, the induced EMF in a coil due to changing current linkage is governed by Faraday's Law: $$e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt} = -\frac{d(LI)}{dt}$$ Where $L$ is the inductance in **Henry (H)**, $N$ is the number of turns, $\phi$ is the magnetic flux in **Weber (Wb)**, and $I$ is the current in **Ampere (A)**. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plunger & Water Tap" Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy (Read this first if LVDT is new to you!):** > > Imagine you have a **water tap** connected to a pump. The flow of water depends on how *open* the tap is. > > - In an **Inductance Transducer**, the **magnetic flux** is like water. > - The **iron core** sliding inside the coil is like the **tap handle**. > - When the core moves **further inside** the coil, it "opens the tap wider" — the magnetic flux increases, the inductance rises. > - When the core moves **outward**, the flux decreases, the inductance drops. > > The clever part: by using *two* secondary coils arranged in **opposition** (like a tug-of-war), the device compares the two signals. If both are equal, output is zero (null position). Any movement creates an imbalance — like a weighing scale tipping to one side — that is proportional to the displacement. --- ### 1.3 Classification Overview Inductance-type transducers are broadly classified into **two families**: | Family | Mechanism | Key Device | |--------|-----------|------------| | **Self-Inductance Type** | Change in *own* inductance of a single coil | Variable Reluctance Pickup | | **Mutual-Inductance Type** | Change in coupling between *two* coils | **LVDT**, RVDT | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic to remember:** "S**e**lf = **o**ne coil"; "**M**utual = **t**wo coils" → S e o / M t t ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Self-Inductance Type Transducers The self-inductance of a coil is given by the fundamental relation: $$L = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{N^2 \mu A}{l}$$ Where: - $L$ = Inductance in **Henry** - $N$ = Number of turns (dimensionless) - $\mathcal{R}$ = Reluctance of the magnetic path in **At/Wb** - $\mu$ = Permeability of the core medium in **H/m** - $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the core in **m²** - $l$ = Length of the magnetic path in **m** The reluctance $\mathcal{R}$ is the magnetic analogue of electrical resistance: $$\mathcal{R} = \frac{l}{\mu A}$$ #### Sub-Types of Self-Inductance Transducers The change in inductance is achieved by varying one of three parameters: **l, A, or μ**. **(a) Variable Reluctance Type (Change in Air-Gap Length `l_g`)** - The iron armature attached to the measurand moves relative to a fixed core, changing the air gap. - Most commonly used configuration. **(b) Variable Area Type (Change in `A`)** - The overlap area between the armature and the pole pieces changes with displacement. - Produces a **linear** output over a wider range than air-gap type. **(c) Variable Permeability Type (Change in `μ`)** - The measurand alters the position of a magnetic shunt, changing the effective permeability of the flux path. --- ### 2.2 Mutual-Inductance Type — LVDT (Detailed) **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is the most important transducer in this family. It operates on the **transformer principle** with a movable ferromagnetic core. #### 2.2.1 Construction The LVDT consists of: 1. **One Primary Coil (P)** — centrally located, energized by AC excitation. 2. **Two Secondary Coils (S₁ and S₂)** — wound on either side of the primary, with **equal number of turns**, and connected in **series opposition**. 3. **A Movable Soft-Iron Core** — coupled to the measurand (displacement input). 4. **A Shielded Housing** — provides electrostatic and magnetic shielding. #### 2.2.2 Working Principle - Primary is excited by an AC source: $V_p = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. - A.C. flux is set up in the core, which links with both secondaries. - **EMFs** $E_{s1}$ and $E_{s2}$ are induced in S₁ and S₂, respectively. - Since the secondaries are in **opposition**, the net output is: $$E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$$ - **Null Position** (core centered): $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ ⇒ $E_o = 0$ - **Core displaced toward S₁**: $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ ⇒ $E_o$ is **positive** and proportional to $x$. - **Core displaced toward S₂**: $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$ ⇒ $E_o$ is **negative** and proportional to $x$. #### 2.2.3 Phase-Sensitive Detection (Critical for KTU) A **Phase Sensitive Demodulator** is used to determine the *direction* of displacement: - Positive $x$ → output is in-phase with primary excitation - Negative $x$ → output is **180° out-of-phase** with primary excitation - After demodulation and filtering, a **DC output voltage** is produced whose **magnitude and polarity** indicate the magnitude and direction of displacement. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print This!) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for the KTU Board Exam** | # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Key Condition | |---|----------|----------|-------|---------------| | 1 | Self-Inductance | $L = N^2 \mu A / l$ | H | General solenoid | | 2 | Reluctance | $\mathcal{R} = l / (\mu A)$ | At/Wb | Magnetic Ohm's law | | 3 | Inductance (with air gap) | $L = N^2 \mu_0 A / l_g$ | H | Air gap dominates ($\mu_{iron} \gg \mu_0$) | | 4 | LVDT Output Voltage | $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ | V | Series-opposition connection | | 5 | LVDT Linear Output | $V_{out} = S \cdot x$ | V | $x$ is displacement from null in m | | 6 | Sensitivity | $S = dV_{out} / dx$ | V/m or V/mm | Slope of linear region | | 7 | LVDT EMF in Secondary | $E_{s} = -M \cdot (dI_p/dt)$ | V | Mutual inductance coupling | | 8 | Null Voltage | $V_{null} \approx 0$ | V | Residual voltage due to imperfect winding | | 9 | Change in L (variable gap) | $\Delta L \approx N^2 \mu_0 A \cdot \Delta l_g / l_g^2$ | H | For small $\Delta l_g$ | | 10 | Operating Frequency | $f = 50 \text{ Hz} \text{ to } 10 \text{ kHz}$ | Hz | Typical carrier frequency | > [!NOTE] > In the table above, observe the use of `\vert` for "per" — we use `$\text{V/m}$" (slash notation) instead of `\vert V/m \vert` to keep the markdown table parser happy. The actual *value* of sensitivity can be positive or negative (sign indicates direction). --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility LVDTs are ubiquitous in: - **Aerospace:** Flight control surface position feedback, landing-gear actuation. - **Hydraulic Systems:** Cylinder piston position in CNC machines and earth-movers. - **Civil Engineering:** Long-stroke displacement monitoring in bridges and dams. - **Process Industries:** Valve position feedback in chemical reactors. - **Automotive:** Throttle position, suspension travel sensors. **Why LVDT is preferred in industry:** Infinite resolution (no friction), excellent linearity, robust construction, and inherent immunity to position-based loading errors. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inductance of a Coil with Air Gap Consider a magnetic circuit consisting of an iron core of length $l_i$ and permeability $\mu_i$, with an air gap of length $l_g$ and permeability $\mu_0$. The cross-sectional area is $A$ (assumed constant). **Step 1:** Total reluctance of the series magnetic path is the sum of iron and air-gap reluctances: $$\mathcal{R}_{total} = \mathcal{R}_{iron} + \mathcal{R}_{gap} = \frac{l_i}{\mu_i A} + \frac{l_g}{\mu_0 A}$$ **Step 2:** Because $\mu_i \gg \mu_0$ (typically $\mu_i \approx 5000 \mu_0$ for soft iron), the term $\frac{l_i}{\mu_i A}$ becomes negligible compared to $\frac{l_g}{\mu_0 A}$. **Step 3:** Apply the dominant-term approximation: $$\mathcal{R}_{total} \approx \frac{l_g}{\mu_0 A}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute into the inductance formula $L = N^2 / \mathcal{R}_{total}$: $$\begin{aligned} L &= \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}_{total}} \\ &= \frac{N^2}{\dfrac{l_g}{\mu_0 A}} \\ &= \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{l_g} \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** The inductance of a coil with a dominant air gap is **inversely proportional** to the air-gap length. A small decrease in $l_g$ produces a large increase in $L$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sensitivity of Variable Reluctance Transducer We want the rate of change of inductance with respect to air-gap length. **Step 1:** Start with the expression derived above: $$L = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{l_g}$$ **Step 2:** Differentiate $L$ with respect to $l_g$: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{dL}{dl_g} &= \frac{d}{dl_g} \left( N^2 \mu_0 A \cdot l_g^{-1} \right) \\ &= N^2 \mu_0 A \cdot (-1) \cdot l_g^{-2} \\ &= -\frac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{l_g^{\,2}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** The negative sign indicates that inductance *decreases* as the gap *increases*. The **magnitude of sensitivity** is: $$\left| S_{\text{rel}} \right| = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{l_g^{\,2}}$$ **Conclusion:** Sensitivity is **inversely proportional to the square of the air gap**. Smaller gaps → dramatically higher sensitivity, but reduced linear range. This is a fundamental design trade-off. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Voltage of an LVDT This is the **most important derivation** for KTU. We model LVDT as two coupled transformers sharing a common primary. **Step 1:** Primary excitation. The primary coil is energized by a sinusoidal voltage: $$V_p(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 2:** Primary current. The primary draws a current $I_p$ that lags $V_p$ by phase angle $\phi_p$ due to primary inductance and resistance: $$I_p(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ Where $I_m = V_m / Z_p$ and $Z_p = \sqrt{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2}$. **Step 3:** Induced EMFs in the two secondaries. By Faraday's law of mutual induction: $$E_{s1}(t) = -M_1 \frac{dI_p}{dt}$$ $$E_{s2}(t) = -M_2 \frac{dI_p}{dt}$$ Where $M_1$ and $M_2$ are the mutual inductances between the primary and secondary 1 / secondary 2, respectively. **Step 4:** Compute the derivative of primary current: $$\frac{dI_p}{dt} = I_m \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ **Step 5:** Substitute back into the EMF equations: $$E_{s1}(t) = -M_1 \cdot I_m \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ $$E_{s2}(t) = -M_2 \cdot I_m \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ **Step 6:** Net differential output. The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, so: $$E_o(t) = E_{s1}(t) - E_{s2}(t)$$ $$\begin{aligned} E_o(t) &= -M_1 I_m \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi_p) - \left( -M_2 I_m \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi_p) \right) \\ &= -(M_1 - M_2) \cdot I_m \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi_p) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7:** Displacement dependence. For small displacements $x$ from the null position, the difference in mutual inductances is linearly proportional to $x$: $$M_1 - M_2 = K \cdot x$$ Where $K$ is a constant determined by coil geometry and core properties. **Step 8:** Final output equation: $$E_o(t) = -K \cdot x \cdot I_m \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ **Step 9:** The RMS value of the output is: $$E_{o,\text{rms}} = K \cdot x \cdot I_{m,\text{rms}} \cdot \omega = S \cdot x$$ Where $S = K \cdot I_{m,\text{rms}} \cdot \omega$ is the **sensitivity of the LVDT** (in V/m). **Conclusion:** The LVDT output is **linearly proportional to displacement**, and its phase indicates direction — a textbook example of a ratiometric, bi-directional transducer. --- ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (For Practice) > **Problem:** A variable reluctance transducer has a coil of 200 turns wound on a core of cross-sectional area $A = 4 \text{ cm}^2$ and air gap $l_g = 2 \text{ mm}$. Calculate (a) the inductance at null position, and (b) the sensitivity. Given $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$. **Solution:** **Part (a):** $L = N^2 \mu_0 A / l_g$ $$L = \frac{(200)^2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (4 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$L = \frac{40000 \cdot 5.0265 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.0106 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$L = 1.005 \times 10^{-2} \text{ H} = 10.05 \text{ mH}$$ **Part (b):** Sensitivity $\vert S \vert = N^2 \mu_0 A / l_g^2$ $$\vert S \vert = \frac{(200)^2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (4 \times 10^{-4})}{(2 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$ $$\vert S \vert = \frac{2.0106 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.027 \text{ H/m}$$ **Conclusion:** The inductance is $\approx 10$ mH and sensitivity is $\approx 5$ H/m. Note how a small gap produces a relatively large sensitivity. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 LVDT — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional signal flow** within an LVDT measurement system: ```mermaid graph LR AC[AC Excitation
Source 1-10 kHz] Pri[Primary Coil P
Np turns] Core[Soft Iron Core
Coupled to Measurand] S1[Secondary Coil S1
Ns1 turns] S2[Secondary Coil S2
Ns2 turns] Series[Series Opposition
Connection] Diff[Differential
EMF Eo Es1 minus Es2] Demod[Phase Sensitive
Demodulator] Filter[Low Pass
Filter] Amp[DC Amplifier] Out[Final DC Output
Vout Sx] AC --> Pri Pri --> Core Core --> S1 Core --> S2 S1 --> Series S2 --> Series Series --> Diff Diff --> Demod Demod --> Filter Filter --> Amp Amp --> Out ``` **Reading the diagram:** The AC source energizes the primary. The movable core modulates the flux linking S1 and S2. The series-opposition connection subtracts the two EMFs, producing a differential output. The phase-sensitive demodulator resolves the direction of motion, the filter removes carrier ripple, and the amplifier provides a clean DC voltage proportional to displacement. --- ### 4.2 Variable Reluctance Transducer — Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TB Meas[Measurand
Displacement or Force] Arm[Movable
Ferromagnetic Armature] Core[Fixed
Iron Core] Coil[Energizing Coil
N turns] Agap[Variable
Air Gap Lg] Lout[Inductance L
equals N squared mu0 A by Lg] Meas --> Arm Arm --> Agap Agap --> Lout Core --> Agap Coil --> Lout ``` **Reading the diagram:** The measurand (e.g., a force pushing on a diaphragm) moves the armature, changing the air-gap length $l_g$, which directly modulates the inductance $L$ of the coil. The change in $L$ is measured by an AC bridge or oscillator circuit. --- ### 4.3 LVDT Output Characteristics — Qualitative Plot (Schematic) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Linear Region direction LR NegX[Core displaced
toward S2] Null[Null Position
Eo equals 0] PosX[Core displaced
toward S1] end NegX --> Null Null --> PosX ``` > [!TIP] > **Visualizing the LVDT output curve:** Imagine a **straight line** passing through the origin. For positive displacement $x$ (core toward S1), the output voltage rises linearly with a positive slope. For negative $x$ (core toward S2), the output goes negative with the *same* slope. The slope of this line is the **sensitivity $S$** of the LVDT. Outside the linear range, the curve bends (nonlinearity error). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]** Define an **inductance-type transducer**. List its classifications with one example each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** An inductance-type transducer is a passive transducer that converts a mechanical input (displacement, force, pressure) into a change in the inductance of an electrical coil, which can then be measured using an AC bridge or oscillator circuit. **Classifications:** | Type | Operating Principle | Example | |------|--------------------|---------| | Self-Inductance Type | Change in own coil inductance $L$ | Variable Reluctance Pickup | | Mutual-Inductance Type | Change in coupling between two coils | LVDT, RVDT | *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for table + 1 mark for examples)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]** State the **principle of operation of an LVDT** and explain the term **"null position"**. **Model Answer (3 marks):** LVDT operates on the principle of a **mutual-inductance variation** between a primary and two secondary windings due to the axial movement of a soft-iron core. The **null position** is the central position of the core where the magnetic coupling to both secondary coils S1 and S2 is **identical**, producing equal and opposite EMFs in them. The differential output voltage $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ is therefore **zero** at the null position. *(1 mark for principle + 2 marks for null position explanation)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE Module 2, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of an LVDT**. Discuss its **output characteristics**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive the **expression for the output voltage** of an LVDT in terms of mutual inductances and primary current. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution (a):** **Construction (3 marks):** The LVDT consists of: - A **cylindrical hollow former** (non-magnetic, non-conductive). - **One primary winding** (P) at the center, connected to an AC source (typically 1–10 kHz, 5–12 V RMS). - **Two identical secondary windings** (S1 and S2) on either side of the primary, connected in **series opposition** (their dot polarities are reversed). - A **movable soft-iron core** (high-permeability, low-coercivity) attached to the measurand via a non-magnetic push rod. - An **outer shield** to block external electromagnetic interference. **Working Principle (2 marks):** - When the core is at the **null (central) position**, the flux linkage to S1 and S2 is equal. The induced EMFs are equal in magnitude but opposite in phase (due to series opposition), so the net output $E_o = 0$. - When the core moves **toward S1** by a displacement $x$, the flux linkage to S1 increases and to S2 decreases. The differential output $E_o$ becomes **positive** and proportional to $x$. - When the core moves **toward S2**, the differential output $E_o$ becomes **negative** and proportional to $x$. - A **phase-sensitive demodulator** followed by a low-pass filter and DC amplifier produces a clean bipolar DC voltage whose magnitude indicates displacement and polarity indicates direction. **Output Characteristics (2 marks):** - The output voltage $V_{out}$ vs displacement $x$ is **linear** over a specified range (typically $\pm 5$ mm to $\pm 25$ mm depending on model). - The slope of the linear region is the **sensitivity** $S$ (V/mm). - Outside the linear range, the curve becomes non-linear due to fringing flux. - A small **residual voltage** appears at the null position due to imperfect winding symmetry and harmonics, typically $< 1\%$ of full-scale output. --- **Model Solution (b):** Refer to **Derivation 3 in Section 3.3** above. The key final equation is: $$E_o(t) = -(M_1 - M_2) \cdot I_m \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ With $M_1 - M_2 = K \cdot x$ for small displacements, the RMS output is: $$E_{o,\text{rms}} = K \cdot x \cdot I_{m,\text{rms}} \cdot \omega = S \cdot x$$ *(7 marks distributed as: 2 marks for primary current, 2 marks for mutual-inductance EMF equations, 2 marks for series-opposition output, 1 mark for displacement proportionality.)* --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE Module 2, 2024 Scheme, Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the **variable reluctance type inductance transducer** with a functional block diagram. Derive its **sensitivity equation**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A variable reluctance transducer has a coil of **N = 500 turns** wound on a magnetic core with air gap $l_g = 1.5$ mm and uniform cross-sectional area $A = 6 \text{ cm}^2$. If the air gap changes by $\Delta l_g = 0.3$ mm due to an applied force, calculate the **change in inductance** $\Delta L$. Take $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution (a):** **Construction & Working (4 marks):** A variable reluctance transducer consists of: - A **ferromagnetic core** of high permeability with a small **air gap** $l_g$. - A **magnetic armature** attached to the measurand, which moves relative to the core, changing $l_g$. - A **coil** of $N$ turns wound on the core, energized by an AC source. The reluctance of the magnetic path is dominated by the air gap: $\mathcal{R} \approx l_g / (\mu_0 A)$. The inductance is therefore $L = N^2 \mu_0 A / l_g$. When the measurand causes the armature to move, $l_g$ changes, modulating $L$ proportionally. The change in $L$ is measured by an **AC bridge** (e.g., Maxwell bridge) or by feeding the coil into an **LC oscillator** and measuring the frequency change. **Sensitivity Derivation (3 marks):** Refer to **Derivation 2 in Section 3.2**: $$\frac{dL}{dl_g} = -\frac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{l_g^{\,2}}$$ The negative sign indicates inverse relationship; the magnitude $\vert dL/dl_g \vert$ is the sensitivity. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** $N = 500$, $l_g = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $A = 6 \text{ cm}^2 = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $\Delta l_g = 0.3 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m. **Step 1 — Inductance at initial gap:** $$L_1 = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{l_g} = \frac{(500)^2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (6 \times 10^{-4})}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$L_1 = \frac{250000 \cdot 7.5398 \times 10^{-10}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.8850 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$L_1 = 0.1257 \text{ H} = 125.66 \text{ mH}$$ **Step 2 — Inductance at modified gap** $l_g' = l_g + \Delta l_g = 1.8$ mm: $$L_2 = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A}{l_g'} = \frac{1.8850 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1047 \text{ H} = 104.72 \text{ mH}$$ **Step 3 — Change in inductance:** $$\Delta L = L_1 - L_2 = 0.1257 - 0.1047 = 0.0210 \text{ H} \approx 21.0 \text{ mH}$$ **Alternative direct calculation** (using the sensitivity formula $\Delta L \approx (N^2 \mu_0 A / l_g^2) \cdot \Delta l_g$): $$\Delta L \approx \frac{(500)^2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (6 \times 10^{-4})}{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2} \cdot (0.3 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$\Delta L \approx \frac{1.8850 \times 10^{-4}}{2.25 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot 0.3 \times 10^{-3} = 83.78 \cdot 0.3 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\Delta L \approx 0.0251 \text{ H} \approx 25.1 \text{ mH}$$ *(Note: The exact and approximate answers differ slightly because the linearization $\Delta L \approx (dL/dl_g) \cdot \Delta l_g$ assumes small $\Delta l_g$. The exact answer $\Delta L = 21.0$ mH is preferred for board-mark credit.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the sensitivity equation $dL/dl_g = -N^2\mu_0 A / l_g^2$ — KTU examiners *will* deduct 1 mark if you omit the sign indicating inverse proportionality. > > 2. **Confusing "null position" with "zero output"**: At the null position, the *differential* output is zero, but each secondary still produces a finite EMF. Write "the differential output $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ is zero", not "the output is zero". > > 3. **Units trap in numericals**: Cross-sectional area must be in **m²** (not cm²), and air gap in **m** (not mm). Many students lose 1 mark by leaving units inconsistent. > > 4. **Series vs Parallel Opposition**: LVDT secondaries are in **series opposition**, not parallel. Mixing this up shows a conceptual gap and costs heavily. > > 5. **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator omission**: A complete LVDT answer must mention the demodulator circuit; without it, the examiner cannot award full marks for "direction detection". --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision — Pin This to Your Wall!** - ✅ **Inductance type transducers** convert mechanical quantity → change in inductance $L$. - ✅ **Two main families:** Self-inductance (single coil) and Mutual-inductance (two coils — LVDT). - ✅ **Core formula:** $L = N^2 \mu_0 A / l_g$ (when air gap dominates). - ✅ **Sensitivity of reluctance transducer:** $\vert S \vert = N^2 \mu_0 A / l_g^2$ — inversely proportional to **square** of gap. - ✅ **LVDT** = **L**inear **V**ariable **D**ifferential **T**ransformer — three coils (1 primary, 2 secondaries) + movable core. - ✅ **Series opposition** of S1 and S2 produces differential output $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$. - ✅ **Null position** → Core centered → $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ → $E_o = 0$. - ✅ **Direction detection** requires **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator** (very important for KTU). - ✅ **Operating AC frequency** is typically **1–10 kHz** (high enough to avoid 50 Hz mains interference). - ✅ **Advantages of LVDT:** Frictionless, infinite resolution, robust, bidirectional, high sensitivity. - ✅ **Disadvantages of LVDT:** Requires AC excitation + signal conditioning, dynamic response limited by carrier frequency. - ✅ **Real-world uses:** Aircraft flight-control feedback, hydraulic cylinder position, valve position, civil-structural monitoring. - ✅ **RVDT** = Rotary version of LVDT; measures **angular displacement** instead of linear. - ✅ **Permeability constant** to remember: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m. - ✅ **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Construction + working of LVDT (7 marks), derivation of output voltage (7 marks) — this is the **guaranteed** 14-mark question pattern.

topics.title: Capacitance Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capacitance Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Capacitance Transducer** (also called a *Capacitive Sensor* or *Variable Capacitance Transducer*) is a passive transducer that converts a measurand — such as displacement, force, pressure, level, or humidity — into a measurable change in electrical **capacitance**. The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is given by the fundamental relationship:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where $\varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space (**≈ 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**), $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the medium, $A$ is the effective overlapping plate area, and $d$ is the distance between the plates. Any physical input that alters one of these three parameters ($\varepsilon_r$, $A$, or $d$) produces a corresponding change in capacitance, which is then conditioned and displayed by an electronic circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Capacitive transducers fall under the category of **passive electrical transducers** because they require an external excitation source (AC voltage) to produce an output. They do not generate electricity on their own — they merely *modulate* an excitation signal in response to the measurand.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Intuition

Imagine two parallel metal plates as **two water buckets facing each other with a thin gap between them**. The "water" represents electrical charge. The ability of this bucket-system to store water (charge) at a given applied pressure (voltage) is its **capacitance**.

- **Increase the plate area** → bigger buckets → more charge stored → capacitance goes **UP**.
- **Move the plates closer** → shorter pipe connecting them → more coupling → capacitance goes **UP**.
- **Insert a denser insulator (like oil) between the plates** → the medium holds charge better → capacitance goes **UP**.

So any physical change that *enlarges the area, narrows the gap, or improves the dielectric* will *increase* capacitance — and that is exactly what the instrument detects and measures.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** |
| Permittivity of air (approx.) | $\varepsilon_{air}$ | **1.0006** (≈ 1) |
| Permittivity of water | $\varepsilon_{water}$ | **≈ 80** |
| Permittivity of oil | $\varepsilon_{oil}$ | **2 to 5** |
| Standard plate materials | — | **Brass, Aluminium, Copper** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In numerical problems, KTU often assumes $\varepsilon_{air} = 1$ and uses $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ unless otherwise stated. Always quote units explicitly in your final answer to fetch the **"Units" mark** in ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A capacitive transducer operates on the principle that the **capacitance of a capacitor is a function of three geometric/dielectric parameters**. The general operating logic is:

1. **Excitation Applied** — A stable high-frequency AC excitation (typically 50 kHz to 1 MHz) or DC-biased AC is applied across the capacitive element.
2. **Measurand Acts on Geometry/Dielectric** — The physical input (force, displacement, level, humidity, etc.) changes one of: gap $d$, overlap area $A$, or dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$.
3. **Capacitance Modulates** — As per $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, the capacitance value changes in a deterministic way.
4. **Signal Conditioning** — The change in capacitance is converted to a change in voltage, current, or frequency using a bridge circuit, oscillator, or charge amplifier.
5. **Output Display** — The conditioned signal is calibrated and displayed as the value of the measurand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three and only three** physical mechanisms exist by which a capacitance transducer can operate — corresponding to the three variables in the fundamental equation. KTU questions almost always test your ability to identify which mechanism is being employed in a given application.

## 2.2 Types of Capacitive Transducers

### (a) Variation in Plate Separation (Gap Variation) — *Most Common*

The distance $d$ between the plates changes due to the applied force, pressure, or displacement. Since $C \propto 1/d$, the relationship is **non-linear (inverse/hyperbolic)**.

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

**Sensitivity (Fringe benefit):** Gap variation gives the **highest sensitivity** among the three methods, which is why most pressure-sensing capacitive transducers use this principle.

### (b) Variation in Overlapping Area

The effective overlapping area $A$ between the plates changes due to lateral displacement. Since $C \propto A$, the relationship is **linear**.

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot w \cdot x}{d}$$

where $w$ is the plate width and $x$ is the lateral displacement. Used for **larger displacement measurement** (a few mm to cm).

### (c) Variation in Dielectric Constant

The dielectric material between the plates is partially replaced or fully changed by the measurand (e.g., liquid level, humidity, thickness of a sheet). Since $C \propto \varepsilon_r$, the relationship is **linear**.

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

Used for **liquid level measurement, humidity sensing, and thickness gauging**.

## 2.3 Differential Capacitor Arrangement

To overcome the non-linearity of gap variation and improve temperature stability, two capacitors are arranged in a **differential configuration**:

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d - \Delta d} \quad ; \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d + \Delta d}$$

The output of a differential arrangement is the **difference ratio**:

$$\frac{C_1 - C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{\Delta d}{d}$$

This gives a **linear relationship** with displacement $\Delta d$ and provides excellent **common-mode rejection** of stray capacitance, temperature drift, and noise — a key KTU exam point.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | **Fundamental** parallel-plate capacitance |
| 2 | $C \propto \dfrac{1}{d}$ | Gap variation — non-linear, high sensitivity |
| 3 | $C \propto A$ | Area variation — linear, used for displacement |
| 4 | $C \propto \varepsilon_r$ | Dielectric variation — used for level/humidity |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{d}$ | **Approximate linearised** sensitivity for small gap changes |
| 6 | $C_{series} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ | Two dielectrics in series (different $\varepsilon_r$) |
| 7 | $\dfrac{C_1 - C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{d}$ | **Differential** output — linearised |
| 8 | $Q = CV$ | Charge relationship — used in charge-amplifier readout |
| 9 | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C}$ | Capacitive reactance — frequency-domain readout |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_0 = \dfrac{1}{36 \pi \times 10^9} \text{ F/m}$ | Permittivity of free space (KTU-shortcut form) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** The variable $d$ in formula 1 is the **plate gap** in **metres**, *not* millimetres. A common mistake is substituting mm directly, giving a capacitance off by a factor of $10^3$. Always convert all units to SI (m, m², F/m) **before** computation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Capacitive transducers are extensively used in:
- **Automotive:** Tyre-pressure monitoring, fuel-level sensing, accelerator-pedal position sensing.
- **Aerospace:** Fuel gauging in aircraft (capacitance-based fuel quantity indicators).
- **Process Industry:** Level measurement of corrosive liquids, slurries, and high-temperature molten metals.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Touchscreens, fingerprint sensors, proximity detectors, humidity sensors in HVAC.
- **Manufacturing:** Non-contact thickness measurement of paper, plastic films, and metal sheets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sensitivity of a Variable-Gap Capacitive Transducer

**Problem setup:** A parallel-plate capacitive transducer has a nominal capacitance $C_0$ at a gap $d_0$. The gap is reduced by a small amount $\Delta d$ due to an applied force. Derive the expression for fractional change in capacitance $\Delta C / C_0$.

**Step 1 — Write the initial capacitance.**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

**Step 2 — Write the changed capacitance when the gap becomes $(d_0 - \Delta d)$.**

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta d}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the change in capacitance $\Delta C = C_1 - C_0$.**

$$\Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta d} - \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

**Step 4 — Take common denominator.**

$$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left[ \frac{d_0 - (d_0 - \Delta d)}{d_0 (d_0 - \Delta d)} \right] = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left[ \frac{\Delta d}{d_0 (d_0 - \Delta d)} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio $\Delta C / C_0$.**

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta d}{d_0 (d_0 - \Delta d)} \times \frac{d_0}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0 - \Delta d}$$

**Step 6 — Linearise for small $\Delta d \ll d_0$ (Taylor expansion approximation).**

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} \quad \text{when } \Delta d \ll d_0$$

**Interpretation:** For **small displacements**, the fractional change in capacitance is approximately equal to the fractional change in gap — giving a **linear** relationship. For larger displacements, the relationship becomes non-linear (hyperbolic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This derivation is the most frequently asked 7-mark analytical question in Module-2. Marks are split typically as: (1) initial setup — 2 marks, (2) algebraic manipulation — 3 marks, (3) linearisation & interpretation — 2 marks.

## 3.2 Derivation: Capacitance with Two Dielectric Layers in Series (Liquid-Level Application)

**Problem setup:** A cylindrical capacitive level sensor has two dielectrics in series — the upper portion of height $h_1$ filled with air ($\varepsilon_1 = 1$) and the lower portion of height $h_2$ filled with the liquid of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_2$. The total height is $H = h_1 + h_2$. Derive the expression for the total capacitance as a function of liquid level $h_2$.

**Step 1 — Treat the two dielectric sections as two capacitors in series.**

$$C_{air} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot 1 \cdot A_{eff}}{h_1} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A_{eff}}{h_1}$$

$$C_{liquid} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_2 A_{eff}}{h_2}$$

**Step 2 — Combine in series.**

$$C_{total} = \frac{C_{air} \cdot C_{liquid}}{C_{air} + C_{liquid}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expressions.**

$$C_{total} = \frac{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A_{eff}}{h_1} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_2 A_{eff}}{h_2}}{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A_{eff}}{h_1} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_2 A_{eff}}{h_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $\varepsilon_0 A_{eff}$ from numerator and denominator.**

$$C_{total} = \frac{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0^2 \varepsilon_2 A_{eff}^2}{h_1 h_2}}{\varepsilon_0 A_{eff} \left( \dfrac{1}{h_1} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_2}{h_2} \right)}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$C_{total} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_2 A_{eff} \cdot h_1}{h_1 h_2 + \varepsilon_2 h_1^2}$$

**Step 6 — Use $h_1 = H - h_2$ to express in terms of liquid level $h_2$ only.**

$$C_{total} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_2 A_{eff} (H - h_2)}{(H - h_2) h_2 + \varepsilon_2 (H - h_2)^2} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_2 A_{eff}}{h_2 + \varepsilon_2 (H - h_2)}$$

**Final Compact Result:**

$$\boxed{C_{total} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_2 A_{eff}}{h_2 + \varepsilon_2 (H - h_2)}}$$

**Interpretation:** When $h_2 = 0$ (empty tank) the formula reduces to $C = \varepsilon_0 A_{eff} / H$ (all air). When $h_2 = H$ (full tank) the formula gives $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_2 A_{eff} / H$ (all liquid). The change between these extremes is used to indicate the **liquid level**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism vs. Application

| Measurand | Mechanism | Variable Changed | Relation | Application Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small displacement (µm) | Gap variation | $d$ | $C \propto 1/d$ | Pressure sensor diaphragm |
| Large displacement (mm–cm) | Area variation | $A$ | $C \propto A$ | Position sensor in machine tools |
| Liquid level | Dielectric variation | $\varepsilon_r$ | $C \propto \varepsilon_r$ | Fuel tank, chemical reactor |
| Humidity | Dielectric variation | $\varepsilon_r$ | $C \propto \varepsilon_r$ | Hygrometer, weather stations |
| Thickness (sheet) | Dielectric variation | $\varepsilon_r$, $A$ | Composite | Paper/plastic film thickness |
| Force / Pressure | Gap variation | $d$ | $C \propto 1/d$ | Load cell, barometer |
| Acceleration | Gap variation (mass on diaphragm) | $d$ | $C \propto 1/d$ | Seismic accelerometer |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three Mechanisms of Capacitive Transduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Input Measurand] --> nodeB{Which Parameter Changes?}
    nodeB -->|Gap d| nodeC[Gap Variation Type]
    nodeB -->|Area A| nodeD[Area Variation Type]
    nodeB -->|Dielectric eps_r| nodeE[Dielectric Variation Type]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Capacitance C Modulated]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[AC Bridge or Oscillator Circuit]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Amplifier and Rectifier]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Output Display in Engineering Units]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Capacitive Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Physical Input Force Pressure Level Humidity] --> node2[Capacitive Sensing Element]
    node2 --> node3[Change in C, d, or A]
    node3 --> node4[High Frequency AC Excitation Source]
    node4 --> node5[AC Bridge or Carrier Amplifier]
    node5 --> node6[Demodulator and Filter]
    node6 --> node7[Linearisation Circuit]
    node7 --> node8[Display and Recorder Unit]
    node8 --> node9[Final Output in Engineering Units]
```

## 4.3 AC Wheatstone Bridge with Differential Capacitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Excitation AC Source Vs] --> node2[Node A Top]
    node1 --> node3[Node B Top]
    node2 --> node4[Fixed Capacitor C1]
    node2 --> node5[Variable Capacitor Cx]
    node3 --> node6[Fixed Capacitor C2]
    node3 --> node7[Variable Capacitor Cy]
    node4 --> node8[Node C Bottom]
    node5 --> node8
    node6 --> node9[Node D Bottom]
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node10[Differential Amplifier]
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11[Output Voltage Vo]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** In a balanced state, $C_1 \cdot C_y = C_2 \cdot C_x$. When the measurand acts on the differential capacitor pair, the bridge becomes **unbalanced**, and the differential amplifier produces a voltage proportional to the imbalance — which is proportional to the measurand.

## 4.4 Signal Conditioning Path for Capacitive Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Capacitive Sensor] --> nodeB[Charge Amplifier or AC Bridge]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Rectifier DC Conversion]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Low Pass Filter Noise Removal]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Amplifier Gain Stage]
    nodeE --> nodeF[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Microcontroller or Display Unit]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State any three advantages of capacitive transducers over inductive transducers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **High sensitivity and resolution** — Capacitive transducers can resolve displacements as small as 10⁻¹⁰ m (sub-nanometre) — far superior to most inductive types.
2. **High input impedance and low power consumption** — They draw negligible current from the measurand, making them ideal for measuring delicate or high-impedance sources.
3. **Good high-frequency response** — They have no magnetic coils or moving heavy masses, allowing faithful response to dynamic inputs up to several hundred kHz.
*(Bonus point: Can measure both static and dynamic quantities; usable in harsh, explosive, or magnetic-interference environments.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the three mechanisms by which a capacitive transducer can convert a physical input into a change in capacitance. Mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mechanism | Variable Changed | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Variation in plate separation (gap) | $d$ | Diaphragm-type pressure sensor |
| Variation in effective overlapping area | $A$ | Linear displacement measurement |
| Variation in dielectric constant | $\varepsilon_r$ | Liquid level or humidity measurement |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module-Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the fractional change in capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitive transducer when one plate is displaced by a small amount $\Delta d$ towards the fixed plate. Show clearly the linearised form for small displacements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel-plate capacitive transducer has plate area $A = 25 \text{ cm}^2$ and an air gap $d = 2 \text{ mm}$. If the gap is reduced by 0.1 mm due to an applied pressure, calculate:
(i) The initial capacitance.
(ii) The new capacitance.
(iii) The percentage change in capacitance.
Take $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m and $\varepsilon_r = 1$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Initial capacitance with gap $d_0$.

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

**Step 2:** New capacitance when gap becomes $(d_0 - \Delta d)$.

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta d}$$

**Step 3:** Change in capacitance.

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_0 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left[ \frac{1}{d_0 - \Delta d} - \frac{1}{d_0} \right] = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d_0 (d_0 - \Delta d)}$$

**Step 4:** Fractional change.

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0 - \Delta d}$$

**Step 5:** For $\Delta d \ll d_0$, applying binomial approximation $(1 - x)^{-1} \approx 1 + x$:

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}$$

Thus, **the fractional change in capacitance is approximately equal to the fractional change in gap** for small displacements, and the relationship is **linear**.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $A = 25 \text{ cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, $d_0 = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\Delta d = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\varepsilon_r = 1$.

**(i) Initial capacitance:**

$$C_0 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 25 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{221.35 \times 10^{-16}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 110.675 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F}$$

$$\boxed{C_0 = 11.0675 \text{ pF} \approx 11.07 \text{ pF}}$$

**(ii) New capacitance with reduced gap $(d_0 - \Delta d) = 1.9 \times 10^{-3}$ m:**

$$C_1 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 25 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_1 = \frac{221.35 \times 10^{-16}}{1.9 \times 10^{-3}} = 116.5 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F}$$

$$\boxed{C_1 = 11.65 \text{ pF}}$$

**(iii) Percentage change in capacitance:**

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \times 100 = \frac{11.65 - 11.07}{11.07} \times 100 = \frac{0.58}{11.07} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \times 100 \approx 5.24\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks):** Failing to convert cm² to m² and mm to m is the *single most common mistake*. Always convert all dimensions to SI base units **before** substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting the linearisation condition (–1 mark):** If the problem says "small displacement", you must explicitly write the linearised form and state the assumption $\Delta d \ll d_0$.
> 3. **Not quoting the final answer in standard form (pF, nF, µF) (–1 mark):** A raw answer of $11.0675 \times 10^{-12}$ F is technically correct but examiners prefer pF notation for readability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the principle of operation of a **capacitive liquid-level measurement** system. Derive the expression for the total capacitance as a function of liquid height when the liquid acts as the dielectric between two concentric cylinders. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A capacitive humidity sensor uses a hygroscopic dielectric ($\varepsilon_r = 1$ when dry, $\varepsilon_r = 5$ when fully wet) of thickness $d = 0.5$ mm and plate area $A = 4 \text{ cm}^2$. Calculate the capacitance in both dry and wet conditions, and the sensitivity in pF per unit change in $\varepsilon_r$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Capacitive Liquid-Level Measurement (7 marks):**

**Construction:** Two concentric metal cylinders of radii $r_1$ (inner) and $r_2$ (outer) are placed vertically inside the tank. The space between them is filled from the bottom with the liquid (dielectric $\varepsilon_2$) and the upper portion contains air ($\varepsilon_1 = 1$).

**Working Principle:** As the liquid level rises, more of the inter-electrode space is occupied by the high-$\varepsilon$ liquid, increasing the total capacitance between the cylinders. The total capacitance is the **series combination** of the air-column capacitor and the liquid-column capacitor.

**Derivation:** Let total length of cylinder be $H$, liquid level be $h$, so air column = $H - h$.

Capacitance of air-filled portion (cylindrical capacitor):

$$C_{air} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (H - h)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

Capacitance of liquid-filled portion:

$$C_{liq} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_2 h}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

Total (series combination):

$$\frac{1}{C_T} = \frac{1}{C_{air}} + \frac{1}{C_{liq}} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0} \left[ \frac{1}{H - h} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2 h} \right]$$

$$\boxed{C_T = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_2 h (H - h)}{\varepsilon_2 h + (H - h)}}$$

**Part (b) — Humidity Sensor Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $d = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, $A = 4 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.

**(i) Dry condition ($\varepsilon_r = 1$):**

$$C_{dry} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{35.416 \times 10^{-16}}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{C_{dry} = 7.083 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 7.083 \text{ pF}}$$

**(ii) Wet condition ($\varepsilon_r = 5$):**

$$C_{wet} = 5 \times C_{dry} = 5 \times 7.083 = 35.415 \text{ pF}$$

$$\boxed{C_{wet} = 35.42 \text{ pF}}$$

**(iii) Sensitivity:**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta \varepsilon_r} = \frac{C_{wet} - C_{dry}}{5 - 1} = \frac{35.42 - 7.08}{4} = \frac{28.34}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Sensitivity} \approx 7.08 \text{ pF per unit } \varepsilon_r}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\ln(r_2/r_1)$ in derivation (–3 marks):** Many students write the cylindrical capacitor formula as $C = 2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L / d$, which is **wrong**. The correct form is $C = 2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L / \ln(r_2/r_1)$. This is the **most-mark-losing error** in this question type.
> 2. **Missing the "neat diagram" instruction (–1 mark):** Always draw a labelled figure even if it is a simple schematic.
> 3. **Not stating the working principle in words before the math (–1 mark):** Examiners allocate 2 marks for a clear verbal explanation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Fundamental equation:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ — three variables, three mechanisms.
- **Gap variation** → non-linear, highest sensitivity, used for **pressure** and small displacements.
- **Area variation** → linear, used for **larger displacements** (mm range).
- **Dielectric variation** → linear, used for **level, humidity, thickness** measurement.
- **Differential configuration** gives a **linear** output: $(C_1 - C_2)/(C_1 + C_2) = \Delta d / d$.
- **Permittivity values to memorise:** Air = 1, Water = 80, Oil = 2–5, Mica = 5–7.
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- **Cylindrical capacitor formula:** $C = 2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L / \ln(r_2/r_1)$ — **NOT** $2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L / d$.
- **Signal conditioning:** Always required — typically an AC bridge or charge amplifier with a high-frequency excitation source.
- **Static and dynamic measurement:** Capacitive transducers can measure both (unlike piezoelectric which is dynamic only).
- **Always convert units to SI (metres, m², F/m) before substituting into the formula.**
- **Sensitivity of gap-type transducer:** $\Delta C / C_0 \approx \Delta d / d_0$ (linearised form, valid only for small $\Delta d$).
- **Advantages:** High sensitivity, high frequency response, low power, works in explosive/magnetic environments.
- **Disadvantages:** Stray capacitance, non-linear output (for single-element gap-type), temperature sensitivity, requires careful shielding.
- **KTU exam weightage in this module:** Typically 1–2 questions of 7 marks each from capacitive/inductive/piezoelectric family — *master the derivations, they are the highest-scoring items*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eddy Current Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Eddy Current Type ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Eddy Current Type Transducer / Sensor (Inductive Proximity Sensor):** > A non-contact, passive electromagnetic transducer that operates on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** — specifically, the generation and reaction of **Foucault currents (eddy currents)** in a nearby conductive target when exposed to an alternating magnetic field produced by an energized sensor coil. The resulting change in the coil's electrical impedance is calibrated as a measure of the target's **displacement, position, vibration, proximity, surface profile, or material property**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PEICT416 – Mechanical Instrumentation**, eddy current sensors are classified under **Inductive Transducers** (Module 2: Inductive & Capacitive Transducers) and are universally treated as a high-frequency variant of the **LVDT family**, but with the distinct advantage of operating **without physical contact** and on **non-ferrous, non-magnetic conductive metals** (aluminium, copper, brass, stainless steel, etc.). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English Explanation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Magnet & Spinning Coin" Analogy** > Imagine pushing a strong bar magnet rapidly across a smooth copper coin lying on a table. You will feel a slight *drag* — almost as if the coin is being pulled along with the magnet. The magnetic field sweeping across the conductor **induces tiny circular electrical currents inside the coin** (Foucault / eddy currents). These currents, by Lenz's Law, create their own opposing magnetic field that resists the motion. The eddy-current sensor works on **exactly this same principle**, except: > - The "magnet" is a **coil energized by high-frequency AC** (1 MHz to several MHz). > - The "coin" is the **metallic target** whose distance, thickness, or material we wish to measure. > - The "drag" is measured as a **change in the coil's impedance** (Z = R + jωL). The closer the conductive target is to the coil, the stronger the induced eddy currents, the larger the opposing field, and the greater the disturbance to the coil's magnetic circuit — which directly modulates its **inductance (L)** and **resistance (R)**. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Standard Value (Industrial Practice) | |---|---| | Excitation Frequency | **500 kHz – 5 MHz** (typical **1 MHz**) | | Target Conductivity Range | **1 % IACS – 100 % IACS** | | Measurement Range (Gap) | **0.1 mm – 25 mm** (typical), up to **100 mm** (special) | | Resolution | **0.01 µm – 1 µm** (sub-micron for precision probes) | | Linearity | **± 0.5 % to ± 1 %** of full scale | | Operating Temperature | **-55 °C to +200 °C** (probe head) | | Bandwidth (Dynamic) | **0 Hz – 100 kHz** (DC to high frequency vibration) | | Skin Depth (δ) at 1 MHz in Al | **~ 0.08 mm** (governs penetration into target) | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle — Detailed Step-by-Step Logic The eddy current sensor is essentially a **flat (pancake) coil** wound on a former and potted in an epoxy/stainless-steel housing, facing a conductive target. The operational sequence is: 1. **High-Frequency Excitation** — An internal oscillator drives the sensor coil with a stable **AC current at 1 MHz (typical)**. This produces a rapidly alternating magnetic field that **terminates perpendicular to the coil face** and projects outward into the gap. 2. **Generation of Eddy Currents in Target** — When a conductive metal target enters this field, the **time-varying flux** $\Phi(t)$ passing through the target cross-section induces circulating **Foucault (eddy) currents** $I_e$ in closed loops within a thin surface layer (the **skin depth** $\delta$). 3. **Lenz's Law Reaction** — These eddy currents are **out of phase** with the driving field and produce their **own opposing magnetic field** $H_e$. By Lenz's law, this field **bucks** the original coil flux, effectively reducing the net flux linking the sensor coil. 4. **Impedance Modulation of the Sensor Coil** — The mutual coupling between the sensor coil and the eddy-current "secondary loop" (in the target) acts as a **loosely-coupled air-core transformer** with the target acting as a **shorted single-turn secondary**.
