$$C(t) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A_{max}}{2 \pi \, d} \cdot \omega t$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A_{max}}{2 \pi \, d} \cdot \omega$$

**Step 5 — Linear ramp behaviour.**

This shows that during the half-cycle $0 \leq t \leq T/2$, the capacitance **ramps linearly** with time, producing a **sawtooth-like waveform** when the rotor is a half-disc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** A half-disc rotor produces a **single capacitance cycle per shaft revolution** (one ramp-up + one ramp-down), so $N_{effective} = 1$ in the frequency formula. Thus, $f_{out} = n/60$ Hz for a half-disc rotor.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Voltage from AC Bridge

**Problem Setup:**
A capacitive tachometer with nominal capacitance $C_0 = 100$ pF is connected to an AC Wheatstone bridge excited by $V_{ex} = 10$ V (RMS) at 1 kHz. The rotor rotation causes a peak-to-peak capacitance swing of $\Delta C = 10$ pF. Determine the peak output voltage.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Recall the AC bridge output equation.**

For a balanced bridge, the differential output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{2 C_0}$$

The factor of 2 in the denominator accounts for the differential sensing (two arms of the bridge).

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$V_{out} = 10 \cdot \frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$V_{out} = 10 \cdot \frac{10}{200} = 10 \cdot 0.05$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 0.5 \text{ V (RMS)}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to peak voltage.**

$$V_{peak} = V_{RMS} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 0.5 \cdot 1.414 = 0.707 \text{ V}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Parameter Given | Find | Formula to Apply | Typical KTU Twist |
|-----------------|------|------------------|-------------------|
| $N$ teeth, $n$ RPM | $f_{out}$ | $f_{out} = Nn/60$ | Convert to angular velocity |
| $f_{out}$, $N$ | $n$ in RPM | $n = 60 f_{out}/N$ | Calculate time period |
| $A_{max}$, $d$, $\varepsilon_r$ | $C_{max}$ | $C_{max} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A_{max}/d$ | Unit conversion (cm² → m²) |
| $V_{ex}$, $\Delta C$, $C_0$ | $V_{out}$ | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \Delta C / 2C_0$ | RMS vs Peak conversion |
| $\omega$ rad/s, $N$ | $f_{out}$ | $f_{out} = N \omega / 2 \pi$ | Hz ↔ rad/s conversion |

## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A capacitive tachometer has a rotor with 60 teeth. The output frequency measured by a digital counter is 2.4 kHz. Calculate:
(a) The shaft speed in RPM.
(b) The angular velocity in rad/s.
(c) The time period of one capacitance pulse.

**Solution:**

**(a) Shaft Speed in RPM:**

Using $f_{out} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{60}$:

$$2400 = \frac{60 \cdot n}{60}$$

$$n = 2400 \text{ RPM}$$

**(b) Angular Velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi n}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 2400}{60} = 80 \pi \approx 251.33 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(c) Time Period of One Pulse:**

The number of pulses per second is $f_{out} = 2400$ Hz. Therefore, time per pulse is:

$$T_{pulse} = \frac{1}{f_{out}} = \frac{1}{2400} \approx 416.67 \, \mu s$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Capacitive Tachometer Signal Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **block-level functional architecture** of a capacitive tachometer, showing the signal path from the rotating shaft to the digital display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ShaftUnderTest] -->|MechanicalCoupling| B[RotorPlateWithNTeeth]
    B -->|VariesOverlapArea| C[AirGapDielectric]
    C -->|VaryingCapacitanceCofT| D[StatorPlateFixed]
    D -->|ACSignal| E[ACWheatstoneBridge]
    E -->|DifferentialVoltage| F[AmplifierStage]
    F -->|ConditionedSignal| G[SchmittTrigger]
    G -->|SquareWave| H[FrequencyCounter]
    H -->|DigitalCount| I[Microcontroller]
    I -->|ProcessedRPM| J[DisplayUnitLCD]
    E -.->|Excitation| K[Oscillator1kHz]
    K -.->|ReferenceSignal| E
    D -->|ShieldedCoaxial| L[FaradayShield]
    L --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The shaft under test mechanically drives the rotor. The varying capacitance is sensed via the AC bridge (driven by an oscillator), then amplified, shaped into a square wave by a Schmitt trigger, and finally counted by a frequency-to-RPM converter. The Faraday shield around the stator prevents stray capacitance pickup.

## 4.2 Operational State Diagram — Capacitance vs Rotor Angle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialAlignment
    InitialAlignment --> MaxOverlap: theta equals 0
    MaxOverlap --> DecreasingOverlap: theta increases 0 to pi over 2
    DecreasingOverlap --> ZeroOverlap: theta equals pi over 2
    ZeroOverlap --> IncreasingOverlap: theta increases pi over 2 to pi
    IncreasingOverlap --> MaxOverlap: theta equals pi
    MaxOverlap --> DecreasingOverlap: theta exceeds pi
    DecreasingOverlap --> [*]
    note right of MaxOverlap
        C of theta is maximum
        Cmax equals epsilon0 epsilon r A max divided by d
    end note
    note right of ZeroOverlap
        C of theta is minimum
        Cmin equals zero
    end note
```

## 4.3 Physical Construction Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RotorAssembly
        R1[Shaft] --> R2[Hub]
        R2 --> R3[ToothedDisc60Teeth]
        R3 --> R4[GuardRing]
    end
    subgraph StatorAssembly
        S1[FixedStatorPlate] --> S2[InsulatingSpacer]
        S2 --> S3[BackPlateGrounded]
    end
    subgraph AirGap
        AG[AirDielectricGapdmeters]
    end
    RotorAssembly -->|Distance d| AirGap
    AirGap -->|Distance d| StatorAssembly
    StatorAssembly -->|CoaxialCable| ConditioningCircuit
    ConditioningCircuit --> Display
```

## 4.4 Signal Waveform (Conceptual)

| Rotor Position | Capacitance $C(\theta)$ | AC Bridge Output |
|----------------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| $0°$ (aligned) | $C_{max}$ | +$V_{peak}$ |
| $90°$ | $0.5 \, C_{max}$ | 0 V |
| $180°$ | $0$ | $-V_{peak}$ |
| $270°$ | $0.5 \, C_{max}$ | 0 V |
| $360°$ | $C_{max}$ | +$V_{peak}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The waveform is **sinusoidal for a toothed rotor** and **triangular/sawtooth for a half-disc rotor**. In KTU board exams, always state the waveform type explicitly when describing a capacitive tachometer output.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define a capacitive tachometer and state the principle on which it works.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A capacitive tachometer is a non-contact device used for measuring the angular velocity of a rotating shaft. **(1 Mark)**

It works on the principle of **variable capacitance**, where the effective area of overlap between a stationary stator plate and a rotating rotor plate changes with rotation, producing a time-varying capacitance. **(1 Mark)**

This varying capacitance is converted into a proportional AC voltage or frequency output, whose frequency is directly proportional to the rotational speed of the shaft. **(1 Mark)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: A capacitive tachometer rotor has 50 teeth. The measured output frequency is 1.5 kHz. Find the shaft speed in RPM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Given: Number of teeth $N = 50$, Output frequency $f_{out} = 1.5 \text{ kHz} = 1500$ Hz. **(0.5 Mark)**

Formula: $f_{out} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{60}$ **(1 Mark)**

Rearranging: $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_{out}}{N} = \dfrac{60 \times 1500}{50}$ **(1 Mark)**

$$\boxed{n = 1800 \text{ RPM}}$$ **(0.5 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the construction and working principle of a capacitive tachometer with a neat block diagram. Derive the relationship between output frequency and shaft speed.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A capacitive tachometer uses a rotor with 120 teeth. The excitation frequency of the AC bridge is 50 kHz. Calculate: (i) the output frequency for a shaft speed of 3000 RPM, (ii) the time period of one capacitance cycle, and (iii) the angular velocity in rad/s.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working:**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
A capacitive tachometer consists of:
- A **rotor plate** mounted on the shaft under test, having $N$ equally spaced teeth or shaped as a half-disc.
- A **stator plate** fixed in position, parallel to the rotor, with a small air gap $d$ between them.
- **Dielectric medium** (air, $\varepsilon_r = 1$) filling the gap.
- A **Faraday shield** to protect against stray capacitance.
- An **AC Wheatstone bridge** with an excitation oscillator for signal conditioning.
- A **frequency counter** or microcontroller for digital display.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
As the shaft rotates, the effective overlapping area between the rotor and stator plates changes periodically. This causes the capacitance to vary sinusoidally (for toothed rotor) with time. The varying capacitance modulates the AC bridge output, producing a voltage whose frequency equals the capacitance variation frequency. This frequency is proportional to shaft RPM.

**Derivation (2 Marks):**

$$f_{out} = \frac{N \cdot n}{60} \quad \text{or} \quad f_{out} = \frac{N \cdot \omega}{2 \pi}$$

where $N$ = number of teeth, $n$ = RPM, $\omega$ = angular velocity in rad/s.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $N = 120$ teeth, $n = 3000$ RPM. **(0.5 Mark)**

**(i) Output frequency (2 Marks):**

$$f_{out} = \frac{N \cdot n}{60} = \frac{120 \times 3000}{60} = 6000 \text{ Hz} = 6 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Time period of one capacitance cycle (2 Marks):**

The excitation frequency (50 kHz) is irrelevant for the output pulse frequency calculation; the output frequency is determined solely by mechanical rotation.

$$T = \frac{1}{f_{out}} = \frac{1}{6000} \approx 166.67 \, \mu s$$

**(iii) Angular velocity (2.5 Marks):**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi n}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 3000}{60} = 100 \pi \approx 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing excitation frequency with output frequency:** The 50 kHz excitation is the AC bridge carrier, NOT the output signal frequency. Many students incorrectly use 50 kHz in the formula. (–2 Marks)
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion:** $n$ is in RPM, so dividing by 60 is mandatory. (–1 Mark)
> 3. **Not drawing the block diagram:** Skipping the diagram costs at least 2 marks. Always include it.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a functional block diagram, describe the working of a variable-area type capacitive tachometer. State any four advantages of using a capacitive tachometer over other speed-measuring devices.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A capacitive tachometer has a rotor plate shaped as a semicircle of radius 5 cm, separated from a stationary full-disc stator by an air gap of 1 mm. Calculate the maximum capacitance. If the shaft rotates at 1200 RPM, find the output frequency. (Given $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Variable-Area Capacitive Tachometer (3 Marks for diagram + working):**

The variable-area capacitive tachometer operates by varying the effective overlapping area between the rotor and stator plates while the distance and dielectric remain constant.

**Working:** As the rotor turns, the projected area facing the stator changes. This produces a capacitance $C(\theta)$ that is a function of the rotor's angular position $\theta$. The capacitance is fed into an AC bridge whose output is an AC voltage at a frequency equal to the rotation frequency (for half-disc) or $N$ times the rotation frequency (for toothed rotor).

**Four Advantages (4 × 1 = 4 Marks):**
1. **Non-contact operation** — Zero mechanical loading on the shaft.
2. **High sensitivity** — Detects very small capacitance changes (sub-pF range).
3. **Wide frequency range** — From nearly zero RPM to over 100,000 RPM.
4. **No wear and tear** — Ideal for long-term industrial use; virtually infinite life.
5. *(Bonus)* High linearity and bidirectional capability.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: Radius of half-disc $r = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m, Air gap $d = 1$ mm $= 10^{-3}$ m, $\varepsilon_r = 1$, $n = 1200$ RPM. **(0.5 Mark)**

**Step 1 — Maximum area of the full stator disc (1.5 Marks):**

$$A_{max} = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (0.05)^2 = \pi \times 0.0025 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Maximum capacitance (2.5 Marks):**

$$C_{max} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A_{max}}{d} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}}$$

$$C_{max} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 7.854 = 6.954 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} \approx 69.54 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 3 — Output frequency (2.5 Marks):**

For a half-disc rotor, $N_{effective} = 1$:

$$f_{out} = \frac{1 \times 1200}{60} = 20 \text{ Hz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting to convert cm and mm to metres leads to a wrong answer by 6 orders of magnitude. (–3 Marks)
> 2. **Using full-disc area instead of half-disc:** For $C_{max}$ at full overlap, the FULL stator area must be used (since at $\theta = 0$ the half-disc fully overlaps with one half of the stator). However, in this problem, the *full-disc stator* has area $\pi r^2$ and the *half-disc rotor* modulates the effective overlap. The maximum overlap area equals the half-disc area, i.e., $A_{max,overlap} = \pi r^2 / 2 = 3.927 \times 10^{-3}$ m². Using this, $C_{max} \approx 34.77$ pF. **(Marks awarded for correct interpretation of the geometry.)**
> 3. **Not mentioning $\varepsilon_r$ for air:** Always state that $\varepsilon_r = 1$ for air. (–0.5 Mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ A **capacitive tachometer** measures **angular velocity** using the **variable-capacitance principle**.
- ✅ Core formula: $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d}$ where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- ✅ **Output frequency** is linearly related to RPM: $f_{out} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{60}$ Hz.
- ✅ **$N$ = number of teeth** on the rotor; for a half-disc rotor, $N = 1$.
- ✅ **Variable-area type** is most common; **variable-distance** and **variable-dielectric** are alternatives.
- ✅ Always include a **Faraday shield** to eliminate stray capacitance effects.
- ✅ The output is typically processed via an **AC Wheatstone bridge** with sinusoidal excitation.
- ✅ Conversion: $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi n}{60}$ rad/s; $n = \dfrac{60 \omega}{2 \pi}$ RPM.
- ✅ Time period of one pulse: $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{out}}$ seconds.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Non-contact, high sensitivity, wide range, no wear, bidirectional.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Sensitive to temperature, humidity, and stray capacitance; requires careful shielding.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** Engine RPM sensing, turbine speed monitoring, motor speed control, CNC spindles, aerospace rotor monitoring.
- ✅ **KTU Board Pattern:** Always convert cm² → m² and mm → m in numerical problems. State $\varepsilon_r$ explicitly.
- ✅ Common waveform: **Sinusoidal for toothed rotor**, **sawtooth/triangular for half-disc rotor**.
- ✅ The **differential capacitive tachometer** can detect **direction of rotation** through phase analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drag-cup Type Tachometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Drag-cup Type Tachometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Drag-cup Type Tachometer (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> A drag-cup tachometer is an electromagnetic (eddy-current) speed-measuring instrument in which a thin, non-magnetic conductive cup (typically **aluminium** or **copper**) is suspended in the air gap of a magnetic field produced by a permanent magnet or an AC-excited field winding coupled to the rotating shaft. The relative motion between the rotating magnetic field and the stationary (or slightly deflectable) drag cup induces **eddy currents** in the cup, and the interaction between these induced currents and the magnetic field produces a deflection torque that is **directly proportional to the angular velocity** of the shaft.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the device belongs to the family of **electromechanical analog tachometers** and is widely classified into two functional variants:
- **Drag-cup Indicating Tachometer** – Gives a pointer deflection proportional to speed.
- **Drag-cup A.C. Tachogenerator** – Produces an output **A.C. voltage** whose amplitude is proportional to speed and whose frequency equals the reference (excitation) frequency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy: "The Spinning Magnet and the Slow-Hand Cup"**
> Imagine you spin a bar magnet rapidly between the pages of a closed book. The pages feel a force trying to drag them along with the magnet. The faster you spin, the stronger the "drag." Now replace the book with a thin aluminium cup and the magnet with a permanent-magnet rotor. The cup is held back by a soft spring and carries a pointer. The faster the magnet spins, the more the cup gets dragged, the more the spring stretches, and the further the pointer moves on a calibrated dial. That pointer position is your **speed reading**. The cup is "dragged" along with the field, hence the name **drag-cup tachometer**.

The same principle is used in induction motors, but here the cup is intentionally free to twist by a tiny amount — just enough to drive a needle.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Drag-cup material conductivity:** Aluminium ≈ **3.5 × 10⁷ S/m**, Copper ≈ **5.8 × 10⁷ S/m** (Aluminium preferred due to lower density and adequate conductivity).
> - **Cup wall thickness:** Typically **0.5 mm to 1.5 mm** — thin enough to allow deep eddy-current penetration, thick enough for mechanical rigidity.
> - **Operating range:** Indication type: **0–10,000 rpm**; Tachogenerator type: **0–4,000 rpm**.
> - **Accuracy class:** Industrial grade ±1% to ±2% of full scale.
> - **Output voltage (tachogenerator):** Linear with speed; e.g., **E₀ = K·N** where K is typically **0.05 V/rpm** to **0.1 V/rpm** depending on design.

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot (KTU Quick Glance)

| Variant | Input (Shaft) | Output | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drag-cup **Indicating** Tachometer | Permanent magnet rotor | Pointer deflection on dial | Direct human-readable rpm |
| Drag-cup **A.C. Tachogenerator** | Drag cup rotated by shaft | Sinusoidal AC voltage | Speed feedback in control loops |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics – Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The working of the drag-cup tachometer rests on three pillars of classical electromagnetism. Let us break them down:

### Pillar 1: Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction
> [!NOTE]
> Whenever a conductor cuts magnetic flux, an EMF is induced: $\varepsilon = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = B \cdot \ell \cdot v$ (motional EMF form).

When the rotor magnet spins, the **drag cup (stationary conductor) experiences a time-varying flux** through it. By Faraday's law, this induces an EMF around the cup's circular cross-section.

### Pillar 2: Lenz's Law – Direction of Induced Current
The induced eddy currents flow in such a direction that they **oppose the very cause that produced them** (i.e., the relative motion). So the cup experiences a force that tries to "chase" the rotating field. The faster the rotation, the stronger the induced currents and hence the larger the opposing drag force.

### Pillar 3: Lorentz Force → Mechanical Torque
Each eddy-current element $i \, d\vec{\ell}$ in the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ experiences a force $d\vec{F} = i \, d\vec{\ell} \times \vec{B}$. Integrated over the closed loop of the cup, this gives a **net torque** $T_{eddy}$ on the cup that tends to rotate it in the same direction as the rotor.

### Static Equilibrium of the Drag Cup
The cup is restrained by a **helical spiral spring** of stiffness $k_s$. At equilibrium:

$$T_{eddy} = T_{spring} = k_s \cdot \theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angular deflection of the cup (and pointer).

The eddy-current torque is **proportional to the speed** $N$ (rpm) of the rotor, so:

$$\theta = \frac{K_1}{k_s} \cdot N = K \cdot N$$

The pointer deflection $\theta$ is therefore a **linear measure of the rotational speed** — the dial can be calibrated directly in rpm.

## 2.2 Constructional Details (Component-by-Component)

1. **Permanent Magnet Rotor** – A 2-pole (or 4-pole) permanent magnet coupled rigidly to the input shaft. The flux it produces sweeps across the stationary drag cup.
2. **Drag Cup** – A thin-walled cylindrical cup of non-magnetic metal (aluminium). It is suspended coaxially around the rotor with a small uniform **air gap** (≈ 0.5–1.5 mm). It can twist by a few degrees against the restoring spring.
3. **Restoring Spring** – A calibrated spiral spring that returns the cup to the zero position when speed = 0.
4. **Pointer & Dial** – The pointer is attached to the cup spindle; the dial is graduated linearly in rpm.
5. **Damping Mechanism** – Often a small permanent magnet acting on an aluminium damping vane, providing **eddy-current damping** to suppress pointer oscillations.
6. **Yoke/Case** – Magnetic return path and mechanical protection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for A.C. Tachogenerator variant:** The rotor here is **NOT a permanent magnet**. Instead, a stationary **laminated stator** carries two windings — a **reference winding** (excited by a stable AC source of frequency $f$) and an **output winding** (where the speed-proportional voltage is generated). The drag cup itself rotates with the shaft inside the air gap of this stator.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = B \cdot \ell \cdot v$ | Motional EMF (Faraday) | V | Per moving conductor element | 
| 2 | $T_{eddy} = K_1 \cdot \omega$ | Eddy-current torque on cup | N·m | $\omega$ in rad/s; $K_1$ depends on geometry & $B$ | 
| 3 | $T_{spring} = k_s \cdot \theta$ | Spring restoring torque | N·m | $k_s$ = spring constant (N·m/rad) | 
| 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{K_1}{k_s} \cdot \omega = K \cdot N$ | Pointer deflection | rad (or deg) | $N$ in rpm; $K$ = tachometer constant | 
| 5 | $E_0 = K_v \cdot N$ | Output voltage (AC tachogenerator) | V | $K_v$ = voltage constant (V/rpm) | 
| 6 | $f_{out} = f_{ref}$ | Output frequency (AC tachogenerator) | Hz | **Independent of speed** – key advantage | 
| 7 | $f_{ref} = \dfrac{P \cdot N_s}{120}$ | Synchronous relation | Hz | $P$ = poles, $N_s$ = synchronous rpm | 
| 8 | $E_2 = \dfrac{N}{N_s} \cdot E_{ref}$ | Transformer-like EMF ratio | V | $E_{ref}$ = reference winding voltage | 
| 9 | $\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{N_{indicated} - N_{actual}}{N_{actual}} \times 100$ | Accuracy check | % | KTU board question favourite | 

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-symbol caution in tables:** All absolute-value or ratio expressions use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity (e.g., $E_2 = \tfrac{N}{N_s}\cdot E_{ref}$, never with literal pipes).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Servo Control Loops:** A.C. tachogenerators provide speed feedback to motor drives; the constant output frequency is decoupled from the input supply variations.
- **Machine Tool Spindles:** Direct speed indication on lathes, mills, and drilling machines.
- **Tape Recorders & Turntables:** Velocity feedback for stable turntable speed (a classic historical use).
- **Automotive & Aerospace Test Rigs:** Reliable non-contacting speed measurement over long runs.
- **Process Industries:** Where the standard centrifugal tachometer's bearing wear would be a maintenance issue.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Torque-Speed Linearity for the Indicating Type

**Step 1 — Define the air-gap flux density.**
Let the permanent magnet rotor produce a sinusoidal radial flux density distribution in the air gap (justified by the uniform cylindrical geometry):

$$B(\phi, t) = B_0 \cos(\omega t - p\phi)$$

where:
- $B_0$ = peak flux density at the cup surface (T),
- $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ = mechanical angular velocity of rotor (rad/s),
- $p$ = number of pole pairs,
- $\phi$ = angular position around the cup circumference (rad).

**Step 2 — Compute the motional EMF in a cup filament.**
Consider an elemental conductor strip of length $\ell$ (axial length of the cup) moving with relative velocity $v = r\omega$ with respect to the field:

$$e_{filament} = B(\phi,t) \cdot \ell \cdot v = B_0 \ell r \omega \cos(\omega t - p\phi)$$

**Step 3 — Find the induced eddy current in the cup wall.**
The cup has a wall resistance $R_c$ for the eddy current loop. Ignoring cup inductance (valid because the magnetic circuit permeability is dominated by the iron path, not the cup):

$$i_{eddy}(\phi, t) = \frac{e_{filament}}{R_c} = \frac{B_0 \ell r \omega}{R_c} \cos(\omega t - p\phi)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the force on the elemental current-carrying strip.**
The Lorentz force on the strip, in the tangential direction that tries to drag the cup along:

$$dF_{tangential} = B(\phi,t) \cdot i_{eddy}(\phi,t) \cdot \ell = \frac{B_0^2 \ell^2 r \omega}{R_c} \cos^2(\omega t - p\phi)$$

**Step 5 — Integrate around the cup to get total tangential force, then torque.**
Integrating $dF$ around the full circumference ($\phi = 0$ to $2\pi$) and multiplying by the cup mean radius $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eddy} &= \int_0^{2\pi} dF_{tangential} \cdot r \, d\phi \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{B_0^2 \ell^2 r^2 \omega}{R_c} \cos^2(\omega t - p\phi) \, d\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Use the trigonometric identity $\cos^2 x = \dfrac{1 + \cos 2x}{2}$ and the orthogonality of cosine over a full period.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eddy} &= \frac{B_0^2 \ell^2 r^2 \omega}{R_c} \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega t - 2p\phi)}{2} \, d\phi \\
&= \frac{B_0^2 \ell^2 r^2 \omega}{R_c} \left[ \frac{1}{2}\cdot 2\pi + 0 \right] \\
&= \frac{\pi B_0^2 \ell^2 r^2}{R_c} \cdot \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Identify the proportionality constant.**

$$\boxed{\,T_{eddy} = K_1 \cdot \omega \quad \text{where} \quad K_1 = \frac{\pi B_0^2 \ell^2 r^2}{R_c}\,}$$

This proves the **fundamental result**: the eddy-current torque on the drag cup is **directly proportional to the rotor angular velocity** $\omega$. The proportionality constant $K_1$ depends only on design parameters (flux density, cup dimensions, cup resistance), not on speed.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Pointer Deflection vs. Speed

**Step 1 — Spring equilibrium condition.**
At steady state, the cup stops accelerating. The electromagnetic torque is balanced by the spring:

$$T_{eddy} = T_{spring}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the formulas derived above.**

$$K_1 \cdot \omega = k_s \cdot \theta$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the pointer deflection $\theta$.**

$$\theta = \frac{K_1}{k_s} \cdot \omega = \frac{K_1}{k_s} \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Define a single tachometer constant $K$.**

$$\boxed{\,\theta = K \cdot N \quad \text{where} \quad K = \frac{2\pi K_1}{60 \, k_s}\,}$$

Thus the pointer deflection is **linearly proportional to the speed $N$ in rpm** — justifying a uniformly graduated dial.

## 3.3 Derivation III: A.C. Tachogenerator Output Voltage

In the AC tachogenerator variant, the reference winding is excited by a stable AC source of voltage $E_{ref}$ and frequency $f$. The drag cup rotates with the shaft inside the air gap of a stationary laminated stator carrying the reference and output windings.

**Step 1 — Synchronous relationship.**
With $P$ poles, the synchronous speed is $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$. The rotor (drag cup) runs at speed $N$.

**Step 2 — Transformer EMF analogy.**
The reference winding acts as the primary of a rotating transformer whose secondary is the output winding, and whose coupling is modulated by the cup's rotation. The induced EMF in the output winding is:

$$E_0 = \frac{N}{N_s} \cdot E_{ref}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $N_s$ in terms of frequency.**

$$\boxed{\,E_0 = \frac{N \cdot P}{120 f} \cdot E_{ref} = K_v \cdot N \quad \text{with} \quad K_v = \frac{P \cdot E_{ref}}{120 f}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Output frequency observation.**
Because the cup modulates an already-oscillating field at frequency $f$, the output voltage has the **same frequency** $f$ as the reference:

$$f_{out} = f_{ref}$$

This is the key advantage of the AC tachogenerator: the output frequency is **immune to speed variations** — only the **amplitude** carries the speed information. This makes downstream signal processing (filtering, threshold detection) much simpler.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite)

| Feature | Drag-cup Indicating | Drag-cup A.C. Tachogenerator | Centrifugal Tachometer | DC Tachogenerator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output form | Pointer angle | AC voltage | Pointer angle | DC voltage |
| Contacting parts? | No (frictionless) | No | Yes (governor weights) | Yes (brushes & commutator) |
| Linearity | Good (linear) | Excellent (linear) | Poor at low speed | Good |
| Frequency of output | N/A | Constant (= $f_{ref}$) | N/A | Zero (DC, but with ripple) |
| Maintenance | Very low | Very low | Moderate (bearings) | High (brush wear) |
| Accuracy | ±1–2% | ±0.5% or better | ±2–3% | ±1% |
| Cost | Moderate | Moderate–High | Low | High |
| Suitability for control | Poor | **Excellent** | Poor | Good |
| Direction sensing | No (by itself) | Possible via phase | No | Yes (polarity) |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** A drag-cup tachometer has a rotor with $P = 4$ poles, a reference winding voltage of $E_{ref} = 100$ V at $f = 50$ Hz, and an output winding with a turns ratio equivalent constant such that the output voltage constant is $K_v = 0.05$ V/rpm. The drag cup has $r = 20$ mm, $\ell = 30$ mm, wall resistance $R_c = 0.02\ \Omega$, and air-gap flux density $B_0 = 0.8$ T. The spring constant is $k_s = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ N·m/rad. Determine:
(a) The eddy-current torque on the cup when $N = 3000$ rpm.
(b) The pointer deflection.
(c) The output voltage of the equivalent A.C. tachogenerator configuration.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Eddy-current torque at 3000 rpm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 100\pi \approx 314.159 \text{ rad/s} \\
K_1 &= \frac{\pi B_0^2 \ell^2 r^2}{R_c} \\
    &= \frac{\pi \times (0.8)^2 \times (0.030)^2 \times (0.020)^2}{0.02} \\
    &= \frac{\pi \times 0.64 \times 9 \times 10^{-4} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{0.02} \\
    &= \frac{\pi \times 2.304 \times 10^{-7}}{0.02} \\
    &= \pi \times 1.152 \times 10^{-5} \\
    &\approx 3.619 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N·m·s/rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eddy} &= K_1 \cdot \omega = 3.619 \times 10^{-5} \times 314.159 \\
         &\approx 0.01137 \text{ N·m} \\
         &\approx 11.37 \text{ mN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Pointer deflection.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{T_{eddy}}{k_s} = \frac{0.01137}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \\
       &= 22.74 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

This absurdly large angle is unrealistic — it shows that the numbers in the question are illustrative, not realistic. In practice, $K_1$ is engineered to be much smaller (e.g., by reducing $B_0$ or $\ell$ or $r$). **KTU exam tip:** If you get an unrealistic angle, recompute — but report the formula, not the unrealistic number.

**Part (c) — Output voltage of A.C. tachogenerator.**

$$
E_0 = K_v \cdot N = 0.05 \times 3000 = 150 \text{ V}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output frequency is $f = 50$ Hz, **independent of speed** — note this in the answer for full marks.

## 3.6 Sources of Error — Examiner's Checklist

- **Residual magnetism & cup inertia at very low speeds** → dead zone.
- **Cup heating** → $R_c$ changes, $K_1$ drifts.
- **Spring fatigue** → $k_s$ changes, $K$ drifts.
- **Temperature drift of reference voltage** (tachogenerator variant) → gain error.
- **External magnetic fields** → zero shift.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – Indicating Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Shaft] --> B[Coupling]
    B --> C[Permanent Magnet Rotor]
    C --> D{Rotating Magnetic Field}
    D --> E[Aluminium Drag Cup]
    E --> F[Induced Eddy Currents]
    F --> G[Lorentz Tangential Force]
    G --> H[Drag Cup Deflects by Theta]
    H --> I[Restoring Spring Twists]
    I --> J{Equilibrium: T_eddy = k_s x theta}
    J --> K[Pointer Attached to Cup]
    K --> L[Calibrated Dial - rpm]
    M[External Damping Magnet] --> N[Eddy-current Damping Vane]
    N --> H
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram – A.C. Tachogenerator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Shaft] --> B[Drag Cup Rotor]
    B --> C[Air Gap]
    C --> D[Stator Laminated Core]
    D --> E[Reference Winding W1]
    D --> F[Output Winding W2]
    G[Stable AC Source] -->|E_ref at f Hz| E
    F -->|E0 = Kv x N, freq = f| H[Signal Conditioning]
    H --> I[Speed Indicator / Controller]
    E -.inductive coupling.-> F
```

## 4.3 Detailed Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input
        shaftX[Rotating Shaft]
        speedN[Speed N in rpm]
        shaftX --> speedN
    end

    subgraph Electromechanical_Conversion
        magnet1[Permanent Magnet Rotor]
        gap1[Air Gap with Rotating Flux]
        cup1[Aluminium Drag Cup]
        eddy1[Eddy Current Loops]
        force1[Lorentz Force / Torque T_eddy]
        magnet1 --> gap1
        gap1 --> cup1
        cup1 --> eddy1
        eddy1 --> force1
    end

    subgraph Mechanical_Output
        spring1[Restoring Spring k_s]
        theta1[Deflection Theta]
        pointer1[Pointer]
        dial1[Calibrated Dial in rpm]
        force1 --> theta1
        spring1 --> theta1
        theta1 --> pointer1
        pointer1 --> dial1
    end

    speedN --> magnet1
    theta1 -.visual feedback.-> dial1
```

## 4.4 Comparative Working Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Shaft Rotates at N rpm]) --> gen[Magnet / Reference Winding Excites Air-gap Flux]
    gen --> sweep[Flux Sweeps Across Cup at Frequency f x p / P]
    sweep --> induce[Faraday: EMF Induced in Cup]
    induce --> curr[Eddy Currents Flow in Cup Wall]
    curr --> lorentz[Lorentz Force on Current Elements]
    lorentz --> torque[Net Torque T_eddy Proportional to N]
    torque --> deflect[Drag Cup Deflects Theta = K x N]
    deflect --> dial[Pointer Reads Speed on Dial]
    torque --> damp[Damping Vane Provides Eddy-current Damping]
    damp --> stable[Steady Deflection Reached]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams instead of physical drawings?**
> A real drag-cup tachometer has concentric cylindrical geometry, internal lamination stacks, and fine mechanical details that are extremely hard to render in pure text/Mermaid. The **functional architecture** preserves all the information a KTU examiner expects — namely, the signal flow from shaft rotation to final output, including the electromagnetic coupling and mechanical feedback through the spring.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define a drag-cup tachometer. List its two main types.**

**Model Answer:**
A drag-cup tachometer is an electromagnetic instrument used to measure the rotational speed of a shaft. It works on the principle of eddy-current induction: a rotating magnetic field (from a permanent magnet or AC-excited winding) cuts a thin, non-magnetic conductive cup (drag cup) and induces eddy currents in it. The interaction between these currents and the magnetic field produces a torque proportional to the shaft speed.

**The two main types are:**
1. **Drag-cup indicating tachometer** – provides a pointer deflection proportional to speed.
2. **Drag-cup A.C. tachogenerator** – provides an A.C. output voltage proportional to speed, with constant frequency equal to the reference frequency.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only one type or confuse the drag-cup tachometer with a magnetic pickup or stroboscope. Always mention **both** types for full 3 marks.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional II]**
**State the principle of operation of a drag-cup tachometer. Why is aluminium used for the cup?**

**Model Answer:**
**Principle:** When a magnetic flux linking a closed conducting loop changes, an EMF is induced in the loop (Faraday's Law). This EMF drives eddy currents whose interaction with the original magnetic field produces a tangential force (Lorentz force / Lenz's Law). The resulting torque on the drag cup is proportional to the relative angular velocity between the rotating field and the cup.

**Why aluminium?**
- High electrical conductivity-to-weight ratio (good eddy-current response with low inertia).
- Non-magnetic (does not distort the air-gap flux pattern).
- Low cost and easy to fabricate as thin-walled cups.
- Adequate mechanical strength for the small deflections involved.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Some students write "because it is light" without mentioning **conductivity** or **non-magnetic** property. These are the **two essential reasons** the examiner awards the mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) – Choice 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**a)** With the help of a neat functional diagram, explain the construction and working of a **drag-cup type indicating tachometer**. Derive the relationship between the pointer deflection and the rotational speed. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A drag-cup tachometer has the following specifications: $B_0 = 0.6$ T, $\ell = 25$ mm, $r = 18$ mm, $R_c = 0.015\ \Omega$, $k_s = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ N·m/rad. Compute the pointer deflection at $N = 1500$ rpm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution – Q-A (a)

**Construction (4 marks):**
The drag-cup indicating tachometer consists of:
- A **permanent-magnet rotor** with 2 or 4 poles, coupled to the input shaft whose speed is to be measured.
- A **stationary aluminium drag cup**, cylindrical, surrounding the rotor with a small uniform **air gap** (≈ 0.5–1.5 mm).
- A **calibrated spiral spring** that resists the rotation of the cup and provides the restoring torque.
- A **pointer** rigidly attached to the cup spindle, moving over a graduated dial marked in rpm.
- A **damping arrangement** using a small permanent magnet and a damping vane on the cup spindle to provide eddy-current damping.
- A **magnetic yoke** providing the return path for the rotor flux.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. As the input shaft rotates, the permanent-magnet rotor produces a **rotating magnetic field** that sweeps across the stationary drag cup.
2. This changing flux **induces eddy currents** in the cup wall (Faraday's Law).
3. The eddy currents, in the presence of the radial magnetic field, experience a **Lorentz force** tangential to the cup (Lenz's Law). This produces a net torque $T_{eddy}$ on the cup, tending to drag it in the direction of rotation.
4. The cup twists against the spring until the spring torque equals $T_{eddy}$.
5. The resulting **pointer deflection $\theta$** is calibrated directly in rpm.

**Derivation (already covered in Section 3.1 & 3.2):**

$$\boxed{\,\theta = \frac{2\pi K_1}{60 \, k_s} \cdot N = K \cdot N\,}$$

where $K_1 = \dfrac{\pi B_0^2 \ell^2 r^2}{R_c}$.

Hence the **deflection is linearly proportional to the shaft speed**, justifying a uniformly graduated dial.

---

#### Model Solution – Q-A (b)

**Given:**
$B_0 = 0.6$ T, $\ell = 25$ mm $= 25 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $r = 18$ mm $= 18 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $R_c = 0.015\ \Omega$, $k_s = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ N·m/rad, $N = 1500$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Compute the angular velocity.**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K_1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 &= \frac{\pi B_0^2 \ell^2 r^2}{R_c} \\
    &= \frac{\pi \times (0.6)^2 \times (25 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times (18 \times 10^{-3})^2}{0.015} \\
    &= \frac{\pi \times 0.36 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.24 \times 10^{-4}}{0.015} \\
    &= \frac{\pi \times 7.29 \times 10^{-8}}{0.015} \\
    &= \pi \times 4.86 \times 10^{-6} \\
    &\approx 1.527 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N·m·s/rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the eddy-current torque.**

$$T_{eddy} = K_1 \cdot \omega = 1.527 \times 10^{-5} \times 157.08 \approx 2.398 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m} \approx 2.4 \text{ mN·m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the pointer deflection.**

$$\theta = \frac{T_{eddy}}{k_s} = \frac{2.398 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.995 \approx 6.0 \text{ rad}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Always carry **units through every step** and write the final answer in the **requested unit** (rad here, but examiners may accept degrees: $6.0 \text{ rad} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 343.8°$). Failing to convert units loses a half-mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**a)** Explain the construction and working of a **drag-cup type A.C. tachogenerator**. Why is its output frequency independent of shaft speed? Derive the expression for the output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Compare the drag-cup tachometer with the centrifugal tachometer and the D.C. tachogenerator on at least **six** performance parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution – Q-B (a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
A drag-cup A.C. tachogenerator consists of:
- A **stationary laminated stator** with two windings placed in slots:
  - **Reference (or excitation) winding W₁** — supplied from a stable AC source of constant amplitude and frequency $f$.
  - **Output winding W₂** — from which the speed-proportional voltage is taken.
- A **rotating drag cup** (aluminium) attached to the input shaft, rotating inside the air gap of the stator. (In some designs, the rotor is the drag cup itself; in others, an intermediate ferromagnetic rotor carries the cup.)

**Working (2 marks):**
1. The reference winding produces an alternating flux that links the output winding through the rotating drag cup.
2. The rotation of the cup modulates the coupling between W₁ and W₂.
3. An EMF is induced in W₂ whose **magnitude is proportional to the speed** $N$ of the cup (rotor), but whose **frequency** is locked to the reference frequency $f$.

**Why output frequency is independent of speed (1 mark):**
The output winding's flux variation is governed entirely by the **reference AC source's frequency** $f$. The drag cup's rotation only changes the **amplitude** of this linkage, not its time-period. Hence $f_{out} = f_{ref}$, independent of $N$.

**Derivation (1 mark — see Section 3.3):**

$$\boxed{\,E_0 = K_v \cdot N, \quad \text{with} \quad K_v = \frac{P \cdot E_{ref}}{120 f}, \quad f_{out} = f_{ref}\,}$$

---

#### Model Solution – Q-B (b)

**Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per well-explained parameter; 6 required for full marks + 1 for tabular format and concluding remark):**

| Parameter | Drag-cup Tachometer | Centrifugal Tachometer | D.C. Tachogenerator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Output form** | Pointer / AC voltage | Pointer (mechanical) | DC voltage |
| **Linearity** | Excellent | Poor (sinusoidal) | Good (with linear mag. circuit) |
| **Friction/Wear** | None (frictionless) | Bearing friction | Brush & commutator wear |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.5–2% | ±2–3% | ±1% |
| **Direction sensing** | Limited (via phase) | Not possible | Easy (polarity reversal) |
| **Maintenance** | Very low | Moderate | High |
| **Speed range** | Wide (0–10,000 rpm) | Limited at high speed | Moderate |
| **Suitability for control** | Excellent (AC tachogenerator) | Poor | Good |

**Concluding remark (1 mark):**
The drag-cup tachometer combines the **frictionless operation** of an electromagnetic device with the **rigorous linearity** of an inductive coupling. Its AC tachogenerator variant is **the preferred speed transducer in modern closed-loop servo systems** because of its constant output frequency and excellent linearity, while the indicating variant remains a reliable analog panel meter for direct human reading.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Conflating "tachometer" with "tachogenerator":** The two have different output forms. Spell them out.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit of $K_1$:** Always state $K_1$ has units of N·m·s/rad. The examiner will deduct for missing unit annotation.
> 3. **Sign error in the Lorentz force direction:** Lenz's law gives a **drag** (opposing) force, but the **net torque on the cup** is in the **same direction** as the rotor. Conflating these loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Assuming output frequency = shaft frequency in the AC tachogenerator:** This is wrong. Output frequency = reference frequency, **not** a multiple of shaft speed.
> 5. **Not stating the "why aluminium" answer explicitly** — examiner marks are reserved for the conductivity and non-magnetic arguments, not just "it is light."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drag-cup Tachometer — KTU 2024 Rapid-Fire Recap**

- **Definition:** Electromagnetic (eddy-current) speed transducer; uses a thin non-magnetic cup in a rotating magnetic field.
- **Two types:** (1) Indicating (pointer output), (2) A.C. Tachogenerator (voltage output).
- **Core formulas (must memorize):**
  - $T_{eddy} = K_1 \cdot \omega$, where $K_1 = \dfrac{\pi B_0^2 \ell^2 r^2}{R_c}$
  - $\theta = K \cdot N$ (pointer deflection linear in speed)
  - $E_0 = K_v \cdot N$, $f_{out} = f_{ref}$ (AC tachogenerator)
  - $K_v = \dfrac{P \cdot E_{ref}}{120 f}$
- **Three physics pillars:** Faraday's law (induction) → Eddy current → Lorentz force (torque) → Spring equilibrium (deflection).
- **Cup material:** Aluminium — non-magnetic + good conductivity + low inertia.
- **Frequency trick:** AC tachogenerator output frequency is **independent of speed**; only **amplitude** carries speed info.
- **Advantages:** Frictionless, no brushes, linear, low maintenance, wide speed range, good for control.
- **Limitations:** Sensitive to temperature (changes $R_c$), residual magnetism at low speed, requires stable AC reference.
- **Comparison anchors:** vs Centrifugal (frictionless, more accurate) | vs DC Tacho (no commutator, lower maintenance, but harder to sense direction).
- **KTU 2024 Examiner Expectations:** Construction with labelled parts, working principle in 3–4 crisp steps, derivation with units, comparison table with at least 6 parameters, and a numerical with **all steps shown**.
- **Common traps:** Sign of Lorentz force, units of $K_1$, frequency of output, "why aluminium" — all are 1-mark items each.

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Drag-cup Type Tachometer (PEICT416, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceleration: Null Balance Accelerometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Acceleration: Null Balance Accelerometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

A **Null Balance Accelerometer** (also called a **Force-Balance Accelerometer** or **Servo Accelerometer**) is a closed-loop, feedback-type acceleration-measuring transducer in which a **seismic (proof) mass** is actively maintained at a fixed reference (null) position by an **electromagnetic restoring force**. The magnitude of the feedback current required to sustain this null condition is directly proportional to the applied acceleration, providing an output signal of high linearity, accuracy, and dynamic range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT416 – Module 2):**
> The null-balance principle removes the dependency on the stiffness of the mechanical spring, which is the dominant source of non-linearity, hysteresis, and drift in conventional open-loop accelerometers. The instrument therefore belongs to the **closed-loop / servo class** of transducers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Invisible Hand"

Imagine you are holding a tray of water on a moving train. The water surface tilts whenever the train accelerates. Now suppose a tiny mechanical "hand" (an electromagnet) is constantly pushing the tray back to perfectly level — **the harder the train accelerates, the more force the hand must apply**.

- The **tray** = the seismic mass
- The **train's jerk** = the acceleration being measured
- The **invisible hand** = the electromagnetic feedback force
- The **effort the hand puts in** = the output current (which becomes your measurement)

This "effort" can be measured with extreme precision, even when the displacement of the tray is too small to see — that is the essence of the null-balance technique.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** In a null-balance accelerometer, **displacement is the error signal**, and the **feedback force is the measurement signal**. The seismic mass is never allowed to move appreciably from its reference position.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities at a Glance

The following quantities govern the behaviour of a null-balance accelerometer and must be memorized:

- **Seismic mass, $m$** — typically a few grams, expressed in **kg**.
- **Magnetic flux density, $B$** — strength of the permanent-magnet field, in **Tesla (T)**.
- **Length of the current-carrying conductor, $l$** — in **metres (m)**.
- **Restoring (electromagnetic) force, $F_e$** — produced by the coil, in **Newton (N)**.
- **Output current, $I_o$** — the measurable quantity, in **Ampere (A)**.
- **Applied acceleration, $a$** — in **m/s²**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details

A typical null-balance accelerometer consists of the following sub-assemblies:

1. **Seismic Mass Assembly** — a small proof mass suspended between two flexure springs (or a cantilever beam), located in the gap of a permanent magnet.
2. **Position Sensor (Pick-off)** — a non-contact displacement transducer (LVDT, capacitive plate pair, or differential transformer) that senses any micro-displacement of the mass from the null.
3. **Servo Amplifier** — a high-gain electronic amplifier that converts the position-sensor error signal into a coil-driving current.
4. **Restoring Coil (Force Generator)** — a voice-coil-type winding immersed in the magnetic field of the permanent magnet; the current through it produces the electromagnetic force $F_e$ opposing the inertial force.
5. **Damping Element** — often a dashpot or eddy-current damper ensuring dynamic stability of the servo loop.
6. **Output Stage** — delivers a voltage or current proportional to acceleration, often through a calibrated resistor (sense resistor).

## 2.2 Working Logic — Step-by-Step Operating Sequence

1. **Acceleration $a$ is applied** to the instrument's case along its sensitive axis.
2. The **inertial (pseudo) force** $F_i = m \cdot a$ acts on the seismic mass in the opposite direction (Newton's second law on a non-inertial frame).
3. The mass **tends to deflect** by a tiny amount against the flexure springs.
4. The **position sensor detects** this micro-displacement $\Delta x$ from the null and produces an error voltage.
5. The error voltage is **amplified by the servo amplifier** to drive the restoring coil.
6. The coil, sitting in the magnetic field $B$, generates a **Lorentz force** $F_e = B I_o l$ that pushes the mass back toward the null.
7. The loop reaches a steady state when $F_e = F_i$ and the mass is **restored to (or very near) the original null position**.
8. The **current $I_o$ flowing in the coil** is now a precise analogue of the input acceleration $a$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the system so accurate?**
> Because the proof mass barely moves, the spring stiffness, mass, and damping tolerances have a negligible effect on the measurement. The accuracy is dominated by the **linearity of the magnetic field $B$** and the **gain stability of the servo amplifier** — both of which can be made very stable.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relationship | Equation | Unit / Notes |
|---|------------------------|----------|--------------|
| 1 | Inertial force on proof mass | $F_i = m \cdot a$ | $N$ |
| 2 | Electromagnetic (Lorentz) restoring force | $F_e = B \, I_o \, l$ | $N$ |
| 3 | Null-balance equilibrium condition | $F_e = F_i$ | $N = N$ |
| 4 | Output current vs. acceleration | $I_o = \dfrac{m \cdot a}{B \cdot l}$ | $A$ |
| 5 | Output voltage across sense resistor $R_s$ | $V_o = I_o \cdot R_s = \dfrac{m \, R_s}{B \, l} \, a$ | $V$ |
| 6 | Sensitivity (current type) | $S_I = \dfrac{I_o}{a} = \dfrac{m}{B \, l}$ | $A / (m/s^2)$ |
| 7 | Sensitivity (voltage type) | $S_V = \dfrac{V_o}{a} = \dfrac{m \, R_s}{B \, l}$ | $V / (m/s^2)$ |
| 8 | Closed-loop bandwidth (approx.) | $f_{-3dB} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{servo} \cdot B l}{m}}$ | Hz |
| 9 | Open-loop natural frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz — design check |
| 10 | Damping ratio (closed loop) | $\zeta \approx \dfrac{c_{eq}}{2\sqrt{m \, K_{servo} \, B l}}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical boundary condition:** The above relations hold only when the servo loop gain $K_{servo}$ is sufficiently high that the steady-state displacement $\Delta x \rightarrow 0$ (true "null" condition).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Null-balance accelerometers are indispensable wherever **low-level, low-frequency, high-accuracy acceleration or tilt measurement** is required:

- **Aerospace:** Inertial navigation, flight-control surface feedback, and rocket-vibration monitoring where drift and non-linearity are unacceptable.
- **Civil Engineering:** Structural health monitoring of bridges, dams, and tall buildings (seismic activity).
- **Geophysics:** Earthquake and ground-motion instrumentation.
- **Automotive:** Ride-comfort analysis, airbag trigger threshold verification, and EV road-load testing.
- **Marine & Sub-sea:** Submarine tilt sensing, wave-buoy motion, and offshore platform monitoring.
- **Process Industries:** Inclination of large vessels, centrifuges, and rotating machinery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fundamental Output Equation of the Null-Balance Accelerometer

**Given:** A seismic mass $m$ suspended on flexure springs of stiffness $k$, immersed in a magnetic field $B$, with a coil of effective length $l$ carrying current $I_o$.

**To Derive:** The relationship between output current $I_o$ and applied acceleration $a$ at the null (force-balance) condition.

### Step 1 — Write the equation of motion of the proof mass

When the case of the accelerometer accelerates with $a$, the mass experiences a pseudo-force (in the frame of the case):

$$
F_i = m \cdot a
$$

If the mass deflects by $x$ from null, the flexure exerts a spring force $F_s = k x$, and the coil exerts a Lorentz force $F_e = B \, I_o \, l$ in the opposite direction.

The dynamic equation of motion is therefore:

$$
m \ddot{x} + c \dot{x} + k x = m a - B \, I_o \, l
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the steady-state null-balance assumption

At steady state and with very high loop gain, the displacement is forced to a vanishingly small value:

$$
x \rightarrow 0, \quad \dot{x} \rightarrow 0, \quad \ddot{x} \rightarrow 0
$$

Substituting these limits into the equation of motion:

$$
0 + 0 + 0 = m a - B \, I_o \, l
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the coil current

$$
B \, I_o \, l = m a
$$

$$
I_o = \frac{m a}{B \, l}
$$

### Step 4 — Introduce the sense resistor to obtain a voltage output

The coil current is passed through a precision sense resistor $R_s$. The output voltage is:

$$
V_o = I_o \, R_s = \frac{m \, R_s}{B \, l} \, a
$$

### Step 5 — Final Form and Sensitivity Definition

$$
\boxed{\,V_o = \left(\frac{m R_s}{B l}\right) a \quad \text{and} \quad S_V = \frac{m R_s}{B l}\,}
$$

The constant $\dfrac{m R_s}{B l}$ is the **voltage sensitivity** of the instrument, expressed in $V / (m/s^2)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Loop Bandwidth and Stability Margin

The closed-loop transfer function of the force-balance loop can be obtained by writing the loop equation in the Laplace domain.

### Step 1 — Plant (open coil-on-mass) transfer function

Treating the coil + magnetic circuit as a current-controlled force source with transconductance $K_f = B l$:

$$
\frac{X(s)}{I_o(s) - \dfrac{m}{B l} A(s)} = \frac{1}{m s^2 + c s + k}
$$

### Step 2 — Error detector and amplifier

Let the pick-off sensor have sensitivity $K_p$ (V/m) and the amplifier a gain $A_v$ (dimensionless). The error voltage is:

$$
E(s) = A_v \, K_p \, X(s)
$$

### Step 3 — Closed-loop characteristic equation

Substituting and gathering terms, the closed-loop characteristic equation becomes:

$$
m s^2 + c s + k + A_v K_p B l = 0
$$

Define the **effective closed-loop stiffness**:

$$
k_{\text{eff}} = k + A_v K_p B l
$$

### Step 4 — Closed-loop natural frequency and damping

$$
f_{n,cl} = \frac{1}{2 \pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{\text{eff}}}{m}}
$$

$$
\zeta_{cl} = \frac{c}{2 \sqrt{m \, k_{\text{eff}}}}
$$

As $A_v \rightarrow \infty$, $k_{\text{eff}} \rightarrow \infty$ and the proof mass becomes extremely stiff in position — justifying the **null-balance** approximation.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A null-balance accelerometer has a proof mass $m = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg}$, magnetic flux density $B = 1.2\ \text{T}$, effective conductor length in the field $l = 0.05\ \text{m}$, and sense resistor $R_s = 1000\ \Omega$. Find (a) the output current when $a = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ (i.e., 1 g), and (b) the output voltage. (c) What acceleration produces an output of $5\ \text{V}$?

### Solution (a) — Output current

$$
I_o = \frac{m a}{B l} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3})(9.81)}{(1.2)(0.05)}
$$

$$
I_o = \frac{4.905 \times 10^{-2}}{6 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.8175\ \text{A}
$$

### Solution (b) — Output voltage

$$
V_o = I_o R_s = (0.8175)(1000) = 817.5\ \text{V}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This voltage is impractically large; in real design, $B$, $l$, and $R_s$ are chosen so that the output is in the typical industrial range of $0$–$10\ \text{V}$ for full-scale $g$.

### Solution (c) — Acceleration for 5 V output

$$
V_o = \frac{m R_s}{B l} a \;\Rightarrow\; a = \frac{V_o \, B l}{m R_s} = \frac{(5)(1.2)(0.05)}{(5 \times 10^{-3})(1000)} = \frac{0.3}{5} = 0.06\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Null-Balance vs. Open-Loop Accelerometer

| Performance Parameter | Open-Loop (Spring–Mass) Accelerometer | Null-Balance (Force-Balance) Accelerometer |
|----------------------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Linearity | Limited by spring stiffness | Excellent — set by $B$, $l$ stability |
| Hysteresis | Present (spring material) | Virtually eliminated |
| Sensitivity to spring ageing | High | Negligible (mass stays at null) |
| Cross-axis sensitivity | Moderate | Low (closed-loop stiffness rejects it) |
| Dynamic range | Limited | Very wide (10⁵ or more) |
| Frequency response | Set by $k/m$ of spring | Extended by servo gain |
| Complexity & cost | Low | High (electronics + coil) |
| Typical applications | Vibration, shock | Navigation, tilt, seismic, structural |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Construction — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **signal and force flow** within a null-balance accelerometer. (Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rules, all node IDs are alphanumeric and labelled in plain uppercase text without any markdown formatting.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[INPUT ACCELERATION a APPLIED TO CASE] --> A2[SEISMIC PROOF MASS m EXPERIENCES INERTIAL FORCE F_i EQUAL TO m TIMES a]
    A2 --> A3[POSITION PICK OFF SENSOR DETECTS MICRO DISPLACEMENT DELTA x]
    A3 --> A4[SERVO AMPLIFIER HIGH GAIN PRODUCES ERROR VOLTAGE V_e]
    A4 --> A5[OUTPUT STAGE DRIVES CURRENT I_o THROUGH RESTORING COIL]
    A5 --> A6[COIL IMMERSED IN MAGNETIC FIELD B PRODUCES LORENTZ FORCE F_e EQUAL TO B I_o l]
    A6 --> A7[RESTORING FORCE F_e OPPOSES INERTIAL FORCE F_i]
    A7 --> A8{IS F_e EQUAL TO F_i AT NULL POSITION}
    A8 -- YES --> A9[STEADY STATE OUTPUT V_o IS PROPORTIONAL TO ACCELERATION a]
    A8 -- NO --> A3
    A9 --> A10[OUTPUT VIA SENSE RESISTOR R_s GIVES VOLTAGE OR CURRENT PROPORTIONAL TO a]
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Block Diagram (Control-Theory View)

The same instrument can be represented in classical control block-diagram form, showing the inner force-balance loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[ACCELERATION INPUT a] --> B2[SUMMING JUNCTION SUBTRACTOR]
    B2 --> B3[MASS SPRING DAMPER PLANT TRANSFER FUNCTION 1 OVER m s SQUARED PLUS c s PLUS k]
    B3 --> B4[DISPLACEMENT x OF PROOF MASS]
    B4 --> B5[POSITION SENSOR GAIN K_p]
    B5 --> B6[SERVO AMPLIFIER GAIN A_v]
    B6 --> B7[COIL DRIVER AND FORCE TRANSCONDUCTANCE K_f EQUAL TO B l]
    B7 --> B8[FORCE FEEDBACK SIGNAL F_e INTO SUMMING JUNCTION]
    B8 --> B2
    B4 --> B9[OUTPUT TAP TO MEASUREMENT CIRCUIT]
    B6 --> B10[OUTPUT VOLTAGE V_o]
```

## 4.3 Force-Balance Equilibrium Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[INERTIAL FORCE F_i EQUAL TO m a ACTING TO RIGHT] --> C2[NULL POSITION OF PROOF MASS]
    C3[ELECTROMAGNETIC FORCE F_e EQUAL TO B I l ACTING TO LEFT] --> C2
    C2 --> C4[NET FORCE AT NULL IS ZERO]
    C4 --> C5[OUTPUT I_o IS PROPORTIONAL TO a]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution pattern followed (KTU 2024 Scheme, ESE – End Semester Evaluation):**
> - Part A: Short-answer questions of **3 marks each** (Recall / Understand level).
> - Part B: Descriptive questions of **14 marks each**, with **internal choice** (Apply / Analyse / Evaluate level), split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define a null-balance accelerometer and state the principle on which it works.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A null-balance accelerometer is a closed-loop, force-balance type of acceleration transducer in which a seismic proof mass is held at a fixed reference (null) position by an electromagnetic restoring force. The principle of operation is the **force-balance (null-balance) principle**: the electromagnetic force produced by a coil in a magnetic field is automatically adjusted via a servo loop to exactly cancel the inertial force $m a$ acting on the mass, so that the **coil current becomes a direct measure of the input acceleration**. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**List any three advantages of a null-balance accelerometer over a conventional spring-mass accelerometer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Higher accuracy and linearity** — output depends on the linear magnetic force $BIl$ rather than on the non-linear spring constant. *(1 mark)*
2. **Negligible hysteresis** — the proof mass never deflects appreciably, so spring hysteresis is bypassed. *(1 mark)*
3. **Wider dynamic range and better cross-axis rejection** — the high closed-loop stiffness makes the mass insensitive to off-axis accelerations. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme rule: every Part B question carries an **internal choice** — the student must attempt **either OR**, not both.

---

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working** of a null-balance accelerometer. Clearly show the role of the position sensor, servo amplifier, and restoring coil.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **output equation** relating the output current $I_o$ to the applied acceleration $a$, and hence obtain the expression for **sensitivity**.

#### Model Solution — (a) Construction & Working [7 marks]

- **Construction** *(3 marks)*: The instrument consists of a **seismic mass** suspended on flexure springs inside a permanent-magnet assembly, a **position pick-off sensor** (LVDT / capacitive), a **high-gain servo amplifier**, a **restoring coil** (voice-coil type) wound on the mass, and a **sense resistor** for output. A schematic block diagram is shown in Section 4.1.
- **Working** *(3 marks)*: When acceleration $a$ acts on the case, the inertial force $F_i = ma$ tries to displace the mass. The pick-off generates an error signal proportional to the displacement. The servo amplifier boosts this signal and drives a current $I_o$ through the restoring coil. The coil, immersed in the magnetic field, produces $F_e = B I_o l$ opposing the inertial force.
- **Null condition** *(1 mark)*: At equilibrium $F_e = F_i$, the mass is restored to its null position and the loop becomes stable. The current $I_o$ is then an exact replica of the applied acceleration.

#### Model Solution — (b) Derivation & Sensitivity [7 marks]

Equating the inertial and electromagnetic forces at the null position:

$$
F_i = F_e
$$

$$
m a = B I_o l
$$

Solving for the output current:

$$
I_o = \frac{m a}{B l}
$$

The current sensitivity of the accelerometer is therefore:

$$
S_I = \frac{I_o}{a} = \frac{m}{B l} \quad \text{with units } A/(m/s^2)
$$

If a sense resistor $R_s$ is used, the output voltage is:

$$
V_o = I_o R_s = \frac{m R_s}{B l} \, a
$$

and the voltage sensitivity is:

$$
S_V = \frac{V_o}{a} = \frac{m R_s}{B l} \quad \text{with units } V/(m/s^2)
$$

**Conclusion:** The output is directly proportional to acceleration, with the proportionality constant determined by the mass, magnetic field, conductor length, and sense resistor. *(7 marks — full derivation credited)*

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]  *(Internal Choice to Question A)*

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **null-balance accelerometer** with the conventional **spring-mass accelerometer** in terms of accuracy, linearity, dynamic range, complexity, and typical applications.

**(b) [7 marks]** A null-balance accelerometer has the following specifications: $m = 2\ \text{g}$, $B = 0.8\ \text{T}$, $l = 0.04\ \text{m}$, $R_s = 500\ \Omega$. Calculate the output voltage when the input acceleration is $4\ \text{m/s}^2$.

#### Model Solution — (a) Comparative Analysis [7 marks]

| Parameter | Spring-Mass Accelerometer | Null-Balance Accelerometer |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Accuracy | Limited by spring tolerance | High (servo stabilises null) |
| Linearity | Depends on spring linearity | Excellent (linear Lorentz force) |
| Dynamic range | Moderate | Very wide |
| Hysteresis | Present | Negligible |
| Cross-axis rejection | Moderate | Excellent |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex (electronics required) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Typical use | Vibration / shock measurement | Inertial navigation, tilt, seismic, structural health monitoring |

*(1 mark per relevant comparison, max 7 marks — well-tabulated answer)*

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical Problem [7 marks]

**Given data:**
- $m = 2\ \text{g} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg}$
- $B = 0.8\ \text{T}$
- $l = 0.04\ \text{m}$
- $R_s = 500\ \Omega$
- $a = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Write the output voltage expression:**

$$
V_o = \frac{m R_s}{B l} \, a
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
V_o = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 500 \times 4}{0.8 \times 0.04}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$
(2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 500 \times 4 = 4.0
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$
0.8 \times 0.04 = 0.032
$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$
V_o = \frac{4.0}{0.032} = 125\ \text{V}
$$

**Answer:** The output voltage is $V_o = 125\ \text{V}$ for an input acceleration of $4\ \text{m/s}^2$. *(7 marks — full substitution and arithmetic credited)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the null-balance assumption** ($x \to 0$) when deriving the output equation — students often keep the spring and damping terms, which yields a wrong steady-state result. *(–2 marks)*
> 2. **Unit mismatch in numerical problems** — proof mass must be in **kilograms**, not grams. Many students leave $m$ in grams and obtain output voltages 1000× too large. *(–2 to –3 marks)*
> 3. **Confusing the electromagnetic force direction** — the Lorentz force must be drawn *opposing* the inertial force; reversing the direction gives a sign error. *(–1 mark)*
> 4. **Omitting the role of the sense resistor** in block-diagram explanations — the resistor converts current to voltage and is part of the output stage, not part of the servo loop. *(–1 mark)*
> 5. **Writing "accelerometer measures force"** — it measures **acceleration**; force is the internal mechanism, not the measurand. *(–1 mark)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **null-balance accelerometer** is a **closed-loop / force-balance / servo** type of accelerometer. *(Recall)*
- The seismic mass is held at a **null (reference) position** by an electromagnetic force generated by a coil in a permanent-magnet field. *(Understand)*
- The governing equation at steady-state null is $B I_o l = m a$, giving $I_o = \dfrac{m a}{B l}$. *(Apply)*
- **Sensitivity** (current) $S_I = m / (B l)$; with sense resistor $R_s$, voltage sensitivity $S_V = m R_s / (B l)$. *(Apply)*
- **Key advantages:** high accuracy, excellent linearity, negligible hysteresis, wide dynamic range, low cross-axis sensitivity. *(Understand)*
- **Key limitations / costs:** require stable electronics, high-gain servo amplifier, and precision magnetic assembly. *(Understand)*
- **Closed-loop natural frequency** rises with the loop gain $A_v$ as $f_{n,cl} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k + A_v K_p B l}{m}}$. *(Analyse)*
- **Real-world applications:** inertial navigation, flight control, structural health monitoring of bridges and dams, earthquake instrumentation, marine tilt sensing, automotive ride and crash testing. *(Apply)*
- **Numerical caution:** always convert proof mass to **kilograms** before substituting. *(Evaluate — pitfall avoidance)*
- **Comparison anchor:** null-balance ≠ spring-mass; the former is closed-loop, the latter is open-loop. *(Remember)*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Torque by Relative Angular Twist
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Torque by Relative Angular Twist # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Measurement of Torque by Relative Angular Twist** is a classical, non-electrical, elastic-deflection method of torque measurement in which the *angular displacement* produced between two cross-sections of a rotating shaft (separated by a known gauge length) is taken as the measurand. Since the applied torque `T` is directly proportional to this angular twist `θ` (within the elastic limit of the shaft material), the torque can be calculated from the fundamental torsion equation: $$T = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}$$ where `G` is the **Modulus of Rigidity**, `J` is the **Polar Moment of Inertia** of the shaft cross-section, and `L` is the axial distance between the two reference flanges/discs. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This method is classified under the **Elastic Force / Deflection Transducers** family and is widely used in laboratories, test rigs, and process industries for static and slowly varying torques because of its high accuracy, inherent linearity, and absence of slip rings or brushes. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are twisting the ends of a soft rubber rod (or even a rolled-up newspaper) with both hands in opposite directions. The rod will *coil* — one end will rotate relative to the other by a small angle. If the rubber were very stiff (like a steel rod), the angle would be microscopic, but it would still exist. Now imagine instead of rubber, you have a **steel drive shaft of a car** transmitting power from the engine to the rear wheels. The torque from the engine causes the front end of the shaft to twist a *tiny* bit ahead of the rear end. That tiny twist is exactly what we measure! **Key Intuition:** - A **stiffer shaft** (larger diameter, higher G) → **smaller twist** for the same torque. - A **longer gauge length** → **larger twist** (that's why we use `L` as a built-in amplifier). - A **stiffer material** (higher G, like steel ≈ 80 GPa) → **smaller twist** per unit torque. > [!IMPORTANT] > The twist in a typical steel shaft under working load is often less than **0.1°** — invisible to the naked eye. Therefore, the *art* of this method lies in **amplifying** that microscopic rotation using mechanical, optical, or electro-mechanical means (lever, mirror, LVDT, or strain gauges). This is a classic KTU high-yield point. ## 1.3 Key Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis) | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Modulus of Rigidity (Steel) | **G** | **≈ 80 GPa (77–83)** | | Modulus of Rigidity (Aluminium) | **G** | **≈ 27 GPa** | | Modulus of Rigidity (Brass) | **G** | **≈ 39 GPa** | | Typical working twist | **θ** | **0.5° to 2°** (in measuring range) | | Polar Moment (Solid, dia `d`) | **J** | **πd⁴/32** | | Polar Moment (Hollow, OD `D`, ID `d`) | **J** | **π(D⁴ − d⁴)/32** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Physics — "Why It Works" When a circular shaft is subjected to a pure torque `T` about its longitudinal axis: 1. **Every cross-section rotates by a small angle** relative to the fixed end. 2. The **angle of twist grows linearly** with the distance from the fixed reference. 3. The deformation is **purely elastic** (recoverable) as long as the shear stress `τ_max ≤ τ_allowable`. 4. The shaft material behaves like a **linear torsion spring** with stiffness: $$K_t = \frac{T}{\theta} = \frac{GJ}{L} \quad [\text{N·m/rad}]$$ This is the angular analog of Hooke's law for linear springs (`F = kx`). ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic - **Step 1 — Mounting:** A cylindrical shaft (the *sensing element*) is coupled between the driving and driven members. - **Step 2 — Reference Flanges:** Two precisely machined **flanges/discs** are keyed to the shaft at a known distance `L`. - **Step 3 — Loading:** Torque applied → front flange rotates by `θ₁`, rear flange by `θ₂`. The *relative* angular twist is `θ = θ₁ − θ₂`. - **Step 4 — Pick-off:** A mechanical pointer, optical mirror, or transducer senses this `θ`. - **Step 5 — Computation:** Substitute measured `θ` into `T = GJθ/L` to obtain the torque. - **Step 6 — Indication:** `T` is displayed either as a numerical readout (calibrated scale) or recorded graphically. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Dynamometer test benches** for engines, motors, gearboxes, pumps. - **Process industries** for monitoring torque on agitators, mixers, extruders. - **Aircraft and ship propulsion** test rigs. - **Educational laboratories** for verifying torsional theory. - **Wind turbine drivetrains** for condition monitoring. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **NEVER use the vertical pipe `|` inside table cells — it breaks markdown table syntax. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values or unit separation.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Angle of twist | $\theta = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$ | rad | Core relation | | 2 | Torque (from twist) | $T = \dfrac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}$ | N·m | Used in calibration | | 3 | Polar moment (solid) | $J_s = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{4}}{32}$ | m⁴ | For solid circular shaft | | 4 | Polar moment (hollow) | $J_h = \dfrac{\pi \, (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$ | m⁴ | `D` outer, `d` inner | | 5 | Shear stress (max) | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^{3}}$ | Pa | Solid shaft design check | | 6 | Angular stiffness | $K_t = \dfrac{G \cdot J}{L}$ | N·m/rad | Torsion spring constant | | 7 | Twist in degrees | $\theta^{\circ} = \theta^{\text{rad}} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ | degrees | Conversion | | 8 | Power (related) | $P = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W | `N` in rpm | | 9 | Design shear stress | $\tau_{allow} = \dfrac{\tau_y}{\text{FOS}}$ | Pa | For elastic-limit design | | 10 | Mechanical amplifier gain | $A = \dfrac{\text{pointer length}}{\text{lever arm}}$ | dimensionless | Pointer-lever type | ### Design Guidelines (often asked in KTU viva & Part A) - **Choose `θ_max ≈ 1°` (≈ 0.01745 rad)** in the middle of the working range. - **Limit shear stress** to roughly **30 % – 50 % of yield shear stress** for safety and linearity. - **Prefer hollow shafts** (higher `J` per unit mass → higher sensitivity). - **Use long gauge length `L`** to *amplify* the twist, but avoid excessive compliance that would affect system dynamics. - **Compensate for temperature** because `G` varies slightly with temperature (≈ −0.03 %/°C for steel). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Angle of Twist Equation Consider a uniform circular shaft of length `L`, fixed at one end and free at the other, subjected to a torque `T` at the free end. **Step A — Shear strain at radius `r`:** A line on the surface originally parallel to the axis becomes a helix. At any radius `r`, the shear strain `γ` is: $$\gamma(r) = \frac{r \cdot \phi}{L}$$ where `φ` is the angle of twist in radians. Here `φ` is the same symbol as `θ` in many textbooks — they represent the same quantity. **Step B — Shear stress at radius `r` (Hooke's law in shear):** $$\tau(r) = G \cdot \gamma(r) = \frac{G \cdot r \cdot \phi}{L}$$ **Step C — Differential torque on a ring of area `dA = 2πr dr`:** $$dT = \tau(r) \cdot dA \cdot r = \frac{G \cdot r \cdot \phi}{L} \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) \cdot r = \frac{2\pi G \phi}{L} \cdot r^{3} \, dr$$ **Step D — Integrate from `0` to outer radius `R`:** $$T = \int_0^R \frac{2\pi G \phi}{L} \cdot r^{3} \, dr = \frac{2\pi G \phi}{L} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{\pi G R^{4} \phi}{2L}$$ **Step E — Substitute `J = πR⁴/2 = πd⁴/32`:** $$T = \frac{G J \phi}{L} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \phi = \frac{T L}{G J}$$ This is the **master equation** for the relative angular twist method. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** If a problem gives you angular twist in **degrees**, convert to radians first using `θ_rad = θ_deg × π/180` *before* substituting into `T = GJθ/L`.
