topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capillarity and Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Surface Tension — The Origin of Capillarity

### Formal Definition
**Surface tension** ($\gamma$) is defined as the tensile force acting per unit length of an imaginary line drawn on the free surface of a liquid, acting perpendicular to that line and tangent to the surface. It arises due to the imbalance of cohesive intermolecular forces experienced by molecules at the liquid-air interface. Mathematically:

$$\gamma = \frac{F}{L} \quad \text{[N/m]}$$

where $F$ is the surface tensile force in **Newtons (N)** and $L$ is the length of the line in **meters (m)**.

Alternatively, surface tension can be interpreted as the excess potential energy per unit area of the free surface, with units of **Joules per square meter (J/m²)** — numerically equivalent to N/m.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Surface tension is the *root cause* of capillarity. Every capillary phenomenon (rise, depression, meniscus shape) is a direct consequence of unbalanced cohesive forces at the liquid boundary.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a stretched rubber sheet lying flat on a table. The rubber sheet "pulls" along its edges — this pull is essentially what surface tension does on a liquid's free surface. Molecules *inside* the bulk liquid are pulled equally in all directions by their neighbors, but molecules *on the surface* have no neighbors above them (only air). This creates a net inward pull, causing the surface to behave like a stretched elastic membrane trying to minimize its area. That "tightness" of the surface is the surface tension.

---

## 1.2 Capillarity — The Phenomenon

### Formal Definition
**Capillarity** (or **capillary action**) is the ability of a liquid to flow in narrow spaces (capillary tubes, porous media, or thin films) without the assistance of, and often in opposition to, external forces like gravity. It is caused by the combination of **cohesive forces** (liquid–liquid attraction) and **adhesive forces** (liquid–solid surface attraction).

When a thin glass tube is dipped into water, the water *climbs up* inside the tube. When dipped in mercury, the mercury level *falls below* the external surface. These are the two classic capillary phenomena:

- **Capillary Rise** (e.g., water in glass) — occurs when **adhesion > cohesion**
- **Capillary Depression** (e.g., mercury in glass) — occurs when **cohesion > adhesion**

### The Contact Angle
The **angle of contact** ($\theta$) is the angle formed at the liquid-solid interface, measured *inside the liquid* between the tangent to the liquid surface and the solid surface.

| Liquid-Solid Pair | Contact Angle | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Water on clean glass | $\theta < 90°$ | Wetting; meniscus concave → capillary **rise** |
| Mercury on glass | $\theta > 90°$ | Non-wetting; meniscus convex → capillary **depression** |
| Water on paraffin wax | $\theta \approx 105°$ | Non-wetting; capillary depression |
| Mercury on clean steel | $\theta \approx 154°$ | Highly non-wetting |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Tip:** A *concave* meniscus always corresponds to capillary *rise*. A *convex* meniscus always corresponds to capillary *depression*.

---

## 1.3 Viscosity — The Internal Resistance

### Formal Definition
**Viscosity** ($\mu$) is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the relative motion of its layers. It is the fluid's internal friction. According to **Newton's Law of Viscosity**, the shear stress ($\tau$) between two adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) normal to the direction of flow:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress in **N/m²** or **Pa**
- $\mu$ = dynamic (absolute) viscosity in **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**
- $\frac{dv}{dy}$ = velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction, in **s⁻¹**

### Two Key Viscosity Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dynamic Viscosity** | $\mu$ | Internal resistance to flow | **Pa·s** |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | $\nu$ | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density | **m²/s** |

The relationship is:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the fluid density in **kg/m³**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of honey pouring from a spoon compared to water pouring from the same spoon. The honey flows sluggishly because its internal layers resist sliding over one another — it has high viscosity. Water flows easily — it has low viscosity. Imagine two glass plates with a thin film of fluid between them; if you push the top plate, the fluid layer just beneath it moves faster than the layer near the bottom plate. Viscosity is the "stickiness" between these layers that *resists* the differential motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** Liquids **decrease** in viscosity with temperature rise (intermolecular bonds weaken), while gases **increase** in viscosity with temperature (momentum transfer intensifies). This is a frequently tested conceptual point.

---

## 1.4 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Foundation

Consider a fluid layer sandwiched between two parallel plates, with the lower plate fixed and the upper plate moving with velocity $U$. Fluid in contact with the lower plate is stationary; fluid in contact with the upper plate moves at velocity $U$. A linear velocity profile develops:

$$v(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$

The velocity gradient is constant: $\frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{U}{h}$. By Newton's law:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h}$$

A fluid satisfying this linear relationship is called a **Newtonian fluid** (water, air, oil, alcohol). Fluids that do **not** obey this linear law are called **Non-Newtonian fluids** (blood, paint, ketchup, polymer solutions).

---

## 1.5 Capillary Action & Viscosity — Why They Matter in Instrumentation

In mechanical instrumentation, both phenomena are fundamental to several measurement techniques:

- **Capillary viscometers** (e.g., Ostwald viscometer) measure viscosity from the *time of fall* of a liquid column through a capillary tube.
- **Capacitance-type humidity sensors** rely on moisture-induced changes in capillary surfaces of hygroscopic dielectrics.
- **Manometer fluids** are chosen partly based on their capillary behaviour in narrow tubes.
- **Lubrication theory** in bearings is a direct application of viscosity in mechanical instrument design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Capillary Rise — Jurin's Law (Detailed Analysis)

Consider a capillary tube of internal radius $r$ dipped into a liquid reservoir. The liquid rises to a height $h$ inside the tube, forming a concave meniscus. We perform a force balance on the liquid column:

### Force Balance Step-by-Step

1. **Upward Force: Surface Tension Component**
   The surface tension acts along the circumference of the meniscus circle. Only the *vertical component* supports the liquid column:
   
   $$F_{up} = \gamma \cdot (2\pi r) \cdot \cos\theta$$
   
   This is the surface tension $\gamma$ acting along perimeter $2\pi r$, with the vertical component extracted by $\cos\theta$.

2. **Downward Force: Weight of Liquid Column**
   The volume of the risen liquid is approximately $\pi r^2 h$ (neglecting the small meniscus volume). The weight is:
   
   $$F_{down} = (\pi r^2 h) \cdot \rho \cdot g$$
   
   where $\rho$ = liquid density, $g$ = gravitational acceleration.

3. **Equilibrium Condition**
   At static equilibrium, $F_{up} = F_{down}$:

   $$\gamma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta = \pi r^2 h \rho g$$

4. **Solving for Height — Jurin's Law**
   
   $$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This expression is known as **Jurin's Law**, named after James Jurin (1718). It is the single most important equation for capillary rise in KTU problems.

### Key Inferences from Jurin's Law

- $h \propto \frac{1}{r}$ → Narrower tubes produce **higher** capillary rise.
- $h \propto \gamma$ → Higher surface tension → greater rise.
- $h \propto \cos\theta$ → Maximum rise occurs at $\theta = 0°$ (perfect wetting).
- For $\theta > 90°$, $\cos\theta < 0$, giving **negative $h$** → capillary depression.

---

## 2.2 Capillary Correction in Manometers

In a U-tube manometer, the meniscus curvature introduces an error in reading the manometric head. The **capillary rise correction** is subtracted (or added) from the observed difference:

$$h_{corrected} = h_{observed} \pm \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

- The sign is **positive** (added) for depression cases (mercury in glass) and **negative** (subtracted) for rise cases (water in glass).

---

## 2.3 Pressure Difference Across a Curved Liquid Interface (Laplace's Law)

For a spherical liquid surface of radius $R$, the pressure difference between the inside and outside is:

$$\Delta P = P_{inside} - P_{outside} = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$$

For a soap bubble (two surfaces):

$$\Delta P = \frac{4\gamma}{R}$$

For a liquid drop:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **pressure is always higher on the concave side** of the meniscus than on the convex side. This pressure difference is what drives liquid up the capillary tube.

---

## 2.4 Newton's Law of Viscosity — Expanded Analysis

Consider a fluid flowing over a flat stationary plate. Let $u(y)$ be the velocity at height $y$ from the plate. The shear stress at any point is:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

### Special Cases in Mechanical Instrumentation

**Couette Flow (Flow between two parallel plates):**
A Newtonian fluid between two parallel plates separated by distance $h$, with the top plate moving at velocity $U$ and bottom plate stationary:

$$u(y) = \frac{U}{h} y$$

$$\tau = \mu \frac{U}{h} = \text{constant}$$

**Flow through a pipe (Hagen-Poiseuille Flow):**
For steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a circular pipe of radius $R$ under a pressure gradient $\frac{dP}{dx}$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4}{8\mu} \left(-\frac{dP}{dx}\right)$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** and is *the* governing formula for capillary viscometer design.

---

## 2.5 Viscosity Variation with Temperature

| Fluid Type | Temperature Dependence | Empirical Relation |
|---|---|---|
| **Liquids** | Viscosity **decreases** with $T$ | $\mu_T = \mu_0 \cdot e^{E/(RT)}$ (Andrade's equation) |
| **Gases** | Viscosity **increases** with $T$ | $\mu \propto \sqrt{T}$ (Sutherland's equation) |

For liquids, $E$ is the activation energy for viscous flow, $R$ is the universal gas constant.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **complete, exam-ready formula set** for capillarity and viscosity. Memorize every entry — KTU problems are direct applications of these.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma = F/L$ | Surface tension (force basis) | **N/m** |
| 2 | $\gamma = W/\Delta A$ | Surface tension (energy basis) | **J/m²** |
| 3 | $h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | **Jurin's Law** (capillary rise) | **m** |
| 4 | $\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$ | Laplace pressure (drop, meniscus) | **Pa** |
| 5 | $\Delta P = \frac{4\gamma}{R}$ | Laplace pressure (bubble) | **Pa** |
| 6 | $\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$ | **Newton's Law of Viscosity** | **Pa** |
| 7 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | Kinematic viscosity | **m²/s** |
| 8 | $Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille (volumetric flow) | **m³/s** |
| 9 | $F = 6\pi \mu r v$ | Stokes' drag on a sphere | **N** |
| 10 | $\mu_{water} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ at 20°C | Dynamic viscosity of water | **Pa·s** |
| 11 | $\gamma_{water} \approx 0.0728$ at 20°C | Surface tension of water | **N/m** |
| 12 | $\mu_T = \mu_0 e^{E/(RT)}$ | Andrade's equation (liquids) | **Pa·s** |

> **Note on Table Notation:** For absolute value expressions such as $\vert h \vert$ or $\vert \cos\theta \vert$, KTU examiners may write magnitudes. Be careful to apply the appropriate sign based on the wetting/non-wetting case.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Phenomenon | Application in Mechanical Instrumentation |
|---|---|
| **Capillary rise** | Capillary viscometers, liquid feed systems in inkjet printers, wick-based cooling |
| **Capillary depression** | Mercury manometers, mercury thermometers (fluid selection) |
| **Surface tension** | Bubble meters, droplet-based flow sensors, capillary electrophoresis |
| **Viscosity** | Lubricant selection in bearings, hydraulic fluid design, damper calibrations |
| **Stokes' law** | Particle sizing in fluids, sedimentation analyzers, dust removal cyclones |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise)

We derive Jurin's Law from first principles with **every algebraic step** explicitly shown. This is a classic 7–8 mark derivation that appears frequently in KTU examinations.

### Step 1: Setup and Free Body Diagram

Consider a glass capillary tube of internal radius $r$, dipped into a liquid reservoir of density $\rho$ and surface tension $\gamma$. The liquid wets the glass with contact angle $\theta < 90°$, and rises to height $h$ inside the tube above the free surface of the reservoir.

A free body diagram of the risen liquid column is sketched below:

```
        Air outside            Air inside
           |                      |
           v                      v
    ======Liquid surface==========
           |                      |
           |       γ acts along   |
           |   ──→ ←──────→      |   ← 2πr (perimeter)
           |   perimeter of       |
           |     meniscus         |
           |                      |
           |         |←——— h ———→|
           |         |            |
    [Liquid Reservoir]          [Capillary Tube]
                                
        Forces:  F_up = 2πr·γ·cosθ  (vertical component of γ)
                 F_down = πr²hρg    (weight of column)
```

### Step 2: Surface Tension Force

The surface tension $\gamma$ acts along the circumference of the meniscus where liquid meets the inner tube wall. The total length of this contact line is:

$$L = 2\pi r$$

The total surface tension force is $\gamma \cdot L$, directed **along the liquid surface** tangent to the meniscus. This makes angle $\theta$ with the vertical tube wall. The **vertical component** (upward) is:

$$F_{up} = \gamma \cdot (2\pi r) \cdot \cos\theta$$

### Step 3: Weight of the Liquid Column

The volume of the liquid column raised inside the capillary (approximately a cylinder, neglecting the small meniscus volume at the top):

$$V = \pi r^2 h$$

Mass of the column:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot \pi r^2 h$$

Weight (downward force due to gravity):

$$F_{down} = m \cdot g = \pi r^2 h \rho g$$

### Step 4: Static Equilibrium Equation

At equilibrium, the upward and downward forces balance. Setting $F_{up} = F_{down}$:

$$\gamma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta = \pi r^2 h \rho g$$

### Step 5: Algebraic Simplification

Divide both sides by $\pi r$:

$$\gamma \cdot 2 \cdot \cos\theta = r h \rho g$$

Solve for $h$ by dividing both sides by $r \rho g$:

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r \rho g}$$

### Step 6: Final Form — Jurin's Law

$$\boxed{h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}}$$

This completes the derivation. 

### Step 7: Sign Analysis and Depression Case

For mercury on glass, $\theta > 90°$, so $\cos\theta < 0$, giving $h < 0$. The negative sign indicates that the liquid level *falls* below the reservoir surface — this is **capillary depression**. The magnitude of depression is:

$$\vert h_{depression} \vert = \frac{2\gamma \cos(180° - \theta)}{\rho g r} = \frac{2\gamma \vert \cos\theta \vert}{\rho g r}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Laplace's Law for a Spherical Surface

We derive the pressure difference across a curved liquid interface. Consider a spherical bubble of radius $R$ in a liquid. The excess pressure $\Delta P$ inside pushes the bubble outward, balanced by the inward pull of surface tension.

### Step 1: Work Done by Surface Tension

Suppose the bubble radius increases by a small amount $dR$ (i.e., $R \to R + dR$). The change in surface area is:

$$dA = 4\pi (R + dR)^2 - 4\pi R^2$$

$$dA = 4\pi (R^2 + 2R\,dR + dR^2) - 4\pi R^2$$

Neglecting the second-order term $dR^2$:

$$dA = 8\pi R\,dR$$

### Step 2: Energy Equivalence

The work done by the excess pressure $\Delta P$ in expanding the bubble volume by $dV$ equals the work done against surface tension:

$$\Delta P \cdot dV = \gamma \cdot dA$$

The volume change of a sphere is:

$$dV = 4\pi R^2 dR$$

### Step 3: Substituting

$$\Delta P \cdot (4\pi R^2 dR) = \gamma \cdot (8\pi R\,dR)$$

### Step 4: Simplification

Divide both sides by $4\pi R\,dR$:

$$\Delta P \cdot R = 2\gamma$$

### Step 5: Final Form

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}}$$

This is the pressure excess across a *single* spherical interface (a droplet). For a soap bubble (two surfaces, inner and outer), the result doubles:

$$\Delta P_{bubble} = \frac{4\gamma}{R}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Hagen-Poiseuille Flow

For fully developed laminar flow in a horizontal circular pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$, the volumetric flow rate is derived as follows.

### Step 1: Force Balance on a Cylindrical Fluid Element

Consider a coaxial cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $dx$. Pressure acts on its flat ends; viscous shear acts on its curved lateral surface.

Pressure force on the left face: $+P \cdot \pi r^2$
Pressure force on the right face: $-(P + dP) \cdot \pi r^2$
Net pressure force: $-dP \cdot \pi r^2$

Shear stress acts on the curved surface of area $2\pi r\,dx$, opposing flow (in the $-x$ direction):

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

(negative sign because velocity decreases with increasing $r$)

Viscous force on the element: $\tau \cdot 2\pi r\,dx$

### Step 2: Equilibrium (Steady State)

For steady flow with no acceleration, net force = 0:

$$-dP \cdot \pi r^2 + \tau \cdot 2\pi r\,dx = 0$$

Substituting $\tau$:

$$-dP \cdot \pi r^2 + \left(-\mu \frac{du}{dr}\right) \cdot 2\pi r\,dx = 0$$

$$-dP \cdot r = 2\mu \frac{du}{dr} dx$$

### Step 3: Separation of Variables

$$\frac{dP}{dx} dx = \frac{2\mu}{r} du$$

Integrate from $r$ to $R$ (where $u = 0$ at the wall, $u = u_{max}$ at center):

$$\int_0^L \frac{dP}{dx} dx = 2\mu \int_{u_{max}}^{0} \frac{du}{r} \cdot \frac{1}{?}$$

A cleaner approach: integrate the equation $r \cdot dP/dx = 2\mu \cdot du/dr$ with $dP/dx$ constant:

$$du = \frac{r}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dx} dr$$

Integrating from $r$ to $R$:

$$u(0) - u(R) = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dx} \int_0^R r\,dr = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dx} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2}$$

Using $u(R) = 0$ (no-slip at wall):

$$u_{max} = -\frac{R^2}{4\mu} \frac{dP}{dx}$$

The general velocity profile is:

$$u(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu} \frac{dP}{dx} (R^2 - r^2)$$

### Step 4: Volumetric Flow Rate

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r\,dr = -\frac{\pi}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dx} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) r\,dr$$

$$Q = -\frac{\pi}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dx} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = -\frac{\pi}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dx} \left[\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right]$$

$$Q = -\frac{\pi}{2\mu} \frac{dP}{dx} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

### Step 5: Final Form

$$\boxed{Q = -\frac{\pi R^4}{8\mu} \frac{dP}{dx}}$$

Or, with a finite pressure drop $\Delta P$ over length $L$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation**, the cornerstone of capillary viscometry.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Parameter | Capillarity (Static) | Viscosity (Dynamic) |
|---|---|---|
| **Governing Equation** | Jurin's Law | Newton's / Hagen-Poiseuille |
| **Primary Force** | Surface tension | Internal friction |
| **Key Variable** | Tube radius $r$ | Velocity gradient $dv/dy$ |
| **Typical Setup** | Tube dipped in liquid | Fluid in pipe / between plates |
| **Measurement Device** | Capillary viscometer, manometer | Viscometer (Ostwald, Redwood) |
| **Time Dependency** | Static equilibrium | Steady / unsteady flow |
| **Controlling Property** | $\gamma$, $\theta$ | $\mu$, $\nu$ |

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **physical–causal chain** of capillary rise, from surface-molecular forces to measurable height.

```mermaid
    C --> D["Meniscus forms with Contact Angle theta"]

    E -- Yes --> F["Concave meniscus: Capillary RISE"]
    E -- No --> G["Convex meniscus: Capillary DEPRESSION"]
    F --> H["Vertical component of gamma supports column"]
    H --> I["Weight of column equals surface tension force"]
    I --> J["Jurins Law: h = 2 gamma cos theta / rho g r"]
    G --> K["Vertical component opposes gravity"]
    K --> L["Level falls below reservoir surface"]
    L --> M["Magnitude: h = 2 gamma cos theta / rho g r (negative)"]

    M --> N
```

This block diagram illustrates the **mechanism of viscous shear** and how it manifests in physical flow scenarios.


A typical **Ostwald capillary viscometer** measures kinematic viscosity from the *time* a fixed volume of liquid takes to flow through a calibrated capillary.

```mermaid
    D --> E["Liquid meniscus passes Mark M2: Stop timer t2"]
    E --> F["Efflux Time t = t2 minus t1 recorded"]
    F --> G["Compare with Calibration Liquid of known nu"]
    G --> H["Compute: nu_sample over nu_water = t_sample over t_water"]
    H --> I["nu_sample = nu_water times t_sample over t_water"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define surface tension. State its SI unit and explain with a molecular theory why a liquid surface behaves like a stretched membrane.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Surface tension is the tensile force per unit length acting on an imaginary line drawn on the free surface of a liquid, the force being perpendicular to the line and tangent to the surface.

$$\gamma = \frac{F}{L}$$

**SI Unit:** **N/m** (equivalently **J/m²**)

**Molecular Explanation:** Molecules in the bulk liquid are pulled equally in all directions by surrounding molecules, resulting in zero net force. However, molecules at the surface have no neighbors above (only air/vacuum), so the net force on them is directed *inward* (toward the bulk). To remain at the surface, the molecule must possess higher potential energy, and the surface behaves as if it is in a state of tension — analogous to a stretched elastic membrane trying to minimize its surface area. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish clearly between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Internal resistance to flow; appears in Newton's law $\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$ | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density |
| Formula | $\mu$ directly | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ |
| SI Unit | **Pa·s** (or N·s/m²) | **m²/s** |

Key difference: Dynamic viscosity measures the absolute resistance, while kinematic viscosity measures the resistance relative to the fluid's inertia (mass). Kinematic viscosity is the relevant parameter in flow problems dominated by gravity (e.g., capillary viscometers, natural convection). (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4, Internal Choice Set 1]**

**(a)** Derive Jurin's law for capillary rise. State clearly the assumptions made in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A glass capillary tube of internal diameter 0.8 mm is dipped into water at 20°C. Calculate the height of capillary rise. Given: surface tension of water at 20°C is **0.0728 N/m**, density of water is **1000 kg/m³**, and contact angle for water-glass is **0°**. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Setup:** Consider a clean glass capillary tube of internal radius $r$ dipped vertically into a liquid reservoir. The liquid wets the tube and rises to a height $h$ above the free surface of the reservoir, forming a concave meniscus with contact angle $\theta$ (measured through the liquid).

**Assumptions:**
1. The capillary tube is perfectly cylindrical with uniform radius $r$.
2. Contact angle $\theta$ is constant along the contact line (clean, smooth surface).
3. The meniscus volume is negligible compared to the cylindrical column.
4. The liquid reservoir is large enough that the external level is essentially unchanged.
5. Temperature is uniform; surface tension $\gamma$ and density $\rho$ are constant.
6. Liquid is in static equilibrium (no flow).

**Free Body Diagram of the Liquid Column:**

```
             ___________
            /           \   ← concave meniscus
           |             |
           |  liquid     |  ←  height h
           |  column     |
           |             |
    _______|_____________|_______
                                 
    ←————————— 2πr  —————————→   (perimeter)
```

**Force Analysis:**

(i) **Upward Force (Surface Tension Component):**

Surface tension $\gamma$ acts tangentially along the meniscus perimeter $2\pi r$. The vertical component (upward) is:

$$F_{up} = \gamma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta$$

(ii) **Downward Force (Weight of Liquid Column):**

Volume of the column: $V = \pi r^2 h$ (cylinder)
Mass: $m = \rho \cdot \pi r^2 h$
Weight:

$$F_{down} = \pi r^2 h \rho g$$

**Equilibrium Condition:**

$$\gamma \cdot 2\pi r \cos\theta = \pi r^2 h \rho g$$

**Solving for h:**

$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r \rho g}$$

This is **Jurin's Law**. ∎ (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Internal diameter $d = 0.8$ mm, so radius $r = 0.4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $\gamma = 0.0728$ N/m
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $\theta = 0°$, so $\cos\theta = 1$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Apply Jurin's Law:**

$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.0728 \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step-by-step Calculation:**

Numerator: $2 \times 0.0728 = 0.1456$ N/m

Denominator: $1000 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 3.924$ N/m²

$$h = \frac{0.1456}{3.924} = 0.03711 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 37.11 \text{ mm} \approx 3.71 \text{ cm}}$$

**Result Interpretation:** The water rises about 3.7 cm inside the capillary tube — a measurable quantity for standard laboratory cathetometers. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4, Internal Choice Set 2]**

**(a)** State and explain Newton's law of viscosity. A fluid of dynamic viscosity 0.9 Pa·s flows between two parallel horizontal plates separated by 5 mm. The upper plate moves at 0.5 m/s while the lower plate is stationary. Calculate the shear stress on the lower plate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define kinematic viscosity. With a neat sketch, describe the working of an Ostwald viscometer and derive the expression used to determine the kinematic viscosity of an unknown liquid. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Newton's Law of Viscosity Statement:**

The shear stress ($\tau$) developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient) and the constant of proportionality is the dynamic viscosity ($\mu$).

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$$

**Explanation:** Consider a fluid element of thickness $dy$ between two parallel layers. If the upper layer moves with velocity $v + dv$ and the lower with $v$, the layer deforms at rate $dv/dy$. Internal friction (viscosity) gives rise to a shear stress $\tau$ that resists this deformation. Fluids obeying this linear relation are Newtonian; otherwise non-Newtonian.

**Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.9$ Pa·s
- Gap between plates: $h = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Upper plate velocity: $U = 0.5$ m/s
- Lower plate: stationary

**Velocity gradient (linear profile for Couette flow):**

$$\frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.5}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Shear stress on lower plate:**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy} = 0.9 \times 100 = 90 \text{ N/m}^2 = 90 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 90 \text{ Pa}}$$

This is the force per unit area the fluid exerts on the lower plate. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definition:** Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$) is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{[m}^2\text{/s]}$$

**Ostwald Viscometer — Working Principle:**

The Ostwald viscometer is a U-tube glass apparatus with:
- A **wide upper bulb (U)** for holding the liquid sample,
- A **lower bulb (R)** of known volume, 
- A **capillary (C)** of calibrated length and radius,
- Two **reference marks M1 and M2** above and below the lower bulb.

**Schematic Diagram:**

```
            |‾‾‾‾| ← M1
            |    |
            |    | ← Upper Bulb (U)
            |    |
            |____| ← M2
              |  |
              |C | ← Capillary
              |  |
              |R | ← Lower Bulb (R)
              |__|
            (Reservoir)
```

**Working Procedure:**

1. A fixed volume $V$ of the test liquid is pipetted into the lower bulb.
2. The liquid is sucked up through the capillary until it rises above mark M1.
3. The suction is released; the liquid flows down by gravity through the capillary.
4. The time $t_1$ taken by the liquid meniscus to fall from M1 to M2 is recorded using a stopwatch.
5. The experiment is repeated with a calibration liquid (typically distilled water) of known kinematic viscosity $\nu_w$, and time $t_2$ is recorded under identical conditions (same volume, same temperature).

**Derivation of the Expression:**

Under identical head and geometry, the Hagen-Poiseuille equation gives:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}$$

For Ostwald viscometer, $\Delta P$ is the hydrostatic head $\rho g H$ (since flow is gravity-driven). Thus:

$$\frac{V}{t} = \frac{\pi R^4 \rho g H}{8\mu L}$$

Rearranging:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{\pi R^4 g H \cdot t}{8 L \cdot V}$$

For the *same* viscometer and identical volume $V$, all geometric constants are the same. For two liquids under identical conditions:

$$\frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2} = \frac{t_1}{t_2}$$

If liquid 2 is water (calibration liquid of known $\nu_w$):

$$\boxed{\nu_{sample} = \nu_w \cdot \frac{t_{sample}}{t_{water}}}$$

This allows determination of unknown kinematic viscosity from a single time measurement. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Jurin's Law Units:** Students frequently forget to convert **mm to m** in tube radius, leading to off-by-1000 errors. Always convert all lengths to SI (meters) before substituting.
> 
> 2. **Sign of $\cos\theta$:** For mercury (non-wetting), students often plug in a *positive* value, giving nonsensical *rise*. Remember: mercury has $\theta > 90°$, $\cos\theta < 0$, giving capillary *depression*.
> 
> 3. **Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Confusion:** Do not apply Newton's law $\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$ to non-Newtonian fluids (blood, paint) with a single constant $\mu$. The viscosity *varies* with shear rate.
> 
> 4. **Hagen-Poiseuille Pressure Term:** Many students write $Q = \frac{\pi R^4 P}{8\mu L}$ instead of $Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}$. The *pressure difference* is required, not absolute pressure.
> 
> 5. **Viscosity of Gases vs Liquids:** A common conceptual trap — students incorrectly state that viscosity of *all* fluids increases with temperature. **Only gases** do; liquids *decrease*.
> 
> 6. **Ostwald Calibration:** Students often forget that the *same viscometer* and *same volume* are essential for the simple ratio $\nu_1/\nu_2 = t_1/t_2$ to be valid.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Capillarity and Viscosity**

**Capillarity Essentials:**
- ☐ Surface tension $\gamma$ has units **N/m** or **J/m²** (numerically identical).
- ☐ Capillary rise occurs when $\theta < 90°$ (wetting); depression when $\theta > 90°$ (non-wetting).
- ☐ **Jurin's Law:** $h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ — memorize the formula and every variable.
- ☐ $h \propto 1/r$ — narrower tube → higher rise.
- ☐ **Laplace's Law:** $\Delta P = 2\gamma/R$ for a drop, $4\gamma/R$ for a bubble.
- ☐ Pressure is always higher on the **concave** side of a meniscus.

**Viscosity Essentials:**
- ☐ **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$ — foundation of all viscous flow.
- ☐ Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ has units **Pa·s**; Kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$ has units **m²/s**.
- ☐ Newtonian fluids obey $\tau \propto \frac{dv}{dy}$ linearly; non-Newtonian do not.
- ☐ **Hagen-Poiseuille:** $Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}$ — for laminar flow in a circular pipe.
- ☐ Couette flow: linear velocity profile, constant shear stress $\tau = \mu U/h$.
- ☐ **Liquids: viscosity DECREASES with T**; **Gases: viscosity INCREASES with T**.
- ☐ **Stokes' Law:** $F_d = 6\pi \mu r v$ (drag on a sphere — relevant to falling-ball viscometer).

**Instrumentation Connections:**
- ☐ Ostwald / Redwood / Saybolt viscometers → viscosity measurement using capillary principles.
- ☐ Capillary correction needed in **manometer readings** for high-precision work.
- ☐ Lubricant viscosity directly affects bearing performance → critical in mechanical instrument design.
- ☐ Mercury vs. water choice in manometers/thermometers depends on capillarity behavior.
- ☐ Surface tension governs performance of bubble meters, droplet sensors, capillary electrophoresis.

**Numerical Problem Toolkit:**
- ☐ Always convert all units to SI (m, kg, s, N) before plugging in.
- ☐ Standard values: $\gamma_{water} = 0.0728$ N/m, $\mu_{water} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s at 20°C.
- ☐ For mercury: $\gamma \approx 0.485$ N/m, $\rho = 13600$ kg/m³, $\theta \approx 140°$ on glass.
- ☐ $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless otherwise specified.

**Common KTU Question Patterns:**
- ☐ "Derive Jurin's Law" — 7 marks, requires full force balance and algebraic derivation.
- ☐ "State and explain Newton's law of viscosity" — classic 3-mark Part A.
- ☐ "Calculate the capillary rise/depression given diameter, surface tension, and contact angle" — numerical Part B sub-question.
- ☐ "Differentiate Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluids with examples" — frequently asked 3-mark question.
- ☐ "Explain the working of Ostwald viscometer" — likely 7-mark Part B with a neat sketch.
