topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Newton's Law of Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: NEWTON'S LAW OF VISCOSITY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Newton's Law of Viscosity** is a fundamental constitutive equation in fluid mechanics that defines the behavior of a real, viscous fluid in laminar flow. It states that the **shear stress** ($\tau$) developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the **rate of shear strain** (velocity gradient) and is constant for a given temperature and pressure.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress acting tangentially between fluid layers, in **N/m²** or **Pa**
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of dynamic (absolute) viscosity, in **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of flow, in **s⁻¹**

Fluids that obey this linear relationship between shear stress and velocity gradient are termed **Newtonian Fluids** (e.g., water, air, thin oils).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This law forms the foundation for deriving equations like the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**, the **Reynolds number**, and the analysis of **boundary layers** — all of which are critical for flow-measurement instrumentation such as orifice meters, venturimeters, and Pitot-static tubes covered later in this module.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thick crowd of people trying to walk through a narrow corridor. The person at the very front moves fastest. The person right behind them drags slightly. The next person drags a little more, and so on. Each person feels a "pulling back" force from the person in front, and a "pushing forward" force from the person behind. This internal friction between layers of motion is exactly what **viscosity** represents in a fluid.

Another simpler analogy:
- **Honey on a spoon** flows slowly because its layers strongly resist sliding past one another — it has high viscosity.
- **Water on a spoon** flows quickly because its layers slide easily — it has low viscosity.

**Physical Constants to Remember:**
- **Standard dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\mu_{water} \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ **Pa·s**
- **Standard kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C:** $\nu_{water} \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ **m²/s**
- **1 Poise (CGS unit)** = 0.1 Pa·s ; **1 centipoise (cP)** = 0.001 Pa·s

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** In KTU examinations, when the term *viscosity* is asked without prefix, the student must state clearly whether they mean **dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)** or **kinematic viscosity ($\nu = \mu/\rho$)**. Marks are frequently lost for this ambiguity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Mechanism

When a real fluid flows over a solid surface (no-slip condition), the layer in immediate contact with the surface is stationary, while layers farther away move with increasing velocity. This creates a **velocity gradient** perpendicular to the flow direction.

**Why does viscosity exist?**
- It arises due to **molecular momentum transfer** between adjacent fluid layers.
- Molecules in faster-moving layers possess higher momentum and transfer it to slower-moving layers, producing an internal friction (shear stress).
- This microscopic molecular exchange is what macroscopically manifests as viscosity.

**How does the law work step-by-step?**

1. Consider two parallel fluid layers separated by a small distance $dy$.
2. The lower layer moves with velocity $u$ and the upper layer with velocity $u + du$.
3. The relative velocity difference is $du$ across distance $dy$.
4. A shear force $F$ is required to maintain this relative motion.
5. By experiment, $F \propto A$ (area of contact) and $F \propto \dfrac{du}{dy}$.
6. Therefore: $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A} = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$, where $\mu$ is the constant of proportionality (dynamic viscosity).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity (defining equation) | Pa = N/m² |
| 2 | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{du/dy}$ | Dynamic (absolute) viscosity definition | Pa·s |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity (where $\rho$ is fluid density) | m²/s |
| 4 | $F = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous force between two parallel fluid layers | N |
| 5 | $\gamma = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Rate of shear strain (velocity gradient) | s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$ | CGS to SI conversion for dynamic viscosity | — |
| 7 | $1 \text{ Stoke} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ | CGS to SI conversion for kinematic viscosity | — |
| 8 | $\mu_{water}(20°C) = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s | Reference benchmark value | Pa·s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory LaTeX Convention:** In all derivations, use the LaTeX vertical bar command `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\mu \mid_{T=20°C}$) instead of the keyboard pipe `|` symbol. This prevents the markdown table parser from breaking the equation rows.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Newton's Law of Viscosity is the cornerstone for:
- **Flow measurement instrumentation** (Orifice plates, Venturi meters, Rotameters, Capillary viscometers)
- **Lubrication engineering** — designing bearings where oil films must sustain shear stress without breakdown
- **Pipeline design** — calculating pressure drops using the Hagen-Poiseuille relation
- **Aerodynamic boundary layer analysis** — determining skin-friction drag on aircraft surfaces
- **Hydraulic systems** — selecting proper hydraulic fluid viscosities for given operating temperatures
- **Process industries** — controlling fluid consistency in paints, polymers, food processing

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** The course **PEICT416 – Mechanical Instrumentation** heavily relies on this law to justify the working of **capillary-tube viscometers**, **rotational viscometers**, and **falling-ball viscometers**, all of which translate viscous shear into measurable signals (pressure drop, torque, or time of fall).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Newton's Law of Viscosity

#### Setup: Two Parallel Plates in Relative Motion

Consider two large, flat, parallel plates of area $A$, separated by a small distance $dy$. The lower plate is **stationary**, and the upper plate moves with a constant velocity $U$ under the action of an applied tangential force $F$. The space between the plates is filled with a fluid.

**Step 1 — Statement of Empirical Observation**

Experimental investigations by Sir Isaac Newton (1687) revealed that the tangential force $F$ required to maintain the upper plate at constant velocity $U$ depends on:
- The contact area $A$ of the fluid layer
- The relative velocity $U$ between the plates
- The separation distance $dy$ between the plates

This is written symbolically as:

$$F \propto A, \quad F \propto U, \quad F \propto \frac{1}{dy}$$

**Step 2 — Combining the Proportionalities**

Combining all three proportionalities into a single expression:

$$F \propto A \cdot \frac{U}{dy}$$

**Step 3 — Introduction of the Constant of Proportionality**

Introducing the constant $\mu$, called the **coefficient of dynamic viscosity**:

$$F = \mu \cdot A \cdot \frac{U}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to Shear Stress Form**

Dividing both sides by the contact area $A$ to obtain the shear stress $\tau$:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{dy}$$

**Step 5 — Generalization to a Velocity Gradient**

In a real fluid, the velocity does not change in discrete jumps. It varies continuously from $0$ at the stationary lower plate to $U$ at the moving upper plate. The ratio $U/dy$ is therefore replaced by the more general velocity gradient $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ measured at any arbitrary point in the fluid:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

This is the **mathematical statement of Newton's Law of Viscosity**.

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation of $\mu$**

Rearranging the defining equation:

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy} = \frac{\text{Shear Stress}}{\text{Rate of Shear Strain}}$$

**Interpretation:** $\mu$ represents the shear stress required to produce a unit rate of shear strain. Fluids with higher $\mu$ require greater shear stress to deform at the same rate — they are "thicker" or more viscous.

**Step 7 — Derivation of the Kinematic Viscosity Relation**

By definition, the **dynamic viscosity** relates shear stress to velocity gradient, while the **density** $\rho$ accounts for the fluid's inertia per unit volume. The **kinematic viscosity** $\nu$ is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to density:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Dimensional Analysis of $\mu$:**

$$\left[\mu\right] = \frac{\left[\tau\right]}{\left[du/dy\right]} = \frac{\text{N/m}^2}{\text{(m/s)/m}} = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

**Dimensional Analysis of $\nu$:**

$$\left[\nu\right] = \frac{\left[\mu\right]}{\left[\rho\right]} = \frac{\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

#### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.9$ Pa·s flows between two parallel plates separated by a gap of $dy = 5$ mm. The velocity of the upper plate is $U = 0.75$ m/s and varies linearly across the gap. Calculate the shear stress on the fluid and the viscous force per unit area.

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.9$ Pa·s
- $dy = 5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $U = 0.75$ m/s

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Velocity Gradient: } \frac{du}{dy} &= \frac{U}{dy} = \frac{0.75}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ s}^{-1} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.75}{0.005} \text{ s}^{-1} = 150 \text{ s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\text{Shear Stress: } \tau &= \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.9 \times 150 \text{ N/m}^2 \\[4pt]
&= 135 \text{ N/m}^2
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\text{Force per unit area} &= \tau = 135 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}

**Result:** The fluid exerts a shear stress of **135 N/m²** (or 135 Pa) on the plates.

#### 3.3 Numerical: Converting cP to Pa·s

**Problem:** A lubricant has a viscosity of 250 cP. Express it in Pa·s and in Poise.

**Solution:**

\begin{aligned}
\mu &= 250 \text{ cP} = 250 \times 0.001 \text{ Pa·s} = 0.25 \text{ Pa·s} \\[4pt]
\mu &= 0.25 \text{ Pa·s} = \frac{0.25}{0.1} \text{ Poise} = 2.5 \text{ Poise}
\end{aligned}

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flow of the Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID BETWEEN TWO PLATES] --> B[LOWER PLATE STATIONARY]
    A --> C[UPPER PLATE MOVES AT VELOCITY U]
    B --> D[NO-SLIP CONDITION: u = 0 AT LOWER WALL]
    C --> E[FLUID VELOCITY u INCREASES LINEARLY]
    D --> F[VELOCITY GRADIENT du/dy FORMS]
    E --> F
    F --> G[APPLY EMPIRICAL PROPORTIONALITY F proportional to A x U / dy]
    G --> H[INTRODUCE PROPORTIONALITY CONSTANT mu]
    H --> I[SHEAR STRESS tau = mu times du/dy]
    I --> J[NEWTONS LAW OF VISCOSITY ESTABLISHED]
    J --> K[FLUID CLASSIFIED AS NEWTONIAN]
    J --> L[ENABLES VISCOSITY INSTRUMENT DESIGN]
    L --> M[CAPILLARY VISCOMETER]
    L --> N[ROTATIONAL VISCOMETER]
    L --> O[FALLING BALL VISCOMETER]
```

### 4.2 Velocity Profile & Shear Stress Schematic (Mermaid Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[HIGH VELOCITY U] -->|Top Plate| Q1[Fluid Layer Moves Faster]
    Q1 --> R1[Internal Momentum Transfer]
    R1 --> S1[Velocity Decreases Linearly Downward]
    S1 --> T1[Stationary Bottom Plate u = 0]
    T1 --> U1[Shear Stress tau ACTS BETWEEN LAYERS]
    U1 --> V1[tau = mu times du/dy]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[FLUID BEHAVIOR UNDER SHEAR] --> X1[NEWTONIAN: tau vs du/dy is LINEAR]
    X --> X2[NON-NEWTONIAN: tau vs du/dy is NON LINEAR]
    X1 --> X1A[Water, Air, Thin Oils, Alcohol]
    X1 --> X1B[Viscosity mu is CONSTANT]
    X2 --> X2A[Shear Thinning: Blood, Paint]
    X2 --> X2B[Shear Thickening: Cornstarch Slurry]
    X2 --> X2C[Bingham Plastic: Toothpaste]
    X2 --> X2D[Thixotropic: Ketchup]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** and **logical flow** of Newton's Law of Viscosity. In physical instrumentation textbooks, the velocity profile is drawn as a straight line rising from zero at the stationary wall to $U$ at the moving wall, with the slope equal to $du/dy$. The shear stress $\tau$ is depicted as a tangential force on each layer, opposing the relative motion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Newton's Law of Viscosity. Write its mathematical expression and explain the significance of the proportionality constant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Statement:** Newton's Law of Viscosity states that the shear stress developed in a fluid during laminar flow is directly proportional to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) perpendicular to the direction of flow.
- **(1 mark) Equation:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$
- **(1 mark) Significance of $\mu$:** The constant $\mu$ is the **coefficient of dynamic viscosity**. It represents the internal resistance of the fluid to flow. Its value depends on the type of fluid and temperature, and it is constant for Newtonian fluids.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate clearly between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) |
|----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| Definition | Resistance to deformation under shear stress | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density |
| Mathematical Form | $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$ | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ |
| SI Unit | Pa·s (or N·s/m²) | m²/s |

- **(1 mark)** Definitions as above.
- **(1 mark)** Relation: $\nu = \mu / \rho$.
- **(1 mark)** Units: Pa·s for $\mu$ and m²/s for $\nu$.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a) Derive Newton's Law of Viscosity from fundamental principles with a neat sketch. Explain the physical significance of each term involved. (7 marks)**
**(b) Two parallel plates 1 cm apart are moving with a relative velocity of 1.5 m/s. The space between them is filled with oil of dynamic viscosity 0.85 Pa·s. Calculate the shear stress and the viscous force acting on an area of 0.4 m². (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Setup: Two parallel plates of area $A$ separated by distance $dy$, lower plate stationary, upper plate moving at velocity $U$.
- **(1 mark)** Empirical observation: $F \propto A$, $F \propto U$, $F \propto 1/dy$.
- **(1 mark)** Combine: $F = \mu A (U/dy)$.
- **(1 mark)** Divide by area: $\tau = \mu (U/dy)$.
- **(1 mark)** Generalize: $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$.
- **(1 mark)** Physical significance of $\tau$, $\mu$, and $du/dy$.
- **(1 mark)** Final statement of the law and identification of $\mu$ as the constant of dynamic viscosity.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $dy = 1 \text{ cm} = 0.01$ m, $U = 1.5$ m/s, $\mu = 0.85$ Pa·s, $A = 0.4$ m².

**Step 1 — Velocity gradient:**

\begin{aligned}
\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{dy} = \frac{1.5}{0.01} = 150 \text{ s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Shear stress:**

\begin{aligned}
\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.85 \times 150 = 127.5 \text{ N/m}^2
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Viscous force:**

\begin{aligned}
F = \tau \cdot A = 127.5 \times 0.4 = 51 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}

**Final Answer:** $\tau = 127.5$ N/m², $F = 51$ N.

---

**Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**(a) Define viscosity. With the help of a diagram, derive Newton's Law of Viscosity and explain the role of molecular momentum transfer. (7 marks)**
**(b) The kinematic viscosity of a fluid is $3.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s and its specific gravity is 0.78. Calculate the dynamic viscosity of the fluid in Pa·s and in centipoise. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**
- **(1.5 marks)** Definition of viscosity as the property that offers resistance to deformation.
- **(1.5 marks)** Diagram of two parallel plates with linear velocity profile.
- **(2 marks)** Derivation (same as Question A part (a)).
- **(1 mark)** Role of molecular momentum transfer: faster-moving molecules transfer momentum to slower-moving layers, creating internal friction (viscous shear).
- **(1 mark)** Conclusion and statement of Newton's Law.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\nu = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s, $SG = 0.78$, so $\rho = 0.78 \times 1000 = 780$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Dynamic viscosity:**

\begin{aligned}
\mu = \nu \cdot \rho = 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 780 = 0.273 \text{ Pa·s}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Convert to centipoise:**

\begin{aligned}
\mu = 0.273 \text{ Pa·s} = 0.273 \times 1000 \text{ cP} = 273 \text{ cP}
\end{aligned}

**Final Answer:** $\mu = 0.273$ Pa·s = 273 cP.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Failing to convert mm or cm to meters when calculating $du/dy$ is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks in numericals. Always write units in every step.
> 2. **Confusing $\mu$ and $\nu$:** Many students interchange dynamic and kinematic viscosity. Remember: $\mu$ involves **force (Pa·s)**; $\nu$ involves **area (m²/s)**.
> 3. **Forgetting the "no-slip condition":** Sketches that show fluid slipping at the wall are physically wrong and will be penalized.
> 4. **Missing the linear velocity profile assumption:** Newton's law derivation assumes a **linear** velocity gradient, which only holds in steady, laminar Couette flow.
> 5. **Forgetting that $\mu$ depends on temperature, not pressure (for liquids):** Examiners often ask this as a follow-up; mention that $\mu$ of liquids **decreases** with temperature, while $\mu$ of gases **increases** with temperature.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$
- **Dynamic Viscosity $\mu$** = Shear stress / Velocity gradient; **Unit: Pa·s**
- **Kinematic Viscosity $\nu$** = $\mu / \rho$; **Unit: m²/s**
- **Newtonian fluids** obey a **linear** $\tau$ vs $du/dy$ relationship (constant $\mu$).
- **Non-Newtonian fluids** have a **non-linear** relationship (variable apparent viscosity).
- **CGS Conversions:** 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s; 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s; 1 Stoke = 10⁻⁴ m²/s
- **No-slip condition:** Fluid in contact with a solid boundary has the same velocity as the boundary.
- **Temperature effect:** For liquids, $\mu \downarrow$ as $T \uparrow$; for gases, $\mu \uparrow$ as $T \uparrow$.
- **Engineering applications:** Viscometers, lubrication, pipeline pressure drop, boundary layer theory.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always include a **neat labeled diagram** for full marks in derivations. Diagrams carry at least 1–2 marks by themselves.
- **Dimension check:** $[\mu] = M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ ; $[\nu] = L^2 T^{-1}$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Statics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fluid Statics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Fluid Statics

**Fluid Statics** (also called *Hydrostatics*) is the branch of fluid mechanics that studies fluids at rest — that is, fluids in equilibrium under the action of body forces (primarily gravity) and surface forces (pressure), with no shear stress or relative motion between fluid layers.

Mathematically, the foundational condition of fluid statics is:

$$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = 0$$

Since the velocity field is constant in time at every point, the only forces acting on a fluid element are pressure forces and body forces. This gives the **Eulerian Hydrostatic Equation** (the governing equation of fluid statics):

$$\nabla P = \rho \vec{g}$$

In scalar form for a fluid of constant density under gravity acting in the negative *z*-direction:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial P}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Fluid statics forms the theoretical backbone for almost every pressure-measuring instrument studied in this module — including manometers, dead-weight testers, Bourdon gauges, and diaphragm capsules. You are expected to derive hydrostatic equations and apply them to instrument calibration and design problems.

## 1.2 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$ (often rounded to **9.81 m/s²**)
- **Density of water (at 4°C):** $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of mercury (at 0°C):** $\rho_{\text{Hg}} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Specific weight:** $\gamma = \rho g$, with units of **N/m³**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 760 \text{ mmHg} = 14.696 \text{ psi}$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stack of Invisible Books"

Imagine the fluid as a tall stack of transparent, weightless books piled on top of each other inside a tank. The bottom book does not feel only its own weight — it feels the cumulative weight of **every book above it**. The pressure at the bottom of a fluid column is therefore directly proportional to the **height of the column** and the **density of the fluid**.

This is why:

- A deep diver feels more pressure on their eardrums than a swimmer at the surface.
- A tall column of water can burst a dam at the bottom even if the surface looks calm.
- Mercury, being 13.6 times denser than water, needs only a 1/13.6-th tall column to balance atmospheric pressure — which is why barometers use mercury instead of water.

In instrumentation, we **reverse this logic**: instead of asking "what pressure does this fluid produce?", we ask "given an unknown pressure, how tall a fluid column can it support?" — and that height becomes our measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Every manometer works on the principle of converting a pressure difference into a measurable height (head) of a manometric fluid. Mastering $P = \rho g h$ is non-negotiable for this module.

## 1.4 Governing Principles (At a Glance)

| # | Principle | Statement | Instrument Application |
|---|-----------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Pascal's Law** | Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions. | Hydraulic press, hydraulic brakes |
| 2 | **Hydrostatic Pressure Law** | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Manometers, level measurement |
| 3 | **Continuity of Pressure** | At the same elevation in a connected static fluid, pressure is the same. | Differential manometer design |
| 4 | **Buoyancy (Archimedes)** | A body immersed in a fluid experiences an upward force equal to the weight of fluid displaced. | Hydrometers, level sensors |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation of the Hydrostatic Equation from First Principles

Consider a small fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ at rest inside a static fluid. The forces acting on it are:

1. **Pressure force on the +x face:** $P \cdot dy \, dz$ (acting in the +x direction)
2. **Pressure force on the -x face:** $\left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} dx\right) dy \, dz$ (acting in the -x direction)
3. Similarly for y and z faces.
4. **Body force (gravity):** $-\rho g \cdot dx \, dy \, dz$ in the z-direction.

Applying Newton's second law for equilibrium ($\sum F = 0$):

**x-direction:**

$$P \, dy \, dz - \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} dx\right) dy \, dz = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} dx \, dy \, dz = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = 0$$

**y-direction:**

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial y} = 0$$

**z-direction:**

$$P \, dx \, dy - \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} dz\right) dx \, dy - \rho g \, dx \, dy \, dz = 0$$

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} dz \, dx \, dy - \rho g \, dx \, dy \, dz = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign indicates that **pressure decreases as elevation increases**. A student who writes $\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = +\rho g$ will lose marks in KTU valuation — the convention is critical.

## 2.2 The Master Hydrostatic Equation

For a fluid of constant density, integrating $\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g$ from elevation $z_1$ (where pressure is $P_1$) to elevation $z_2$ (where pressure is $P_2$):

$$P_2 - P_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1)$$

Defining $h = z_1 - z_2$ as the **vertical depth** of point 1 below point 2:

$$\boxed{P_1 = P_2 + \rho g h}$$

This is the **single most important equation** in the entire Fluid Statics module.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Absolute pressure at depth $h$ below free surface | Pa |
| 2 | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Gauge pressure definition | Pa |
| 3 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Absolute pressure from gauge | Pa |
| 4 | $\Delta P = \rho g \Delta h$ | Pressure difference across a fluid column | Pa |
| 5 | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Specific weight | N/m³ |
| 6 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Specific gravity (dimensionless) | — |
| 7 | $P = \rho g h$ (in terms of SG) | $P = SG \cdot \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa |
| 8 | $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Pressure head (head of fluid) | m |
| 9 | $F_b = \rho_{\text{fluid}} \, g \, V_{\text{displaced}}$ | Buoyant force (Archimedes) | N |
| 10 | $P_1 A_1 = P_2 A_2$ | Pascal's law (hydraulic lever) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** Throughout your KTU answer scripts, always use the **vertical** form. For differential manometers, the sign of each term depends on whether the manometric fluid is **above** or **below** the reference level. KTU examiners award step marks specifically for the correct sign convention in manometer equations.

## 2.4 Manometric Instruments (The Heart of the Module)

### 2.4.1 Simple U-Tube Manometer

A U-tube containing a manometric fluid (typically mercury or a heavy liquid) measures the pressure of a fluid in a pipe/tank by balancing the unknown pressure against the column of manometric fluid.

For a pipe containing fluid of density $\rho_1$ at pressure $P_1$, with a U-tube manometer using a manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ (where $\rho_m > \rho_1$), and the manometer reading $h$:

$$P_1 = P_2 + (\rho_m - \rho_1) g h$$

where $P_2$ is the pressure on the open side (often atmospheric, giving gauge pressure).

### 2.4.2 Inclined Manometer

To amplify small pressure differences, one limb of the U-tube is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The vertical height corresponding to a length $l$ along the inclined tube is:

$$h = l \sin\theta$$

The amplified reading $l$ is $\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}$ times larger than the equivalent vertical column — providing higher sensitivity for low-pressure measurements.

### 2.4.3 Well-Type (Reservoir) Manometer

One limb has a large cross-sectional reservoir (well) and the other is a narrow tube. As pressure pushes the manometric fluid down in the well and up in the tube, the drop in the well is negligible (because the well's area is very large compared to the tube), so almost the entire deflection appears in the narrow tube — increasing sensitivity.

### 2.4.4 Differential Manometer

Used to measure the **pressure difference** between two points in a system. The general manometer equation is obtained by tracing the pressure from one point to the other, adding $\rho g h$ when going **down**, and subtracting when going **up**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dead-Weight Pressure Tester:** A primary standard for pressure calibration. A known weight is placed on a piston of known area; the pressure generated is $P = \dfrac{W}{A}$. Fluid statics guarantees this pressure is transmitted undiminished to the gauge under test.
- **Bourdon Tube Calibration:** Hydrostatic principles govern the dead-weight tester used to calibrate industrial Bourdon gauges.
- **Tank Level Measurement:** Hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of a tank is directly proportional to liquid level — enabling level measurement using a single pressure sensor at the bottom.
- **Baro-Thermometric Altimetry:** Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude according to the hydrostatic equation, enabling aircraft altimeters.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Pressure Variation in a Compressible Fluid (Atmosphere)

For a compressible fluid such as the atmosphere, density $\rho$ is itself a function of elevation $z$. Assuming an **isothermal atmosphere** (temperature $T$ = constant), the ideal gas law gives:

$$P = \rho R T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{P}{RT}$$

Substituting into the hydrostatic equation $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\frac{P g}{R T}$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R T} dz$$

Integrating from $z = 0$ (where $P = P_0$, the reference pressure) to elevation $z$:

$$\int_{P_0}^{P(z)} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{R T} \int_{0}^{z} dz$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{P(z)}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{g z}{R T}$$

$$P(z) = P_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{g z}{R T}\right)$$

This is the **isothermal barometric formula** — a key result KTU expects you to know for compressible fluid statics.

For the **adiabatic atmosphere** (where temperature varies with altitude as $T = T_0 - L z$, $L$ being the lapse rate), the integration yields the more complex form:

$$P(z) = P_0 \left(1 - \frac{L z}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g}{R L}}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Buoyancy Force and Centre of Buoyancy

Consider a body of arbitrary shape fully submerged in a static fluid of density $\rho_f$. The pressure at any point on the body's surface depends only on depth. The net vertical force on the body is found by integrating the pressure over the entire wetted surface.

**Vertical force on an elemental horizontal strip** at depth $z$ with projected area $dA_{horiz}$:

$$dF_v = P(z) \, dA_{horiz}$$

The net vertical force (sum of upward components) is:

$$F_b = \int_A P(z) \, dA_{horiz}$$

Substituting $P(z) = P_0 + \rho_f g (z_0 - z)$, where $z_0$ is the free surface elevation:

$$F_b = \int_A \left[P_0 + \rho_f g (z_0 - z)\right] dA_{horiz}$$

The first term (atmospheric + constant hydrostatic) integrates to zero over the closed surface (this is a consequence of the divergence theorem applied to a constant pressure field). Therefore:

$$F_b = \rho_f g \int_A (z_0 - z) \, dA_{horiz}$$

The integral $\int_A (z_0 - z) \, dA_{horiz}$ is exactly the **volume of fluid displaced** by the body, $V_{\text{displaced}}$. Hence:

$$\boxed{F_b = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{displaced}}}$$

This is **Archimedes' Principle**. The point at which this buoyant force effectively acts is called the **centre of buoyancy** — it coincides with the centroid of the displaced fluid volume.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Manometer

| Application | Pressure Range | Recommended Manometer | Reason |
|-------------|----------------|------------------------|--------|
| High-pressure boiler (~ 10 MPa) | Very high | Dead-weight tester | Direct primary standard, no fluid needed |
| Medium gas pressure (~ 100 kPa) | Medium | U-tube mercury manometer | Robust, accurate, simple |
| Very low gas pressure differential (~ 10 Pa) | Low | Inclined manometer with low-density fluid (oil, alcohol) | High amplification via small $\theta$ |
| Differential pressure in flow meter | Variable | Inverted U-tube or differential manometer | Measures $\Delta P$ directly |
| Field-portable gauge calibration | Variable | Well-type manometer | Compact, single-limb reading |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Typical KTU Problem

**Problem:** A U-tube manometer is connected to a pipe carrying water. The manometric fluid is mercury. The mercury level in the limb open to atmosphere is 250 mm below the pipe centerline, while the mercury level in the limb connected to the pipe is 50 mm above the pipe centerline. The pipe contains water of density 1000 kg/m³ above the mercury in the connecting limb. Calculate the pressure in the pipe.

**Step 1: Identify datum.** Take the lower mercury level (in the open limb) as the reference.

**Step 2: Trace pressure from the pipe to the open end.**

Starting at the pipe centerline (point A) at pressure $P_A$, going **down** through water by 50 mm to reach the mercury surface in the connected limb (point B):

$$P_B = P_A + \rho_w g h_{AB}$$

where $h_{AB} = 0.050 \text{ m}$ (downward, so we add).

So $P_B = P_A + (1000)(9.81)(0.050) = P_A + 490.5 \text{ Pa}$.

**Step 3: Cross the mercury interface.** At the same horizontal level within a connected static fluid, pressure is the same. So at the level of the open-limb mercury surface (point C), the pressure equals:

$$P_C = P_B + \rho_{Hg} g \cdot h_{BC}$$

where $h_{BC}$ is the **difference in mercury heights** between the two limbs.

The mercury in the connected limb is at 50 mm above the pipe centerline, and the mercury in the open limb is 250 mm below the pipe centerline. The difference is:

$$h_{BC} = 250 - (-50) = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.300 \text{ m}$$

Wait — let us re-evaluate. The mercury in the connected limb is 50 mm **above** the pipe centerline. The mercury in the open limb is 250 mm **below** the pipe centerline. So the open-limb mercury is lower than the connected-limb mercury by:

$$h_{BC} = 50 + 250 = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.300 \text{ m}$$

Pressure at the level of the open mercury surface, coming through mercury from point B:

$$P_C = P_B + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{BC}$$

$$P_C = P_A + 490.5 + (13600)(9.81)(0.300)$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the mercury term.**

$$(13600)(9.81)(0.300) = 40024.8 \text{ Pa} \approx 40.025 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5: Apply open-end condition.** Point C is exposed to the atmosphere, so $P_C = P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ (or the problem may ask for gauge pressure, in which case $P_C = 0$ gauge).

Setting $P_C = P_{atm}$:

$$101325 = P_A + 490.5 + 40024.8$$

$$P_A = 101325 - 40515.3 = 60809.7 \text{ Pa} \approx 60.81 \text{ kPa (absolute)}$$

If gauge pressure is required:

$$P_{A,\text{gauge}} = P_A - P_{atm} = 60809.7 - 101325 = -40515.3 \text{ Pa} \approx -40.52 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$

The negative gauge pressure indicates a **partial vacuum** in the pipe.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrostatic Pressure Distribution in a Tank

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Free Surface P0 = Patm] --> B[Depth h1 - Pressure P1 = P0 + rho g h1]
    B --> C[Depth h2 - Pressure P2 = P0 + rho g h2]
    C --> D[Depth h3 - Pressure P3 = P0 + rho g h3]
    D --> E[Bottom of Tank Pmax = P0 + rho g H]
    E --> F[Pressure increases linearly with depth]
    F --> G[Slope of pressure-depth line equals specific weight gamma = rho g]
```

**Block-Level Interpretation of the above flow:**

| Node | Physical Meaning |
|------|------------------|
| A | Free surface of the liquid at atmospheric pressure |
| B, C, D | Intermediate depths showing hydrostatic pressure increase |
| E | Maximum pressure at the tank bottom (depth = H) |
| F, G | Governing rule: linear increase with depth, slope = γ |

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a U-Tube Manometer

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Pipe / Tank at Unknown Pressure P1] --> L1[Left Limb - Fluid rho1]
    L1 --> M1[Manometric Fluid Interface Left]
    M1 --> M2[Manometric Fluid - Mercury rho m]
    M2 --> M3[Manometric Fluid Interface Right]
    M3 --> L2[Right Limb - Open to Atmosphere]
    L2 --> P2[Atmospheric Pressure P atm]
    H[Difference in Manometric Heights h] --> EQ[Manometer Equation P1 = Patm + rho m g h - rho1 g h1 + rho1 g h2]
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| P1 (Pipe / Tank) | Source of unknown pressure |
| L1, L2 (Limbs) | Contain working fluid above the manometric fluid |
| M1, M2, M3 (Manometric Zone) | Heavy fluid (mercury) that provides the height-difference measurement |
| H (Height difference) | The measured variable — the entire instrument's output |
| EQ (Equation Block) | Translates height into pressure using the manometer equation |

## 4.3 Differential Manometer — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Point 1 at Pressure P1] --> B[Fluid of Density rho1 in Left Connecting Tube]
    B --> C[Manometric Fluid Interface - Left Side]
    C --> D[Manometric Fluid - Density rho m]
    D --> E[Manometric Fluid Interface - Right Side]
    E --> F[Fluid of Density rho2 in Right Connecting Tube]
    F --> G[Point 2 at Pressure P2]
    H[Traced Manometer Equation] --> I[P1 - P2 = rho m g h - rho1 g h1 - rho2 g h2]
```

**Step-by-step tracing rule** (encoded in the diagram):
1. Begin at Point 1 with pressure $P_1$.
2. Go **down** through fluid 1: **add** $\rho_1 g h_1$.
3. Cross to the other limb through manometric fluid: **add** $\rho_m g h$ if going down, **subtract** if going up.
4. Go **up** through fluid 2: **subtract** $\rho_2 g h_2$.
5. Arrive at $P_2$ on the other side.

> [!NOTE]
> The sign convention in step 3 is the single most frequent source of mark loss in KTU manometer problems. KTU examiners specifically watch for the **direction of traversal** through the manometric fluid.

## 4.4 Dead-Weight Pressure Tester — Schematic Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    W[Known Weights W] --> PI[Piston of Known Area A]
    PI --> PO[Generated Pressure P = W divide by A]
    PO --> TT[Transmitted Through Oil to Test Gauge]
    TT --> TG[Pressure Gauge Under Test]
    TG --> READ[Gauge Reading Compared with P calculated]
    READ --> CAL[Calibration Curve or Adjustment]
```

This block architecture shows how a dead-weight tester — purely a static-fluid device — becomes the **primary standard** for calibrating industrial pressure gauges.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Pascal's Law. Give any one practical application of it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Pascal's Law:** The pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surfaces of the containing vessel.

**Application:** The **hydraulic lift / hydraulic jack / hydraulic brake system**. A small force applied on a piston of small area produces a large force on a piston of large area, with the pressure $P = F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$ being the same in both pistons.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define (i) Gauge pressure, (ii) Absolute pressure, and (iii) Vacuum pressure. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Gauge Pressure:** The pressure measured relative to atmospheric pressure. $P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{abs}} - P_{\text{atm}}$.
- **(ii) Absolute Pressure:** The pressure measured relative to a perfect vacuum (zero pressure). $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} + P_{\text{gauge}}$.
- **(iii) Vacuum Pressure:** The amount by which the absolute pressure falls below atmospheric pressure. $P_{\text{vacuum}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$; valid only when $P_{\text{abs}} < P_{\text{atm}}$.

**Relationship:** $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} + P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vacuum}}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the hydrostatic pressure equation $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g$ for a static incompressible fluid, starting from a force balance on a fluid element. (7 marks)

**(b)** A differential U-tube manometer is connected across two points A and B in a horizontal water pipeline. The manometric fluid is mercury. The mercury levels in the two limbs differ by 80 mm, and water fills the connecting tubes above the mercury. The centerline of the pipeline is at the same elevation as the lower mercury interface. Calculate the pressure difference between points A and B in kPa. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a): Derivation of the Hydrostatic Equation

**Step 1: Set up the fluid element.** Consider a small rectangular fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ at rest inside the static fluid. Let the element be located such that its bottom-left-front corner is at point $(x, y, z)$.

**Step 2: Identify pressure forces on each face.**

- On the face at $x$ (with outward normal in $-x$ direction): pressure $P$ acts inward (in $+x$ direction) with force $P \cdot dy \, dz$.
- On the face at $x + dx$ (outward normal in $+x$ direction): pressure $P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} dx$ acts inward (in $-x$ direction) with force $\left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} dx\right) dy \, dz$.

Similarly for the y and z faces.

**Step 3: Apply equilibrium in the x-direction.**

$$\sum F_x = 0$$

$$P \, dy \, dz - \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} dx\right) dy \, dz = 0$$

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} dx \, dy \, dz = 0$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = 0}$$

**Step 4: Apply equilibrium in the y-direction.** By identical reasoning:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial P}{\partial y} = 0}$$

**Step 5: Apply equilibrium in the z-direction.** The body force is gravity acting downward (in $-z$ direction) with magnitude $\rho g \, dx \, dy \, dz$.

$$\sum F_z = 0$$

$$P \, dx \, dy - \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} dz\right) dx \, dy - \rho g \, dx \, dy \, dz = 0$$

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} dz \, dx \, dy - \rho g \, dx \, dy \, dz = 0$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g}$$

The negative sign indicates that pressure **decreases** with increasing elevation.

**Step 6: Integrate for incompressible fluid (constant $\rho$, constant $g$).**

$$\int dP = -\rho g \int dz \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = -\rho g z + C$$

**Step 7: Apply boundary condition.** At $z = 0$ (free surface), $P = P_0$. So $C = P_0$, and:

$$\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h \quad \text{where } h = -z \text{ is depth below the free surface}}$$

**This is the hydrostatic pressure equation** — the fundamental result of fluid statics.

---

#### Part (b): Differential Manometer Calculation

**Given:**

- Manometric fluid: mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
- Working fluid above mercury: water ($\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
- Mercury level difference: $h = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$
- The centerline of the pipeline coincides with the lower mercury interface.

**Step 1: Identify the configuration.** Assume point A is on the side where the mercury is pushed **down** (higher pressure), and point B is on the side where the mercury is pushed **up** (lower pressure). The mercury level on B's side is therefore 80 mm above that on A's side.

**Step 2: Trace the pressure from A to B using the manometer equation.**

Starting at A with pressure $P_A$, going from the pipe centerline (which is at the lower mercury interface on A's side) **up** through the mercury column of 80 mm to reach the upper mercury interface on B's side:

$$P_{\text{upper Hg}} = P_A - \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h$$

(The pressure decreases as we go up through mercury.)

**Step 3: Continue from the upper mercury interface, going down through water in B's connecting tube back to the pipe centerline at B.** Since the centerline is at the level of the lower mercury interface, the water column in B's tube has length 80 mm.

$$P_B = P_{\text{upper Hg}} + \rho_w \, g \, h$$

(Pressure increases as we go down through water.)

**Step 4: Combine the two equations.**

$$P_B = P_A - \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h + \rho_w \, g \, h$$

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h - \rho_w \, g \, h = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \, g \, h$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values.**

$$P_A - P_B = (13600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.080$$

$$P_A - P_B = 12600 \times 9.81 \times 0.080$$

$$P_A - P_B = 12600 \times 0.7848 = 9888.5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B \approx 9.89 \text{ kPa}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** What is a dead-weight pressure tester? Explain its working principle. Why is it considered a primary standard for pressure calibration? (7 marks)

**(b)** An inclined manometer has its tube inclined at 30° to the horizontal. The manometric fluid is oil of specific gravity 0.85. The reading along the inclined tube is 250 mm. The fluid in the pipeline is air. Calculate the pressure being measured in the pipe, in Pa. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a): Dead-Weight Pressure Tester

**Definition:** A dead-weight pressure tester (also called a dead-weight gauge or piston gauge) is a primary-standard instrument used to generate a precisely known pressure by balancing the force produced by known calibrated weights against the area of a precision piston.

**Working Principle:**

1. The instrument consists of a **vertically mounted piston** of accurately known effective cross-sectional area $A$, which fits snugly into a close-fitting cylinder.
2. The piston rests on a column of oil (or other hydraulic fluid) that is connected to the **gauge under test**.
3. Known standard weights $W$ (including the weight of the piston itself) are placed on a platform on top of the piston.
4. The piston floats in equilibrium when the **pressure generated by the oil** exactly supports the weight.
5. At equilibrium: $P = \dfrac{W}{A}$, where $W$ is the total downward force (weights + piston weight) and $A$ is the piston area.

**Why it is a primary standard:**

- The pressure $P$ is derived **directly from base SI quantities** — mass (the calibrated weights), length (the piston diameter), and time (implicit in $g$). No other pressure instrument is calibrated against it.
- Its accuracy depends only on the precision of the weights, the piston area, and the local value of $g$ — all of which can be measured to very high precision (often $\pm 0.01\%$ or better).
- It is **traceable to national standards** (like NPL, NIST) through mass and length calibrations.
- It can be used to calibrate any other pressure instrument — Bourdon gauges, manometers, pressure transducers — by connecting the gauge to the tester and comparing the gauge reading with the calculated $P = W/A$.

**Typical range:** 5 kPa to 200 MPa, depending on the piston-cylinder combination.

---

#### Part (b): Inclined Manometer Calculation

**Given:**

- Inclination angle: $\theta = 30°$
- Manometric fluid: oil, $SG = 0.85$, so $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Length reading along inclined tube: $l = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.250 \text{ m}$
- Pipe fluid: air (density $\approx 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, which is negligible compared to oil)

**Step 1: Convert the inclined reading to equivalent vertical head.**

$$h = l \sin\theta = 0.250 \times \sin(30°) = 0.250 \times 0.5 = 0.125 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Apply the manometer equation.** Since the pipe fluid is air (negligible density) and the manometer is open to atmosphere on the other side, the pressure in the pipe is:

$$P_{\text{pipe}} = P_{atm} + \rho_{\text{oil}} \, g \, h$$

**Step 3: Calculate the pressure due to the oil column.**

$$\rho_{\text{oil}} \, g \, h = 850 \times 9.81 \times 0.125$$

$$= 850 \times 1.22625 = 1042.3 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4: Final answer.**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{pipe,gauge}} \approx 1042.3 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.04 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$

Or, in absolute terms: $P_{\text{pipe,abs}} \approx 101.325 + 1.042 \approx 102.37 \text{ kPa (absolute)}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Sign in hydrostatic equation:** Writing $\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = +\rho g$ instead of $-\rho g$ — instant 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting to use vertical height:** In inclined manometer problems, students often substitute the inclined length $l$ directly into $P = \rho g h$ instead of computing $h = l \sin\theta$ first. This inflates the answer by a factor of $1/\sin\theta$ — a 1- to 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong sign in manometer equation:** When tracing through the manometric fluid, students often forget whether to add or subtract. The rule is: **going down → add, going up → subtract.**
> 4. **Ignoring manometer-fluid vs. working-fluid densities:** Forgetting the $(\rho_m - \rho_f)$ factor when the working fluid is not air leads to errors of several percent.
> 5. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing mmHg, cmH₂O, kPa, and Pa without conversion. KTU expects answers in SI units (Pa or kPa) unless otherwise stated.
> 6. **Density of water:** Use $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ unless the problem states a different temperature. For mercury, use $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ unless specified.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hydrostatic Equation:** $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g$ — pressure decreases with elevation. *Sign is critical.*
- **Master Formula:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ — the most frequently used relation in this module.
- **Specific Weight:** $\gamma = \rho g$ — the slope of the pressure-vs-depth graph.
- **Gauge vs Absolute:** Always clarify which reference is used. *Default in KTU problems is often gauge pressure unless specified.*
- **Manometer Equation Logic:** Trace pressure step-by-step. **Going down → add $\rho g h$.** **Going up → subtract $\rho g h$.**
- **Differential Manometer:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ for the standard configuration with water as the working fluid.
- **Inclined Manometer:** $h = l \sin\theta$ — converts inclined length to equivalent vertical head.
- **Pascal's Law:** $P_1 = P_2$ throughout an enclosed static fluid. Foundation of hydraulics.
- **Archimedes' Principle:** $F_b = \rho_f g V_{\text{displaced}}$ — for level sensors, hydrometers, and buoyancy-based instruments.
- **Dead-Weight Tester:** Primary standard. $P = W/A$. Traceable to base SI units.
- **Compressible Barometric Formula:** $P(z) = P_0 \exp(-gz/RT)$ for isothermal atmosphere.
- **Centre of Buoyancy:** The point at which the buoyant force effectively acts — the centroid of the displaced fluid volume.
- **Specific Gravity:** $SG = \rho_{\text{fluid}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$ — dimensionless. Use this in problems where density is given as SG.
- **Manometric Fluid Selection:** Mercury for high pressures, oil/alcohol for low pressures, water for moderate. *Heavier fluid = smaller deflection; lighter fluid = higher sensitivity.*
- **Pressure Units to Memorize:** 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 14.696 psi = 10.332 mH₂O.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, **always** show your manometer-tracing steps explicitly — even if the problem seems straightforward. KTU awards step marks (typically 1 mark per correct step), and the manometer-tracing is worth 3 to 4 marks on its own. A correct numerical answer without shown work will receive only partial credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Equations of Fluid Flow: Continuity, Momentum and Energy Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Equations of Fluid Flow: Continuity, Momentum and Energy Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Governing Equations of Fluid Flow

The **basic equations of fluid flow** form the mathematical backbone of mechanical instrumentation. Every flowmeter, pressure sensor, and velocity-measuring device ultimately derives its working principle from one or more of these three foundational conservation laws. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups them under **Module 4** because every flow-measurement instrument (orifice, Venturi, pitot tube, turbine meter, etc.) requires these equations for calibration, accuracy analysis, and discharge computation.

The three governing equations are:

| # | Equation | Conservation Law | Mathematical Statement |
|---|----------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Continuity Equation** | Conservation of Mass | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ |
| 2 | **Momentum Equation** | Newton's Second Law | $\sum \vec{F} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \vec{V} \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ |
| 3 | **Energy Equation** | First Law of Thermodynamics | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} e \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} e \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For mechanical instrumentation, the **Integral forms** of these equations (not the differential forms) are of primary interest, because flow-measuring devices deal with finite **Control Volumes (CV)** bounded by inlet and outlet **Control Surfaces (CS)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Three Laws as a River Story

Imagine a **river flowing through a stretch of land** (this stretch is our *Control Volume*):

- **Continuity Equation = Mass Balance:** Think of it as a bank ledger. Whatever mass of water **enters** the stretch in one second must either **leave** in the same second or **accumulate** (fill up the banks) in that second. *Mass is never created or destroyed — it is just booked.*

- **Momentum Equation = Newton's Law for the River:** The water rushing out the far end has **momentum** ($m\vec{V}$). The forces acting on the control volume — pressure forces at inlet/outlet, weight of the water column, and shear from the riverbed — must equal the **net rate of change of momentum** plus the momentum flux leaving. *For every action on the river, there is an equal and opposite reaction on the momentum balance.*

- **Energy Equation = The River's Energy Bank Account:** Water entering carries **kinetic energy, potential energy, flow energy, and internal energy**. Some energy is added (pump = $\dot{W}$) or lost (friction = heat loss $\dot{Q}$). Whatever is added minus whatever is lost must equal the change in stored energy plus the energy leaving with the outflow. *Energy is conserved; it just changes form.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Recap:** Mass says "what comes in must go out or stay." Momentum says "force equals the push of moving mass." Energy says "the total energy book (heat, work, kinetic, potential, internal) must balance."

---

## 1.3 System vs. Control Volume — The Two Viewpoints

Before writing any equation, the KTU examiner expects the student to be crystal clear on the **frame of reference**:

- **System (Lagrangian View):** A *fixed quantity of mass* is followed (like tracking a particular water molecule). Equations are simple, but the boundary deforms.
- **Control Volume (Eulerian View):** A *fixed region in space* is studied. Mass, momentum, and energy flow *through* its stationary boundary. This is the **operational view for instruments**, because a flowmeter sits at a fixed location in a pipe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)** is the mathematical bridge that converts a system-based conservation law into a control-volume (CV) form. For any extensive property $N$ (mass, momentum, energy):

$$\frac{DN_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \eta \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \eta \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

where $\eta = N/m$ is the **intensive (per-unit-mass)** form of the property, and $\hat{n}$ is the outward unit normal to the control surface.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants (For Quick Reference)

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
|--------|----------|---------|
| $\rho$ | Fluid density | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| $\vec{V}$ | Velocity vector | $\text{m/s}$ |
| $p$ | Static pressure | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ |
| $z$ | Elevation head | $\text{m}$ |
| $V$ | Mean velocity | $\text{m/s}$ |
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area | $\text{m}^2$ |
| $Q$ | Volumetric flow rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| $\dot{m}$ | Mass flow rate | $\text{kg/s}$ |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Integral Conservation Laws — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

**Operational Logic (Why & How):**

1. **Select a Control Volume** fixed in space — typically the inside of a pipe section between two instrument flanges.
2. **Apply the principle:** The mass *inside* the CV can only change if there is a *net mass flux* across its control surface.
3. **Assume steady flow** ($\partial/\partial t = 0$) — this is the standard assumption in most flow-metering problems in KTU papers.
4. **Assume uniform velocity profiles** at inlet (1) and outlet (2) — the *one-dimensional flow* approximation.
5. **Result:** Mass entering per second = Mass leaving per second.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Instrumentation:** The continuity equation is the *first equation* written when solving any orifice, Venturi, or nozzle problem. Without it, you cannot relate the velocity at the throat to the velocity at the inlet.

---

### 2.1.2 Momentum Equation (Newton's Second Law for a Fluid)

**Operational Logic (Why & How):**

1. **Start with Newton's second law:** $\sum \vec{F} = m\vec{a} = \dfrac{D(m\vec{V})_{sys}}{Dt}$
2. **Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem** to convert the system derivative into CV form.
3. **Recognize the two parts of momentum change:**
   - *Local acceleration* — momentum stored inside the CV changing with time (zero for steady flow).
   - *Convective acceleration* — momentum carried in and out by the flowing fluid.
4. **Identify all forces** acting on the CV: pressure forces on inlet/outlet, body force (gravity), and surface forces (shear, normal stresses from pipe walls).
5. **Apply the momentum flux correction factor $\beta$** when the velocity profile is non-uniform (e.g., turbulent flow in a pipe).

> [!NOTE]
> **Instrumentation Relevance:** The momentum equation is the *governing equation for the force balance on the fluid*, which in turn determines the **force on a turbine blade, the torque on a vane anemometer, or the deflection of a pitot-static tube.**

---

### 2.1.3 Energy Equation (First Law of Thermodynamics for a Flowing Fluid)

**Operational Logic (Why & How):**

1. **Start with the First Law:** $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dfrac{dE_{sys}}{dt}$
2. **Expand $E$** to include kinetic, potential, internal, and flow (flow work = $pV$) energy.
3. **Apply RTT** to move from system to control-volume form.
4. **Assume adiabatic flow** ($\dot{Q} = 0$) and **no shaft work** ($\dot{W} = 0$) for an ideal Venturi/orifice/nozzle.
5. **Integrate along a streamline** for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow to obtain **Bernoulli's Equation.**
6. **Introduce a head-loss term $h_L$** to handle viscous (real) flow — this is what is used in practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Bernoulli's equation is the *master key* for differential pressure flowmeters (orifice, Venturi, flow nozzle, pitot tube). The pressure difference measured by the manometer is converted into a velocity and then into a flow rate using this equation.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is the single most important reference for solving Module 4 numerical problems in the KTU 2024 University Exam. Memorize the boxed versions of each equation.**

| # | Equation Name | Integral / Simplified Form | Key Symbols |
|---|---------------|----------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Continuity (General, Unsteady)** | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$ | $\rho, \vec{V}, \hat{n}$ |
| 2 | **Continuity (Steady, 1-D)** | $\boxed{\dot{m} = \rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2}$ | $\dot{m}$: mass flow rate |
| 3 | **Continuity (Incompressible, 1-D)** | $\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q}$ | $Q$: volumetric flow rate |
| 4 | **Momentum Equation (Integral)** | $\sum \vec{F} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \vec{V}\rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \vec{V}\rho(\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ | $\sum \vec{F}$: surface + body forces |
| 5 | **Momentum Eq. (Steady, 1-D, with $\beta$)** | $\boxed{\sum F_x = \dot{m}(\beta_2 V_2 - \beta_1 V_1)}$ | $\beta$: momentum correction factor |
| 6 | **Energy Equation (Integral)** | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} e \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \left(e + \dfrac{p}{\rho}\right)\rho(\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n}) dA$ | $e$: total energy per unit mass |
| 7 | **Bernoulli's Equation (Inviscid, Steady, Incomp.)** | $\boxed{\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2}$ | Heads in metres of fluid |
| 8 | **Bernoulli (Energy per unit mass form)** | $\boxed{\dfrac{p_1}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2}$ | SI: J/kg |
| 9 | **Bernoulli (with friction loss)** | $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | $h_L$: head loss |
| 10 | **Velocity from Differential Head** | $\boxed{V_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g\,\Delta h}{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}}}$ | For Venturi/orifice theory |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Equation | Industrial Application | Specific Instrument |
|----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Continuity** | Inventory of fluids in a tank; mass balance in chemical reactors | Magnetic flowmeter calibration, Coriolis flowmeter |
| **Momentum** | Force on pipe bends, jet impingement, thrust on rocket nozzles | Vane anemometer, target flowmeter, turbine flowmeter torque |
| **Energy** | Conversion of pressure to velocity for flow measurement | Orifice plate, Venturi meter, Flow nozzle, Pitot tube |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Wisdom:** Almost every flow-measuring device in the KTU syllabus **measures a pressure difference** and **uses the energy equation** to convert that $\Delta p$ into a flow rate. That is why the energy equation is the *most-tested* of the three in the KTU University Exam.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Convention:** All derivations below are presented at the *rigorous level* expected for a 14-mark KTU Part-B question. Each algebraic step is explicitly justified in plain English so the student can reproduce the proof in the exam hall.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation (Integral Form)

### 3.1.1 Starting Principle (Law of Conservation of Mass)

> Mass can be neither created nor destroyed in a non-nuclear process. The total mass inside any chosen control volume can change **only** because of net mass flux through its boundary.

Let $m_{CV}$ be the mass contained in the control volume at time $t$. Then:

$$\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV$$

The rate at which mass **leaves** the CV across a small element $dA$ of the control surface is:

$$d\dot{m}_{out} = \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

where $\hat{n}$ is the **outward-pointing unit normal** to the control surface. The dot product $\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n}$ is positive when the fluid is leaving and negative when it is entering.

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Statement

The conservation of mass in integral form is therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0}$$

**Textual Justification of Each Term:**

- The first term (the *unsteady term*) represents the **rate of accumulation of mass inside the CV**.
- The second term (the *flux term*) represents the **net rate of mass efflux** through the control surface.
- Their sum is zero because mass is conserved.

### 3.1.3 Simplification for Steady, Incompressible, One-Dimensional Flow

**Step 1 — Steady flow** means the density field at any fixed point does not change with time:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV = 0$$

**Step 2 — One-dimensional flow** at inlet (section 1) and outlet (section 2) means the velocity is uniform across each cross-section and perpendicular to it. The flux integral splits into two parts:

$$\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \dot{m}_{out} - \dot{m}_{in} = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 - \rho_1 A_1 V_1$$

**Step 3 — Substituting** these into the general equation:

$$0 + \rho_2 A_2 V_2 - \rho_1 A_1 V_1 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Final result** (the working form for instrumentation):

$$\boxed{\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m} \quad \text{(steady flow)}}$$

**Step 5 — If the fluid is additionally incompressible** ($\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$):

$$\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \quad \text{(steady, incompressible flow)}}$$

where $Q$ is the **volumetric flow rate** in m³/s.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for flowmeters:** The continuity equation allows the engineer to *replace* an unknown velocity with a known one. For example, in a Venturi meter, if we know $V_1$ at the inlet (large area $A_1$), we can immediately find $V_2$ at the throat (small area $A_2$) by $V_2 = (A_1/A_2)V_1$. This velocity is then substituted into Bernoulli's equation to predict the pressure drop, which the instrument then measures.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Momentum Equation (Integral Form)

### 3.2.1 Starting Principle (Newton's Second Law)

> The sum of all external forces acting on a system equals the time rate of change of its linear momentum.

For a system of fixed identity:

$$\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = \frac{D}{Dt}\left(m\vec{V}\right)_{sys}$$

### 3.2.2 Application of the Reynolds Transport Theorem

Using RTT with the intensive property $\eta = \vec{V}$ (momentum per unit mass = velocity):

$$\frac{D}{Dt}\left(m\vec{V}\right)_{sys} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \vec{V} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \vec{V} \rho (\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

### 3.2.3 The General Integral Momentum Equation

Equating forces to the rate of change of momentum:

$$\boxed{\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \vec{V} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \vec{V} \rho (\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n}) \, dA}$$

**Forces on the Right-Hand Side of Newton's Law:**

The sum $\sum \vec{F}_{ext}$ on a control volume typically includes:

- **Pressure forces** on the inlet and outlet sections: $p_1 A_1$ and $p_2 A_2$
- **Body force** due to gravity: $\rho g V_{CV}$ (acting downward)
- **Shear forces** on the walls of the CV (relevant for viscous flows)
- **External support forces** (relevant for finding the force on a pipe bend, for instance)

### 3.2.4 Simplification for Steady, Incompressible, 1-D Flow

**Step 1 — Steady flow** eliminates the local (unsteady) term:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \vec{V} \rho \, dV = 0$$

**Step 2 — One-dimensional flow** at the inlet and outlet means uniform velocity and density across each section. The flux integral becomes:

$$\int_{CS} \vec{V} \rho (\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \dot{m}_2 \vec{V}_2 - \dot{m}_1 \vec{V}_1$$

**Step 3 — Net force** is now equal to net momentum efflux:

$$\boxed{\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = \dot{m}(\vec{V}_2 - \vec{V}_1)}$$

### 3.2.5 Inclusion of the Momentum Correction Factor $\beta$

In real (especially turbulent) pipe flow, the velocity profile is **not flat** — it is roughly parabolic or follows a power law. The actual momentum flux is therefore *higher* than the uniform-velocity approximation predicts. The correction factor is defined as:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{A}\int_A \left(\frac{V}{V_{avg}}\right)^2 dA$$

Values commonly used in KTU problems:

- $\beta = 1$ for uniform (plug) flow
- $\beta = 4/3$ for fully developed laminar flow in a circular pipe
- $\beta \approx 1.02 \text{ to } 1.05$ for fully developed turbulent flow

The corrected steady, 1-D momentum equation becomes:

$$\boxed{\sum \vec{F}_{x} = \dot{m}\left(\beta_2 V_{2x} - \beta_1 V_{1x}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Instrumentation Application:** The momentum equation is used to find the **force exerted by a fluid jet on a stationary or moving vane** (e.g., in a vane-type anemometer or a Pelton wheel turbine). It is also used to find the **anchoring force required on a reducing bend** in a pipeline.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Energy Equation (Integral Form)

### 3.3.1 Starting Principle (First Law of Thermodynamics)

> The net rate of energy addition (heat in minus work out) equals the rate of change of total energy of the system.

For a system:

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \frac{dE_{sys}}{dt}$$

where $E_{sys}$ is the total energy (kinetic + potential + internal).

### 3.3.2 Energy Per Unit Mass

For a flowing fluid, the total energy per unit mass is the sum of:

$$e = \underbrace{\frac{V^2}{2}}_{\text{kinetic}} + \underbrace{gz}_{\text{potential}} + \underbrace{u}_{\text{internal}}$$

In addition, the fluid crossing a control surface carries **flow work** $p/\rho$ per unit mass (this is the work done by pressure forces to push fluid into or out of the CV).

The "total" energy per unit mass that is advected through the control surface is therefore:

$$e_{total} = e + \frac{p}{\rho} = \frac{V^2}{2} + gz + u + \frac{p}{\rho}$$

### 3.3.3 Application of Reynolds Transport Theorem

$$\frac{dE_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} e \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} e \rho (\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

The flow-work term $p/\rho$ appears in the flux integral because the pressure does work as fluid enters or leaves:

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} e \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \left(e + \frac{p}{\rho}\right) \rho (\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

### 3.3.4 The General Integral Energy Equation

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} e \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \left(e + \frac{p}{\rho}\right) \rho (\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n}) \, dA}$$

**Textual Justification of Each Term:**

- $\dot{Q}$: Rate of heat transfer *into* the CV (positive when heat enters)
- $\dot{W}$: Rate of work done *by* the CV (positive when work leaves the CV, e.g., a turbine extracting energy)
- First term on RHS: Rate of *accumulation* of energy inside the CV
- Second term on RHS: Net rate of *efflux* of total energy (including flow work) through the control surface

### 3.3.5 Simplification for Steady, Incompressible, 1-D, Adiabatic, Inviscid Flow

**Step 1 — Steady flow:** The accumulation term vanishes:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} e \rho \, dV = 0$$

**Step 2 — No shaft work and adiabatic:** $\dot{W} = 0, \dot{Q} = 0$

**Step 3 — One-dimensional flow at sections 1 and 2:**

The flux integral becomes:

$$\int_{CS} \left(\frac{V^2}{2} + gz + u + \frac{p}{\rho}\right)\rho (\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \dot{m}\left[\frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2 + u_2 + \frac{p_2}{\rho}\right] - \dot{m}\left[\frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 + u_1 + \frac{p_1}{\rho}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Substitution** back into the energy equation:

$$0 = \dot{m}\left[\left(\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}\right) + g(z_2 - z_1) + (u_2 - u_1) + \left(\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Incompressible and inviscid (no friction):** Internal energy is constant ($u_2 = u_1$):

$$\frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 + \frac{p_1}{\rho} = \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2 + \frac{p_2}{\rho}$$

**Step 6 — Final result — Bernoulli's Equation (Energy per unit mass form):**

$$\boxed{\frac{p_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2 \quad \text{[J/kg]}}$$

**Step 7 — Dividing by $g$ gives the "head" form** (preferred in KTU problems, because manometers read in metres of fluid):

$$\boxed{\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 \quad \text{[m]}}$$

**Step 8 — Real (viscous) flow** requires the addition of a head loss term $h_L$ and a kinetic-energy correction factor $\alpha$:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \alpha_1 \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \alpha_2 \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

### 3.3.6 Derivation of the Venturi Velocity Formula

This is the **most-asked derivation** in the KTU Mechanical Instrumentation paper.

**Step 1 — Apply the horizontal Bernoulli equation** between section 1 (inlet, diameter $D_1$) and section 2 (throat, diameter $D_2$):

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the continuity equation** between the same two sections:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $V_1$ in the Bernoulli equation:

$$\frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_2$** (the theoretical throat velocity):

$$\boxed{V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2(p_1 - p_2)/\rho}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2g\,\Delta h}{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}}}$$

where $\Delta h = (p_1 - p_2)/(\rho g)$ is the differential pressure head in metres of fluid.

**Step 5 — Volumetric flow rate** is then:

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-(D_2/D_1)^4}} \sqrt{2g\,\Delta h}}$$

**Step 6 — Actual flow rate** includes a discharge coefficient $C_d$ to account for viscous losses and vena-contracta effects:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-(D_2/D_1)^4}} \sqrt{2g\,\Delta h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $C_d$ is needed:** Real fluids are viscous and the jet may not perfectly fill the throat cross-section. The coefficient $C_d$ corrects the ideal (theoretical) value to the actual value. Typical ranges: Venturi $C_d \approx 0.95$–$0.99$, Orifice $C_d \approx 0.60$–$0.65$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — The Three Equations at a Glance

| Feature | Continuity | Momentum | Energy |
|---------|------------|----------|--------|
| **Origin** | Mass is conserved | Newton's 2nd Law | First Law of Thermodynamics |
| **Vector / Scalar** | Scalar | Vector | Scalar |
| **Primary Unknown** | Velocity (via area change) | Force on a body | Pressure–velocity relationship |
| **Forces Considered** | None (kinematic) | Pressure, gravity, shear | Heat, work, all energy forms |
| **Steady 1-D Form** | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | $\sum F = \dot{m}(V_2 - V_1)$ | $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ |
| **Key Instrument** | Magnetic flowmeter | Turbine, target meter | Orifice, Venturi, pitot tube |
| **Assumptions Made** | Steady, 1-D, well-defined CS | Steady, 1-D, $\beta = 1$ or known | Steady, incomp., inviscid, adiabatic, no shaft work |

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Derivation Logic of Bernoulli's Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1: Apply Continuity between Inlet 1 and Throat 2"]:::step --> S2["STEP 2: Express V1 in terms of V2 using A1 V1 = A2 V2"]:::step
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Apply Bernoulli (energy per unit mass) between 1 and 2"]:::step
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Substitute V1 into the Bernoulli equation"]:::step
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Solve for V2 at the throat"]:::step
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Compute volumetric flow Qth = A2 times V2"]:::step
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Multiply by discharge coefficient Cd to get Qact"]:::step

    S7 --> R["FINAL: Qact = Cd times A2 by square root of 1 minus D2 by D1 to the power 4 times square root of 2g times delta h"]:::result

    classDef step fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#78350f,stroke-width:1px
    classDef result fill:#22c55e,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Identification on a Control Volume (Momentum Equation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CV["CONTROL VOLUME: Pipe Section between Sections 1 and 2"]:::cv --> F1["Pressure Force F1 = p1 A1 acting to the RIGHT at Inlet"]:::force
    CV --> F2["Pressure Force F2 = p2 A2 acting to the LEFT at Outlet"]:::force
    CV --> F3["Body Force W = rho g times Vcv acting DOWNWARD"]:::force
    CV --> F4["Wall Shear Force Fs acting OPPOSITE to flow"]:::force
    CV --> F5["External Anchor Force R required for equilibrium"]:::force

    F1 & F2 & F3 & F4 & F5 --> SUM["Net Force Sigma Fx = F1 minus F2 minus Fs minus Rx"]:::sum
    SUM --> MFLUX["Momentum Flux Efflux = m dot times beta2 V2 minus beta1 V1"]:::flux
    MFLUX --> EQ["EQUATION: Sigma Fx = m dot times beta2 V2 minus beta1 V1"]:::eq

    classDef cv fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef force fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,color:#7c2d12,stroke-width:1px
    classDef sum fill:#fef08a,stroke:#a16207,color:#713f12,stroke-width:1px
    classDef flux fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e,stroke-width:1px
    classDef eq fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Physical Schematic Description — A Venturi Meter (Verbal Walk-Through)

A real Venturi meter is the most direct physical embodiment of all three equations working together. Although we cannot draw it in pure text, here is the architecture for the student's imagination:

- **Section 1 (Inlet / Upstream Tap):** Large diameter $D_1$, area $A_1$. Pressure tap P₁ is located here.
- **Converging Section:** Smooth, tapered contraction (cone angle typically 21°). Velocity increases, pressure decreases.
- **Section 2 (Throat):** Smallest diameter $D_2$, area $A_2$. Pressure tap P₂ is located here.
- **Diverging Section:** Gradual expansion (cone angle typically 5°–7°) that recovers most of the pressure.
- **Section 3 (Outlet):** Same diameter $D_1$ as inlet, with nearly full pressure recovery.

The **manometer** connected between P₁ and P₂ reads the **differential pressure $\Delta h$**, which is then plugged into the derived formula (Section 3.3.6) to compute the flow rate. The three governing equations are applied in sequence:

1. **Continuity** (between 1 and 2) → relates $V_2$ to $V_1$.
2. **Bernoulli (Energy equation)** (between 1 and 2) → relates $(p_1 - p_2)$ to $(V_2^2 - V_1^2)$.
3. **Discharge coefficient $C_d$** → corrects theoretical to actual.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module-4 Takeaway:** Every differential-pressure flowmeter in the syllabus — orifice, Venturi, flow nozzle, pitot tube — is a *physical embodiment* of the continuity + energy equations combined. The momentum equation comes into play when we ask "what force does the fluid exert on the meter body?"
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**

> **State the continuity equation for a steady, one-dimensional, incompressible flow. Mention the physical law on which it is based.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The continuity equation for **steady, one-dimensional, incompressible flow** is:
>
> $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$$
>
> where $A_1, A_2$ are the cross-sectional areas (m²) at sections 1 and 2, $V_1, V_2$ are the corresponding mean velocities (m/s), and $Q$ is the **volumetric flow rate** (m³/s).
>
> **Physical Law:** This equation is based on the **law of conservation of mass**, which states that mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a non-nuclear process.
