topics.title: Measurement of Surface Roughness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Surface Roughness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surface Roughness** is the measure of the finely spaced, microscopic irregularities present on a manufactured surface. It is formally defined as the deviations of a real surface from its ideal (nominal) form in the direction normal to the surface, evaluated over a specified sampling length.

Mathematically, surface texture is described as a composite of four superimposed components defined by **ISO 4287:1997 / ISO 21920**:

- **Form (Primary Profile):** Macroscopic deviations governed by the manufacturing machine (e.g., straightness, flatness, roundness errors).
- **Waviness:** Repetitive, mid-frequency undulations caused by vibration, tool chatter, or machine deflection.
- **Roughness:** High-frequency, closely spaced micro-irregularities produced directly by the cutting tool, grit, or forming process.
- **Lay:** The predominant direction of the surface pattern (e.g., circular, longitudinal, cross-hatched).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Surface roughness is the *most* frequently assessed topic from Module 4. A typical KTU question will ask you to (a) define the standard roughness parameters, and (b) explain the operating principle of the **stylus-type profilometer** with a neat block diagram. Always draw a clean block diagram — marks are awarded for it.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mountain Range" Model

Imagine you are flying over a landscape and the ground beneath you represents a machined metal surface.

- **Form** is the tilt of the entire landmass (e.g., one side of Kerala sloping toward the sea) — the broad, long-wavelength shape.
- **Waviness** is the rolling hills — undulations you can see from a few thousand feet up.
- **Roughness** is the texture of the soil, pebbles, and grass covering each hill — visible only when you put your nose to the ground.
- **Lay** is the prevailing direction the wind has blown the grass (the directional pattern of the tool marks).

When a quality inspector places a **stylus** (a fine diamond needle) on a surface and drags it across, the stylus rides over the *roughness* hills and valleys, ignoring the waviness and form by using a **low-pass electronic filter** called a **cutoff filter**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Surface Roughness Matters in Engineering:**
> - **Tribology:** Determines friction, wear rate, and lubricant retention.
> - **Fatigue Life:** Rougher surfaces have stress-concentration micro-notches → reduced fatigue strength.
> - **Sealing:** Hydraulic/pneumatic seals require controlled roughness to prevent leakage.
> - **Electrical Contact:** Affects contact resistance in switches and slip-rings.
> - **Aesthetics/Feel:** Tactile perception of consumer products (phone bodies, dashboards).

### 1.3 Standard Roughness Parameters (ISO 4287)

The most important parameters in KTU examinations are:

| Parameter | Name | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Ra** | Arithmetic Mean Deviation | Average absolute deviation from the mean line over sampling length |
| **Rz** | Mean Peak-to-Valley Height | Sum of the five largest peak-to-valley distances averaged over the sampling length |
| **Rq (RMS)** | Root-Mean-Square Deviation | Quadratic mean of the ordinates — sensitive to extreme peaks |
| **Rt** | Total Roughness Height | Maximum peak-to-valley distance over the *entire* evaluation length |
| **Rsm** | Mean Spacing of Profile Irregularities | Average horizontal distance between successive peaks |

The single most universally quoted parameter is **Ra**, expressed in **micrometers (µm)** or **microinches (µin)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Surface Profile

Every real surface profile $y(x)$ can be expressed as a superposition:

$$y_{total}(x) = y_{form}(x) + y_{waviness}(x) + y_{roughness}(x)$$

A surface roughness measuring instrument must isolate $y_{roughness}$ from the other two components. This isolation is performed in the **spatial frequency domain** by a **cutoff filter**.

### 2.2 The Concept of the Cutoff Wavelength (λc)

A **cutoff filter** (analogous to a low-pass RC filter in electronics) removes wavelengths **longer** than $\lambda_c$ and passes wavelengths **shorter** than $\lambda_c$. The standard cutoff values are prescribed by **ISO 4288** based on the value of $R_a$:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 80% Rule (ISO 4288):** For a given $R_a$ range, the sampling length $L$ is chosen such that the cutoff $\lambda_c$ produces a stable $R_a$ value. If $R_a$ measured at $\lambda_{c1}$ is within 80%–120% of $R_a$ at $\lambda_{c2}$, the coarser cutoff is chosen. The evaluation length is then $L = 5 \times \lambda_c$ (5 sampling lengths).

| Roughness Range $R_a$ (µm) | Cutoff $\lambda_c$ (mm) | Evaluation Length (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| $0.006 < R_a \leq 0.02$ | 0.08 | 0.40 |
| $0.02 < R_a \leq 0.1$ | 0.25 | 1.25 |
| $0.1 < R_a \leq 2$ | 0.80 | 4.00 |
| $2 < R_a \leq 10$ | 2.50 | 12.50 |
| $10 < R_a \leq 80$ | 8.00 | 40.00 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If a question gives you the cutoff filter value, immediately identify which $R_a$ range is being measured. A common mistake is mixing up the evaluation length with the cutoff — remember, **evaluation length = 5 × cutoff**.

### 2.3 The Stylus Profilometer — Working Principle

The **stylus-type (contact-type) profilometer** (e.g., Talysurf, Perthometer, Surftest) is the workhorse of industrial surface metrology. Its working logic can be broken into six discrete steps:

1. **Traverse Drive:** A precision lead-screw moves the workpiece (or the stylus carriage) at a constant linear speed $v$ (typically 0.5 mm/s or 1 mm/s).
2. **Stylus Contact:** A diamond stylus of tip radius $r_{tip} = 2\,\mu m$ (for $R_a < 0.1\,\mu m$) or $r_{tip} = 10\,\mu m$ (for general use) is brought into contact with the surface under a controlled static force (typically $F_s = 0.75\,mN$ to $4\,mN$).
3. **Vertical Displacement:** As the stylus rides over the asperities, it deflects vertically. The vertical range is small — typically $\pm 100\,\mu m$ to $\pm 800\,\mu m$.
4. **Transduction:** A transducer (LVDT, piezoelectric, or capacitive) converts the vertical displacement into a proportional electrical signal.
5. **Filtering & Amplification:** The raw signal is passed through a **cutoff filter** (Gaussian or 2RC, 2-pole Butterworth) and amplified.
6. **Computation & Display:** A microcontroller computes $R_a$, $R_z$, $R_q$, etc., using digital integration algorithms, and displays the result on an LCD.

> [!WARNING]
> **Stylus Tip Radius Selection Pitfall:** A tip that is too large *rounds off* the true peaks (underestimates $R_a$). A tip that is too small is fragile and easily breaks. ISO 3274 mandates **2 µm** for high-precision surfaces and **5 µm or 10 µm** for general engineering surfaces.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Surface Roughness Measurement

| Industry | Application | Typical Ra Target |
|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic cylinders | Seal bore | 0.2 – 0.8 µm |
| Bearing journals | Journal surface | 0.1 – 0.4 µm |
| Optical mirrors | Reflective surface | < 0.01 µm |
| Automotive body panels | Class-A finish | 0.8 – 1.6 µm |
| Gear flanks | Tooth flank | 0.4 – 0.8 µm |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | Arithmetic mean deviation | µm |
| 2 | $R_a \approx \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i \vert$ | Discrete approximation of $R_a$ | µm |
| 3 | $R_q = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} y(x)^2 \, dx}$ | RMS deviation (quadratic mean) | µm |
| 4 | $R_z = \dfrac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(R_{pi} + R_{vi})$ | Mean of 5 peak-to-valley heights | µm |
| 5 | $R_t = R_{p,\max} + R_{v,\max}$ | Total peak-to-valley over evaluation length | µm |
| 6 | $R_{sk} = \dfrac{1}{R_q^3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} y(x)^3 \, dx$ | Skewness — positive ⇒ porous, negative ⇒ glossy | — |
| 7 | $v = \dfrac{\lambda_c}{t_{sample}}$ | Stylus traverse speed relation | mm/s |
| 8 | $f_s = \dfrac{v}{s_{res}}$ | Sampling frequency (Nyquist criterion) | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Note on Sampling:** The sampling frequency $f_s$ must satisfy the Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$, where $f_{max}$ is the highest spatial frequency of the surface features. In practice, $f_s$ is chosen so that at least **1 sample per 0.5 µm** of horizontal travel is taken.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Arithmetic Mean Roughness $R_a$

Let the surface profile be sampled at $n$ discrete, equally spaced points $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ across a sampling length $L$. At each point, the vertical deviation from the **mean line** (the least-squares line through the profile) is $y_i$.

**Step 1: Definition of the Mean Line**

The mean line is positioned such that the area above it equals the area below it over the sampling length. Mathematically:

$$\int_{0}^{L} y(x) \, dx = 0$$

**Step 2: Definition of $R_a$**

By ISO 4287, $R_a$ is the integral of the absolute value of the ordinate divided by the sampling length:

$$R_a = \frac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$$

**Step 3: Discretization for Digital Computation**

A profilometer outputs sampled voltages proportional to $y(x)$. The integral is approximated by the trapezoidal (or rectangular) sum:

$$\int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i \vert \cdot \Delta x$$

where $\Delta x = L/n$ is the horizontal sampling interval.

**Step 4: Substitute back into the definition of $R_a$**

$$R_a = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i \vert \cdot \frac{L}{n} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i \vert$$

Hence the **discrete working formula** for $R_a$ is:

$$\boxed{R_a = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i \vert}$$

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU problem)**

The ordinate values (in µm) recorded at $n = 11$ equally spaced points across a sampling length $L = 0.8\,mm$ are:

$$y_i = \{-2, +4, -1, +3, -5, +2, -3, +4, -1, +2, -3\}$$

Compute $R_a$.

**Solution:**

Step (a): Sum the absolute values:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{11} \vert y_i \vert = 2 + 4 + 1 + 3 + 5 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 1 + 2 + 3 = 30$$

Step (b): Divide by $n$:

$$R_a = \frac{30}{11} \approx 2.727\,\mu m$$

This value of $R_a \approx 2.73\,\mu m$ places the surface in the cutoff range $2 < R_a \leq 10$, requiring $\lambda_c = 2.5\,mm$ and evaluation length $L_{eval} = 12.5\,mm$ (5 sampling lengths).

### 3.2 Derivation of the RMS Roughness $R_q$

$R_q$ (also called $R_{RMS}$ or $\sigma$) is mathematically more rigorous than $R_a$ because it squares the ordinates, thereby giving **more weight to extreme peaks** (which are the points that initiate fatigue cracks and wear).

**Step 1: Square the Ordinates**

For each sampled point, compute $y_i^2$:

$$y_i^2 = 4, 16, 1, 9, 25, 4, 9, 16, 1, 4, 9$$

**Step 2: Sum the Squares**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{11} y_i^2 = 4 + 16 + 1 + 9 + 25 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 1 + 4 + 9 = 98$$

**Step 3: Take the Mean**

$$\overline{y^2} = \frac{98}{11} \approx 8.909\,\mu m^2$$

**Step 4: Take the Square Root**

$$R_q = \sqrt{8.909} \approx 2.985\,\mu m$$

**Step 5: Relate $R_q$ to $R_a$**

For a Gaussian (random) surface profile, the well-known statistical result is:

$$R_q = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot R_a \approx 1.11 \cdot R_a$$

Verification for our example:

$$1.11 \times 2.727 \approx 3.03\,\mu m \quad (\text{close to our calculated } 2.985\,\mu m)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The factor $1.11$ between $R_q$ and $R_a$ is a direct consequence of the relationship $\sigma = (\pi/2\sqrt{2})\cdot \mu$ for a half-wave-rectified Gaussian distribution. The small discrepancy in our example arises because the profile is not perfectly Gaussian — it has only 11 sample points.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Measurement Methods

| # | Method | Principle | Range ($R_a$) | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stylus (Contact) Profilometer** | Mechanical tracing + LVDT/PZT | 0.01 – 50 µm | Standard, traceable to ISO | Slow, may scratch soft surfaces |
| 2 | **Optical (Interferometric)** | Light interference fringes | 0.001 – 1 µm | Non-contact, 3D mapping | Sensitive to vibration, expensive |
| 3 | **Confocal Microscopy** | Chromatic aberration focus | 0.01 – 10 µm | Non-contact, no prep | Limited vertical range |
| 4 | **Pneumatic** | Pressure drop vs gap | 0.05 – 25 µm | Fast, simple | Indirect, requires calibration |
| 5 | **Capacitive** | Capacitance vs distance | 0.005 – 5 µm | High resolution, non-contact | Sensitive to dirt, limited to conductive surfaces |
| 6 | **Comparison/Visual** | Specimen vs standard | 0.4 – 50 µm | Cheap, fast | Subjective, low accuracy |
| 7 | **Ultrasonic** | Time-of-flight of ultrasonic pulse | 0.5 – 50 µm | Can measure inside pipes | Low resolution, requires couplant |
| 8 | **Electrical (Contact Resistance)** | Resistance across stylus gap | 0.1 – 50 µm | Inexpensive | Only for conductive surfaces |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Stylus-Type Profilometer

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the full signal flow of a modern computer-controlled surface roughness tester (e.g., Talysurf PGI series).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stylus on Surface] --> B[Stylus Assembly + Skid]
    B --> C[LVDT or PZT Transducer]
    C --> D[Pre-amplifier]
    D --> E[Cutoff Filter 2RC Gaussian]
    E --> F[Main Amplifier]
    F --> G[ADC and Microcontroller]
    G --> H[Ra Rq Rz Calculator]
    H --> I[LCD Display]
    H --> J[Printer Output]
    G --> K[Traverse Drive Motor Control]
    K --> L[Lead Screw and Workpiece Stage]
    L --> A
    H --> M[RS232 USB to PC]

    classDef input fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px
    class A,B,C input
    class D,E,F,G,H,K,L process
    class I,J,M output
```

**Description of the Flow:**

1. The **stylus assembly** rides over the surface; vertical motion is converted by the **LVDT/PZT transducer** into a voltage.
2. The **pre-amplifier** boosts the millivolt-level signal.
3. The **cutoff filter** (a 2-pole RC low-pass analog filter per ISO 3274) removes the waviness and form components.
4. The **main amplifier** scales the signal to the ADC input range.
5. The **ADC + microcontroller** samples the signal at the proper rate.
6. The **calculator block** numerically integrates the absolute value (for $R_a$) and the square (for $R_q$), and detects peaks/valleys (for $R_z$).
7. The result is displayed on the **LCD**, sent to a **printer**, or transmitted to a **PC** for SPC charting.

### 4.2 Architecture Flow of a Typical Measurement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Clean Surface] --> P2[Mount Workpiece on Stage]
    P2 --> P3[Set Cutoff from Expected Ra]
    P3 --> P4[Set Traverse Length L = 5 x Cutoff]
    P4 --> P5[Lower Stylus with Correct Force]
    P5 --> P6[Start Traverse at Constant Speed]
    P6 --> P7[Acquire Vertical Displacement Signal]
    P7 --> P8[Apply Cutoff Filter]
    P8 --> P9[Compute Ra Rq Rz]
    P9 --> P10[Display and Record Result]
    P10 --> P11[Compare with Specification]
    P11 --> P12{Pass?}
    P12 -- Yes --> P13[Accept Part]
    P12 -- No --> P14[Reject or Re-machine]

    classDef start fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    classDef compute fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endgood fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#075985,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endbad fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px
    class P1 start
    class P7,P8,P9 compute
    class P12 decision
    class P13 endgood
    class P14 endbad
```

### 4.3 Classification of Surface Finish Measurement Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Surface Roughness Measurement]
    M --> N1[Contact Methods]
    M --> N2[Non-Contact Methods]
    M --> N3[Comparative Methods]

    N1 --> O1[Stylus Profilometer]
    N1 --> O2[Skid-Type Instrument]

    N2 --> P1[Optical]
    N2 --> P2[Pneumatic]
    N2 --> P3[Capacitive]
    N2 --> P4[Ultrasonic]
    N2 --> P5[Inductive]

    P1 --> Q1[Interferometric]
    P1 --> Q2[Light Scattering]
    P1 --> Q3[Confocal]
    P1 --> Q4[Structured Light]

    N3 --> R1[Visual / Tactile Comparison]
    N3 --> R2[Reference Specimen Block]

    classDef root fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:3px
    classDef cat1 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cat2 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px
    class M root
    class N1,N2,N3,O1,O2,P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4,R1,R2 leaf
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "surface roughness." Differentiate between surface roughness and surface waviness.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Surface Roughness:** Surface roughness refers to the small, finely spaced irregularities on a manufactured surface, characterized by short wavelengths and high spatial frequency. It is typically the result of the cutting action of a tool, abrasive grit, or forming process.

**Difference between Roughness and Waviness:**

| Aspect | Surface Roughness | Surface Waviness |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | Short (typically < 1 mm) | Longer (typically > 1 mm) |
| Cause | Tool marks, grit marks, feed marks | Machine vibration, tool deflection, heat treatment stresses |
| Frequency | High spatial frequency | Low spatial frequency |
| Measurement | Above the cutoff wavelength of the filter | Below the cutoff wavelength (filtered out) |
| Symbol | $R_a, R_z, R_q$ | $W_a, W_z$ |

(3 marks awarded for clear definition + tabular difference.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by the "cutoff wavelength" in surface roughness measurement? Why is it necessary?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Cutoff Wavelength ($\lambda_c$):** The cutoff wavelength is the boundary spatial wavelength (or spatial frequency) of a low-pass filter that is applied to the raw surface profile signal in a stylus-type profilometer. Wavelengths longer than $\lambda_c$ are attenuated (treated as waviness/form), while wavelengths shorter than $\lambda_c$ are passed (treated as roughness).

**Why it is necessary:**

1. **To separate roughness from waviness and form** — Without a cutoff, a single $R_a$ value would depend on the traverse length, making measurements incomparable between laboratories.
2. **To standardize measurements** — ISO 4288 prescribes specific $\lambda_c$ values for each $R_a$ range, ensuring globally consistent results.
3. **To prevent inclusion of non-relevant low-frequency errors** (e.g., machine slide straightness).

(3 marks.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **stylus-type surface roughness measuring instrument**. Discuss the role of the cutoff filter. (7 marks)

**(b)** Define $R_a$, $R_z$, and $R_q$. The following ordinate values (in µm) were recorded at 9 equally spaced points across a sampling length of 0.8 mm:

$$y_i = +3, -2, +4, -1, +5, -3, +2, -4, +1$$

Calculate $R_a$ and $R_q$ for this profile. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of a Stylus-Type Profilometer (7 marks):**

Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes. The examiner will look for the following six blocks and their correct sequence:

1. **Stylus Assembly:** A diamond-tipped stylus (tip radius 2–10 µm) is dragged over the surface under a controlled force (≈ 0.75 mN) by a skid or a precision carriage.
2. **Transducer (LVDT/PZT):** Converts vertical displacement of the stylus into a proportional electrical voltage.
3. **Cutoff Filter (2-pole RC or Gaussian):** Low-pass filter with cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c$. Removes low-frequency waviness and form. (For the answer, mention the values of $\lambda_c$ from the ISO 4288 table — 0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 mm.)
4. **Amplifier & ADC:** Amplifies and digitizes the signal.
5. **Microcontroller/Processor:** Computes $R_a, R_z, R_q$ in real time.
6. **Display/Output:** LCD, printer, or PC.

**Role of the Cutoff Filter:** A surface profile contains form + waviness + roughness. The cutoff filter is the **2-pole Butterworth (or 2RC) low-pass filter** that mathematically separates the roughness signal from the lower-frequency components. It is set at a value $\lambda_c$ chosen from the ISO 4288 table based on the expected $R_a$ of the workpiece.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall #1 (–2 marks):** Students often write "filter removes high frequencies." That is **wrong** — the cutoff filter removes **low** frequencies (waviness/form) and **passes** the high-frequency roughness. Read the question carefully.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall #2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to mention the **stylus tip radius** specification (2 µm / 10 µm) per ISO 3274.

(7 marks.)

---

**(b) Calculation of $R_a$ and $R_q$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definitions (1 mark):**
- $R_a$: Arithmetic mean of the absolute ordinates over sampling length.
- $R_z$: Mean of the five largest peak-to-valley heights.
- $R_q$: Root-mean-square of the ordinates.

**Step 2 — Compute $R_a$:**

$$\sum \vert y_i \vert = 3+2+4+1+5+3+2+4+1 = 25$$

$$R_a = \frac{25}{9} \approx 2.778\,\mu m$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_q$:**

$$\sum y_i^2 = 9+4+16+1+25+9+4+16+1 = 85$$

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{85}{9}} = \sqrt{9.444} \approx 3.073\,\mu m$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Cutoff:**

Since $2 < R_a \approx 2.78 \leq 10\,\mu m$, the cutoff $\lambda_c = 2.5\,mm$ and evaluation length $L_{eval} = 12.5\,mm$.

**Step 5 — Verify the $R_q / R_a$ ratio:**

$$\frac{R_q}{R_a} = \frac{3.073}{2.778} \approx 1.106 \approx 1.11 \checkmark$$

This confirms the assumption of a near-Gaussian profile.

(7 marks.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]

**(a)** Explain any **three non-contact methods** of surface roughness measurement. Compare their advantages and limitations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A surface is measured with a stylus of tip radius 10 µm, traversing at 0.5 mm/s. The expected $R_a$ is 0.8 µm. Determine the **(i)** cutoff wavelength, **(ii)** evaluation length, and **(iii)** minimum sampling frequency to avoid aliasing (assume at least 1 sample per 0.5 µm horizontal). (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Non-Contact Methods (7 marks):**

1. **Optical Interferometric Method:** Uses the principle of light interference between a reference beam and a beam reflected from the surface. The fringe pattern is analyzed to extract nanometer-level height information. Used for ultra-precision surfaces (Ra < 0.01 µm). **Limitation:** Sensitive to vibration; requires vibration-isolated table.
2. **Pneumatic Method:** A jet of air at a controlled pressure is directed at the surface. The back-pressure (or flow rate) varies with the gap between the nozzle and the surface. As the surface roughness changes, the pressure varies proportionally. Used for shop-floor inspection of bearing tracks. **Limitation:** Indirect, requires empirical calibration.
3. **Capacitive Method:** A small electrode (probe) is placed near the surface. The surface and the probe form a capacitor whose capacitance depends on the gap. As the surface moves under the probe, the capacitance change is measured. Very high resolution (sub-nm). **Limitation:** Works only on **conductive** surfaces.

(7 marks — 2 marks each for principle + diagram; 1 mark for comparison.)

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R_a = 0.8\,\mu m$, $v = 0.5\,mm/s$, $r_{tip} = 10\,\mu m$.

**(i) Cutoff Wavelength:**

From the ISO 4288 table, since $0.1 < R_a = 0.8 \leq 2\,\mu m$:

$$\boxed{\lambda_c = 0.8\,mm}$$

**(ii) Evaluation Length:**

$$L_{eval} = 5 \times \lambda_c = 5 \times 0.8 = \boxed{4.0\,mm}$$

**(iii) Minimum Sampling Frequency:**

Required sampling resolution: 1 sample per 0.5 µm = $0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,mm$.

At traverse speed $v = 0.5\,mm/s$, the time between samples is:

$$\Delta t = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,mm}{0.5\,mm/s} = 10^{-3}\,s = 1\,ms$$

Therefore the minimum sampling frequency is:

$$f_{s,\min} = \frac{1}{\Delta t} = \boxed{1000\,Hz}$$

(7 marks.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is computing $f_s$ as $v / \lambda_c$ — this gives the spatial frequency component at the cutoff, **not** the ADC sampling rate. The sampling rate is governed by the **horizontal resolution** of the measurement (here, 0.5 µm per sample), not by the cutoff.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Surface roughness = high-frequency, short-wavelength irregularities** on a surface, sitting on top of waviness and form.
- **Most important parameter: $R_a$ (Arithmetic Mean Deviation)** — universally quoted, expressed in µm.
- **$R_a$ discrete formula:** $R_a = \frac{1}{n}\sum \vert y_i \vert$.
- **$R_q$ (RMS) formula:** $R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2}$.
- **$R_z$** = average of 5 peak-to-valley heights within sampling length.
- **For Gaussian profiles:** $R_q \approx 1.11 \cdot R_a$.
- **Cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c$** is selected from ISO 4288 based on the $R_a$ range — common values: **0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 mm**.
- **Evaluation length = $5 \times \lambda_c$** — ALWAYS.
- **Stylus tip radius:** 2 µm (precision) or 10 µm (general).
- **Stylus force:** typically 0.75 mN.
- **Cutoff filter REMOVES LOW frequencies (waviness/form), PASSES HIGH frequencies (roughness)** — not the other way around.
- **Five methods to remember:** Stylus (contact, standard), Optical, Pneumatic, Capacitive, Ultrasonic.
- **Nyquist criterion:** sampling frequency must be high enough to capture the smallest horizontal features without aliasing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Texture: Primary Texture and Secondary Texture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Surface Texture: Primary Texture and Secondary Texture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Surface Texture** is defined as the repetitive or random deviations from the *nominal (ideal) surface* of an object, which form the three-dimensional topography of the surface. It constitutes the micro-geometric irregularities of a manufactured surface and is a critical measurable parameter in metrology and quality control.

According to **ISO 4287:1997** and **ISO 21920** standards, the total surface texture is classified into four hierarchical orders of deviation, arranged by decreasing wavelength:

1. **Form** – Macro-geometric deviation (lowest frequency, longest wavelength)
2. **Secondary Texture (Waviness)** – Intermediate wavelength undulations
3. **Primary Texture (Roughness)** – Short wavelength, fine irregularities
4. **Sub-roughness (Lattice / Molecular irregularities)** – At atomic scale (not measured in conventional instrumentation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Texture (Roughness)** is the component of surface texture characterized by the **shortest wavelength** irregularities that are inherent to the actual manufacturing process itself — such as cutting tool feed marks, grinding grooves, abrasive scratches, or the crystalline structure of the workpiece material.

**Secondary Texture (Waviness)** is the component of surface texture characterized by **intermediate wavelength**, more *periodic* undulations that are *superimposed* upon the primary texture. Waviness originates from secondary causes such as **machine tool vibrations**, **deflection of the workpiece or tool**, **heat treatment distortions**, or **uneven clamping forces**.

| Parameter | Primary Texture (Roughness) | Secondary Texture (Waviness) |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | **0.008 mm – 0.08 mm** (short) | **0.08 mm – 10 mm** (intermediate) |
| Origin | Direct manufacturing process | Machine vibrations / deflections |
| Periodicity | Random or quasi-random | More periodic / repetitive |
| Amplitude | Small (µm scale) | Larger than roughness |
| Filter Used | High-pass (λc filter) | Band-pass filter |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examination context, the **primary texture** is often referred to simply as **"roughness"**, and the **secondary texture** as **"waviness"**. The terms are used interchangeably in many textbooks. Master both terminologies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a freshly ploughed **agricultural field** viewed from an aeroplane:

- From **high altitude (10,000 ft)**, the field looks completely flat — this is the **nominal/ideal form**.
- From a **low altitude (500 ft)**, you start to see large, gentle undulations — the rolling dips and rises spread across metres. These represent the **secondary texture (waviness)** caused by soil settling, drainage, and earth-mover vibrations.
- From **eye level on the ground (1 ft)**, you now see the fine, jagged clods of dirt, tiny furrows, and grainy bumps — this is the **primary texture (roughness)** caused directly by the plough blade and soil tearing action.

Just as a farmer needs to control *both* the large undulations and the fine cloddiness for a good seedbed, a mechanical engineer must control *both* waviness and roughness for a functional mating surface (e.g., bearing surfaces, sealing faces, optical components).

A **second analogy** — a **vinyl LP record**:
- The long, slow warps of the record (you can see them tilt on a turntable) → **secondary texture (waviness)**.
- The microscopic, tightly-packed grooves you can feel → **primary texture (roughness)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standards to Remember:**
> - **Cut-off wavelength (λc)**: standard values = **0.08 mm, 0.25 mm, 0.8 mm, 2.5 mm, 8.0 mm** (per ISO 4288)
> - **Evaluation length (ln)** = **5 × Sampling length (lr)** (per ISO 4288)
> - **Stylus tip radius (for contact profilometer)**: typically **2 µm or 10 µm**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Total Surface Profile

The real surface profile $Z(x)$ measured by a stylus profilometer is the **superposition** of four contributions:

$$
Z(x) = Z_{\text{form}}(x) + Z_{\text{waviness}}(x) + Z_{\text{roughness}}(x) + Z_{\text{sub-roughness}}(x)
$$

Each term operates on a distinct wavelength band. In practice, **sub-roughness is negligible** for engineering surfaces and is dropped.

#### Why Separation is Necessary

A single **Ra** (arithmetical mean roughness) reading cannot distinguish between a wavy surface and a genuinely rough surface, even though their engineering implications are vastly different:
- A wavy bearing surface causes **oil film collapse** and noise.
- A rough bearing surface causes **wear and friction** but supports load better.

Hence, we must **filter** the profile to extract primary and secondary components separately.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Wavelength Filtering (Why & How)

**Why**: Different surface features originate from different physical causes. To diagnose the *cause* of a defect, we must isolate the corresponding *wavelength band*.

**How**: A **Gaussian filter** (per ISO 16610) is applied mathematically to the raw digitized profile to separate wavelengths:

- **High-pass filter** with cut-off $\lambda_c$ → isolates **primary texture (roughness)**
- **Band-pass filter** → isolates **secondary texture (waviness)**
- **Low-pass filter** (very long $\lambda$) → approximates **form**

**Gaussian filter weighting function:**

$$
W(s) = \exp\left[-\pi \left( \frac{s \cdot \ln 2}{\alpha} \right)^2 \right]
$$

where $s$ is the spatial frequency, and $\alpha$ is the filter parameter related to the cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z(x) = Z_{\text{form}} + Z_{\text{wav}} + Z_{\text{rough}}$ | Total profile decomposition | µm |
| 2 | $Ra = \dfrac{1}{lr} \int_0^{lr} \vert Z(x) \vert \, dx$ | Arithmetic mean roughness (primary) | µm |
| 3 | $Rq = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{lr} \int_0^{lr} Z(x)^2 \, dx}$ | Root-mean-square roughness (primary) | µm |
| 4 | $Rt = Z_{\max} - Z_{\min}$ over $lr$ | Total roughness height (primary) | µm |
| 5 | $Wa = \dfrac{1}{ln} \int_0^{ln} \vert W(x) \vert \, dx$ | Mean waviness (secondary) | µm |
| 6 | $Wq = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{ln} \int_0^{ln} W(x)^2 \, dx}$ | RMS waviness (secondary) | µm |
| 7 | $\lambda_c \in \{0.08, \, 0.25, \, 0.8, \, 2.5, \, 8.0\}$ | Standard cut-off wavelengths (ISO 4288) | mm |
| 8 | $ln = 5 \cdot lr$ | Evaluation length = 5 × sampling length | mm |
| 9 | $lr = 5 \cdot \lambda_c$ (typically) | Sampling length selection rule | mm |
| 10 | $f_s \geq 10 \cdot f_c$ | Sampling frequency ≥ 10 × filter cut-off freq. (Nyquist) | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Trap:** In table row 2 and 3, the absolute value bars are rendered as `\vert Z(x) \vert` to prevent markdown table syntax breakage. **Never** write `|Z(x)|` inside a table.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Primary vs. Secondary Texture

| Engineering Application | Critical Parameter | Why it Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Journal bearings** | Primary (Ra) controls friction & wear | Load-supporting asperities |
| **Sealing surfaces (O-rings)** | Secondary (Waviness) controls leak path | Long waves cause leak channels |
| **Optical flats** | Form only; waviness ruins interference | λ/20 flatness needed |
| **Gear flanks** | Both — Ra for pitting, Wt for noise | Vibration excitation |
| **Turbine blades** | Waviness → fatigue crack initiation | Resonant stress concentration |

### 2.5 Selection of Cut-off Wavelength $\lambda_c$

The cut-off is **not arbitrary** — it is selected based on the **expected roughness period** of the manufacturing process (per ISO 4288):

$$
\lambda_c = \text{standard value} \geq 3 \times \text{mean peak spacing (Rsm) of profile}
$$

If the expected Ra is **unknown**, the rule of thumb is:

$$
\lambda_c = 0.8 \text{ mm} \;\; (\text{general turning / milling})
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Selection Rule for Sampling Length $lr$

**Statement:** Prove that the sampling length $lr$ must be at least five times the cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c$ to obtain a statistically stable roughness value.

**Step 1 — Define the cut-off wavelength.**
The cut-off $\lambda_c$ is the wavelength at which the Gaussian filter attenuates the signal amplitude to **50 %** of its original value (the −3 dB point in the spatial frequency domain).

**Step 2 — Establish the filter's spatial frequency.**
The corresponding spatial frequency is:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{\lambda_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Nyquist sampling criterion.**
To digitize the profile without aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the highest frequency content:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_c \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f_s \geq \frac{2}{\lambda_c}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a length-based sampling requirement.**
The minimum number of samples $N$ over a length $lr$ must be:

$$
N = f_s \cdot lr \geq \frac{2 \cdot lr}{\lambda_c}
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the ISO 4288 statistical-stability requirement.**
Empirically, the ISO standard found that for $Ra$ to be stable within **± 4 %**, at least **10 complete periods** of the cut-off wavelength must lie within the sampling length:

$$
lr \geq 10 \cdot \lambda_c
$$

> This is the standard practice; many texts use $lr = 5 \cdot \lambda_c$ as a minimum and report averaged $Ra$ over the longer evaluation length.

**Step 6 — Combine with the evaluation length rule.**
The total evaluation length $ln$ is then:

$$
ln = 5 \cdot lr \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; ln = 5 \cdot 5 \cdot \lambda_c = 25 \cdot \lambda_c
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,lr = 5 \cdot \lambda_c \quad \text{and} \quad ln = 25 \cdot \lambda_c \,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Differences Between Primary and Secondary Texture

| Comparison Axis | Primary Texture (Roughness) | Secondary Texture (Waviness) |
|---|---|---|
| **Wavelength band ($\lambda$)** | $\lambda < \lambda_c$ (short) | $\lambda_c < \lambda < \lambda_f$ (intermediate) |
| **Spatial frequency band** | High frequency | Mid frequency |
| **Source / Origin** | Tool–workpiece interaction, abrasive grain action, crystallographic slip | Machine vibration, tool deflection, thermal distortion, clamping deformation |
| **Periodicity** | Random or quasi-random | More periodic / repetitive |
| **Spacing (Rsm vs Wsm)** | Rsm typically **5 µm – 500 µm** | Wsm typically **0.5 mm – 10 mm** |
| **Measurement filter** | High-pass filter (retains $\lambda < \lambda_c$) | Band-pass filter ($\lambda_c < \lambda < \lambda_f$) |
| **Profile parameter examples** | $Ra, \; Rz, \; Rq, \; Rt, \; Rsk$ | $Wa, \; Wz, \; Wq, \; Wt$ |
| **Effect on friction** | Increases friction at first, but stabilizes wear | Causes dynamic loading, noise, vibration |
| **Effect on sealing** | Holds lubricant in asperity pockets | Creates long leak channels → failure |
| **Detection instrument** | Stylus profilometer (cut-off ON) | Stylus profilometer (cut-off OFF) or special waviness meter |
| **Typical cut-off** | $\lambda_c = 0.8$ mm (general) | $\lambda_f \approx 2.5$ mm or higher |

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A steel shaft is finished on a lathe with a feed of $0.2$ mm/rev. The workpiece diameter is $50$ mm. Using the **RSm-Ra relationship for turned surfaces**, estimate the expected RSm (mean spacing of profile irregularities), and hence recommend a suitable ISO 4288 cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c$.

**Step 1 — Compute the theoretical feed mark spacing.**
The feed marks of a turning process repeat circumferentially once per revolution. The axial spacing equals the feed $f$:

$$
RSm_{\text{theoretical}} = f = 0.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ISO 4288 selection rule.**
The cut-off wavelength must be at least 3 times the mean peak spacing:

$$
\lambda_c \geq 3 \cdot RSm = 3 \times 0.2 = 0.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Round up to the next standard ISO 4288 value.**
Standard values are $\{0.08, \, 0.25, \, 0.8, \, 2.5, \, 8.0\}$ mm.

$$
\boxed{\,\lambda_c = 0.8 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(recommended)}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine sampling and evaluation lengths.**

$$
lr = 5 \cdot \lambda_c = 5 \times 0.8 = 4.0 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
ln = 5 \cdot lr = 5 \times 4.0 = 20.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the roughness contribution to total profile amplitude.**
If the theoretical peak-to-valley of the feed mark is $h = \dfrac{f^2}{8R}$ for a circular profile (where $R$ is the workpiece radius $25$ mm):

$$
h = \frac{(0.2)^2}{8 \times 25} = \frac{0.04}{200} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm} = 0.2 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

The expected theoretical $Rt$:

$$
Rt = 4 \cdot h = 0.8 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

(The factor 4 converts peak-to-valley of a single sinusoid to total range.)

> [!NOTE]
> Real measured values will be **higher** than this theoretical $Rt$ because of built-up edge, vibration, and tool wear — but this gives a useful first-order check.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Surface Profile Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Stylus Profilometer Signal Z of x] --> B{Signal Conditioning and Digitisation}
    B --> C[Digital Profile Array Z of x]
    C --> D[Form Removal Filter - Low Pass - Long Wavelength]
    D --> E[Residual Profile R of x]
    E --> F1[High Pass Filter with Cutoff lambdac]
    E --> F2[Band Pass Filter - lambdac to lambdaf]
    F1 --> G1[Primary Texture - Roughness - Short Wavelength]
    F2 --> G2[Secondary Texture - Waviness - Intermediate Wavelength]
    G1 --> H1[Compute Ra, Rz, Rq, Rt]
    G2 --> H2[Compute Wa, Wz, Wq, Wt]
    H1 --> I1[Primary Texture Output]
    H2 --> I2[Secondary Texture Output]
    I1 --> J[Combined Surface Texture Report]
    I2 --> J
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Causal Origin of Primary vs Secondary Texture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P[Primary Texture Causes]
        P1[Cutting Tool Geometry] --> P2[Feed Marks]
        P3[Abrasive Grain Action] --> P4[Grinding Grooves]
        P5[Crystal Slip] --> P6[Micro-tearing]
        P7[Built-up Edge] --> P8[Irregular Tearing]
    end
    subgraph S[Secondary Texture Causes]
        S1[Machine Tool Vibration] --> S2[Periodic Undulation]
        S3[Tool Deflection] --> S4[Bowed Profile]
        S5[Thermal Distortion] --> S6[Heat-affected Waviness]
        S7[Clamping Force Variation] --> S8[Asymmetric Wave]
    end
    P2 --> O[Primary Texture Output]
    P4 --> O
    P6 --> O
    P8 --> O
    S2 --> T[Secondary Texture Output]
    S4 --> T
    S6 --> T
    S8 --> T
    O --> R[Final Surface Profile]
    T --> R
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cut-off Wavelength Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Manufacturing Process] --> B{Process Type}
    B -->|Turning Milling| C[Estimate RSm from Feed or Pitch]
    B -->|Grinding| D[Estimate RSm from Grit Pitch]
    B -->|Unknown| E[Default to lambdac 0.8 mm]
    C --> F[Apply Rule lambdac greater than 3 RSm]
    D --> F
    F --> G{Rounded to Standard}
    G -->|Standard 0.08| H1[Cutoff 0.08 mm - Very Fine]
    G -->|Standard 0.25| H2[Cutoff 0.25 mm - Fine]
    G -->|Standard 0.8| H3[Cutoff 0.8 mm - General]
    G -->|Standard 2.5| H4[Cutoff 2.5 mm - Coarse]
    G -->|Standard 8.0| H5[Cutoff 8.0 mm - Very Coarse]
    H1 --> I[Compute lr 5 lambdac and ln 5 lr]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description (for Examiner-Oriented Sketch)

A **typical measured surface profile** (as seen on a chart recorder) consists of:
- A gently curving baseline (form)
- Superimposed on it, broad humps of 1–10 mm wavelength (waviness)
- Superimposed on the waviness, fine zig-zags of < 1 mm wavelength (roughness)

The **stylus tip** (radius 2 µm) traces this composite profile. The electronic filter then **strips off** the form, the waviness, leaving only the fine zig-zag pattern (primary texture) for $Ra$ computation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define primary texture and secondary texture of a surface. How are they distinguished from each other?**

**Model Answer:**

**Primary texture (roughness)** refers to the fine, **short-wavelength** irregularities on a manufactured surface that result directly from the material-removal process — such as cutting tool feed marks, grinding grooves, or abrasive scratches. Its wavelength is typically **less than the cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c$**, and its amplitude is in the micrometre range.

**Secondary texture (waviness)** refers to the **intermediate-wavelength**, more periodic undulations that are *superimposed* upon the primary texture. Waviness arises from causes such as **machine tool vibrations, tool deflection, thermal distortion, and uneven clamping forces**, and has wavelengths typically between $\lambda_c$ and $\lambda_f$ (form cut-off).

**Distinguishing features:**

| Feature | Primary | Secondary |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | Short ($< 1$ mm) | Intermediate (1–10 mm) |
| Periodicity | Random | Periodic |
| Cause | Direct process | Indirect (machine, heat) |
| Filter | High-pass | Band-pass |

**(3 marks)** — 1 for each definition, 1 for distinguishing features.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is the significance of the cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c$ in surface texture measurement? List any four standard values of $\lambda_c$ as per ISO 4288.**

**Model Answer:**

The cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c$ is the **spatial wavelength** at which the electronic (or digital Gaussian) filter in a surface profilometer separates **primary texture (roughness)** from **secondary texture (waviness)**. Components of the profile with wavelength **shorter than $\lambda_c$** are retained as roughness, while longer-wavelength components are filtered out as waviness or form.

**Significance:**
- Ensures $Ra$ measurement is **comparable** across different instruments and laboratories.
- Prevents **waviness** from inflating the roughness value.
- Standardises the **sampling length** $lr = 5 \cdot \lambda_c$.

**Four standard values of $\lambda_c$ (ISO 4288):**

$$
\lambda_c \in \{\, 0.08 \text{ mm},\; 0.25 \text{ mm},\; 0.8 \text{ mm},\; 2.5 \text{ mm} \,\}
$$

(The fifth value, 8.0 mm, is used for very coarse surfaces.)

**(3 marks)** — 1 for definition, 1 for significance, 1 for standard values.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **classification of surface texture** into form, waviness (secondary texture), and roughness (primary texture). Discuss the **causes** of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the **sampling length $lr$**, **evaluation length $ln$**, and the **cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c$**. What is the role of the cut-off wavelength in separating primary and secondary texture? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Surface Texture**

The total surface deviation $Z(x)$ of a real engineering surface can be decomposed, by wavelength, into three nested hierarchies:

1. **Form** — the macro-shape error (e.g., flatness, cylindricity). Wavelength: longest.
2. **Secondary Texture (Waviness)** — intermediate undulations, periodic in nature.
3. **Primary Texture (Roughness)** — fine, short-wavelength irregularities.

**Causes:**

| Component | Primary Cause |
|---|---|
| Form | Machine slide errors, fixturing, warping after heat treatment |
| Waviness | Vibration, deflection, thermal distortion, clamping unevenness |
| Roughness | Tool tip geometry, feed, abrasive grain size, material microstructure |

*(Examiner may award 4 marks for sketch, 2 marks for causes, 1 mark for classification.)*

**(b) Derivation of $lr$, $ln$, $\lambda_c$ Relationship**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final result is:

$$
\boxed{\,lr = 5 \cdot \lambda_c \quad \text{and} \quad ln = 25 \cdot \lambda_c \,}
$$

**Role of $\lambda_c$ in separation:**

The cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c$ acts as the **decision threshold** in the Gaussian filter. The filter's transfer function equals 0.5 at $\lambda = \lambda_c$. Thus:
- Spatial components with $\lambda < \lambda_c$ → **passes** → classified as **primary texture (roughness)**.
- Spatial components with $\lambda > \lambda_c$ → **attenuated** → classified as **secondary texture (waviness)** or form.

This wavelength-based separation is the **only rigorous way** to unambiguously distinguish primary from secondary texture.

*(4 marks for derivation, 2 marks for sampling/evaluation rule, 1 mark for role explanation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **stylus-type profilometer** method of measuring surface texture. Explain how the instrument can be configured to measure **primary texture** only, by appropriate choice of cut-off wavelength. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical shaft of $40$ mm diameter is turned on a lathe with a feed of $0.3$ mm/rev. Using ISO 4288 rules, determine the **recommended cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c$**, the **sampling length $lr$**, and the **evaluation length $ln$**. Comment on whether the resulting $Ra$ will predominantly reflect primary or secondary texture. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stylus Profilometer Method**

A **stylus profilometer** consists of:
- A **diamond stylus** (tip radius 2 µm or 10 µm) dragged at constant speed across the surface.
- A **piezoelectric or LVDT transducer** converting vertical stylus motion into an electrical signal.
- An **amplifier and filter chain** (with selectable $\lambda_c$).
- A **digital processor** computing $Ra, Rz, Rq, Rt$.
- A **chart recorder or display**.

**Working:**
The stylus tip follows the surface profile. Vertical displacement is converted to voltage, which is digitised. The digital profile is filtered through a **Gaussian filter** with the selected $\lambda_c$. Only the components with $\lambda < \lambda_c$ remain for the $Ra$ computation.

**To measure primary texture only:**
- Set $\lambda_c = 0.8$ mm (or as per ISO 4288 selection).
- Ensure the high-pass filter eliminates all waviness (which has $\lambda > \lambda_c$).
- Sample over $lr = 5 \lambda_c$, average over $ln = 5 lr$.

*(3 marks for instrument description, 2 marks for working, 2 marks for filter and primary-texture-only configuration.)*

**(b) Numerical Determination**

**Step 1 — Compute theoretical peak spacing.**
For turning, the axial feed mark spacing equals the feed:

$$
RSm_{\text{theoretical}} = f = 0.3 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply ISO 4288 selection rule.**

$$
\lambda_c \geq 3 \cdot RSm = 3 \times 0.3 = 0.9 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Round up to the next standard ISO value.**
Standard set = $\{0.08, \, 0.25, \, 0.8, \, 2.5, \, 8.0\}$ mm.

The next value above 0.9 mm is **2.5 mm**.

$$
\boxed{\,\lambda_c = 2.5 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Sampling and evaluation lengths.**

$$
\boxed{\,lr = 5 \cdot \lambda_c = 5 \times 2.5 = 12.5 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,ln = 5 \cdot lr = 5 \times 12.5 = 62.5 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on primary vs. secondary texture.**
Since the feed mark spacing ($0.3$ mm) is much smaller than $\lambda_c$ ($2.5$ mm), the **feed marks (primary texture)** will *pass* the high-pass filter and contribute fully to the measured $Ra$. Any machine vibration components with wavelength $> 2.5$ mm (e.g., spindle runout undulations) will be filtered out as waviness.

**Conclusion:** The measured $Ra$ will predominantly reflect **primary texture (roughness)**, with secondary texture contributions effectively suppressed.

*(3 marks for step 1–2, 2 marks for step 3–4, 2 marks for the comment.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "wavelength" with "amplitude"** — Many students state "waviness has high amplitude". While true, the *defining* feature is *wavelength*, not amplitude. Examiners will deduct for this.
> 2. **Wrong cut-off rule** — The rule is $\lambda_c \geq 3 \cdot RSm$, **not** $\lambda_c = 3 \cdot RSm$. Always round **up** to the next *standard* value.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 5** — Both $lr = 5\lambda_c$ *and* $ln = 5lr$ must be quoted. Missing the second factor costs a full mark.
> 4. **Mixing up "primary" and "secondary"** — Primary = roughness (fine). Secondary = waviness (broad). This is the single most common error.
> 5. **Not stating the standard** — Always reference **ISO 4287 / ISO 4288** for credit. Vague answers lose marks.
> 6. **Skipping units** — $\lambda_c$ in **mm**, $Ra$ in **µm** (or µm). Unit-less numerical answers are penalised.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Primary Texture = Roughness = fine, short wavelength, random, from direct process**
- [x] **Secondary Texture = Waviness = intermediate wavelength, periodic, from machine vibration / deflection**
- [x] **Decomposition equation:** $Z(x) = Z_{\text{form}} + Z_{\text{wav}} + Z_{\text{rough}}$
- [x] **Standard cut-offs (ISO 4288):** 0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 mm
- [x] **Selection rule:** $\lambda_c \geq 3 \cdot RSm$ → round up to next standard
- [x] **Sampling length:** $lr = 5 \cdot \lambda_c$
- [x] **Evaluation length:** $ln = 5 \cdot lr = 25 \cdot \lambda_c$
- [x] **Filter type for primary texture:** High-pass Gaussian (ISO 16610)
- [x] **Filter type for secondary texture:** Band-pass (between $\lambda_c$ and $\lambda_f$)
- [x] **Stylus tip radius:** typically **2 µm** (for fine surfaces) or **10 µm** (general)
- [x] **Nyquist criterion:** sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_c$
- [x] **Turned surface RSm** = feed per revolution
- [x] **Grinding RSm** $\approx$ grit pitch
- [x] **Effect on bearings:** Primary → wear, Secondary → vibration / oil film failure
- [x] **Effect on seals:** Secondary is *more* harmful (long leak channels)
- [x] **Wavelength defines the texture, not amplitude**
- [x] **Always mention ISO 4287 / ISO 4288** in answers
- [x] **Units:** $\lambda_c$ and $lr$ in **mm**, $Ra$ and $Wa$ in **µm**
- [x] **Plot parameters use letter "R" for roughness, "W" for waviness** (e.g., $Ra$, $Wa$; $Rz$, $Wz$)
- [x] **Form parameters use letter "P"** (per ISO 21920) — newer convention
- [x] **Cause-and-effect link:** Tool marks → Primary; Vibration → Secondary
- [x] **KTU-favourite question type:** "Differentiate primary and secondary texture" + "cut-off selection numerical"
- [x] **Memorise the factor chain:** $\lambda_c \rightarrow 5\lambda_c = lr \rightarrow 25\lambda_c = ln$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Lay Specification for Surface Textures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** The Lay Specification for Surface Textures # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Mechanical Instrumentation** and **Metrology**, the term **"Lay"** refers to the **direction of the predominant surface pattern** produced on a workpiece by the prevailing machining, forming, or finishing process. According to the international standard **ISO 4287** and **ISO 1302**, lay is defined as: > *"The direction of the predominant surface pattern, which is usually determined by the production method used."* In simpler terms, **lay describes the orientation of the microscopic peaks and valleys (irregularities) that make up the texture of a manufactured surface**. It is one of the four fundamental surface texture characteristics recognized by ISO, alongside **roughness, waviness, and flaws**. The **direction of lay** is the geometric orientation of these irregularities relative to a reference line — usually the line of the technical drawing on which the surface texture symbol is placed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Lay is *not* a numerical roughness value. It is a **qualitative geometric descriptor** that tells the manufacturer *which way* the tool marks or surface striations should run on the finished component. ## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Surface Texture Before diving into lay, it is essential to situate it within the broader surface texture framework that KTU expects students to know: | Pillar | Symbol | Description | |---|---|---| | **Roughness** | Ra, Rz | Closely spaced, finely-spaced irregularities produced by the cutting action | | **Waviness** | Wt, Wa | Wider-spaced irregularities caused by tool deflection, vibration, or heat treatment | | **Lay** | (Symbol on drawing) | **Direction of the predominant surface pattern** | | **Flaws** | (Irregular marks) | Random, isolated defects like scratches, cracks, or pores | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wood Grain" Metaphor Imagine a piece of **polished wooden furniture**. The wood has a *grain* — a clear directional pattern that you can both **see and feel** with your fingertips. If you run your finger along the grain, it feels smooth. If you run it across the grain, it feels noticeably rougher. **Lay is the engineering equivalent of wood grain**, but on metal, plastic, or ceramic surfaces. - When a lathe turns a shaft, it leaves **concentric circular lay** around the shaft axis. - When a milling machine cuts a flat plate, the lay runs **perpendicular to the feed direction**. - When a grinder polishes a surface, the lay appears as a series of **fine parallel lines** in the direction of grinding. This directionality is critically important because: 1. **Functional performance** (e.g., bearing surfaces require lay perpendicular to the load axis). 2. **Sealing surfaces** (gaskets, O-rings) require lay in a specific orientation to prevent leakage. 3. **Aesthetic appearance** (consumer products) often demand lay be aligned with the viewing direction. 4. **Friction and wear** characteristics change dramatically with lay direction. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter to a KTU engineer?** A bearing surface machined with lay running *parallel* to the shaft axis (instead of perpendicular) will wear out **3 to 5 times faster** due to micro-ploughing and inadequate hydrodynamic lubrication film formation. ## 1.4 Why Lay is Specified — The Engineering Imperative Modern manufacturing demands that surface texture is **fully communicated** to every stakeholder — from the design engineer to the machine shop floor worker to the quality inspector. A complete surface finish specification must answer three questions: 1. **How much?** → Quantified by *Ra* or *Rz* values. 2. **How spaced?** → Described by *cut-off*, *sampling length*. 3. **In which direction?** → **Answered by the Lay symbol.** Without the lay specification, the manufacturer is free to produce the surface in *any* direction, which may be functionally unsuitable for the application. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Symbol Convention:** The lay symbol is **always placed inside the long arm (or the relevant segment) of the basic surface finish symbol (the tick or the V-in-a-circle)**. It is the only qualitative symbol that has its own dedicated slot in the ISO 1302 graphical language. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The ISO 1302 Standard — The Global Language of Lay The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** published **ISO 1302** specifically to standardize how lay directions are graphically communicated on engineering drawings. The standard defines a set of **eight primary lay symbols**, each corresponding to a unique geometric pattern of surface irregularities. ### 2.1.1 The Complete Lay Symbol Library | No. | Lay Symbol | Geometric Interpretation | Typical Production Process | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **=** | Parallel to the line representing the surface in the drawing | Turning, shaping, broaching, cylindrical grinding | | 2 | **⊥** | Perpendicular to the line representing the surface | End milling, face milling, surface grinding (cross-feed) | | 3 | **X** | Crossed in two directions (approximately 45° to the surface line) | Lapping, honing, lapping, two-pass grinding | | 4 | **M** | Multidirectional (no predominant direction) | Electropolishing, EDM, electrochemical machining, blasting | | 5 | **C** | Circular (concentric circles) | Cylindrical turning on a lathe, cylindrical grinding without traverse | | 6 | **R** | Radial (radiating from a center point) | Centrifugal finishing, certain polishing processes on circular features | | 7 | **P** | Particulate, non-directional (random pits and peaks) | Sand casting, shot blasting, grit blasting, investment casting | | 8 | **—** | (No symbol given) | Manufacturer is **free to choose** any lay direction | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** When the drawing shows **no lay symbol** at all, the **default** is that the manufacturer may select *any* lay direction. This is a deliberate "freedom-of-choice" convention and is not an oversight. ### 2.1.2 Lay Symbol — Reference Line Rules The lay symbol is interpreted **relative to the reference line** on the drawing. The reference line is typically the **centerline or the line of projection** of the surface on the engineering drawing. - **=** means lay is parallel to that reference line. - **⊥** means lay is perpendicular to that reference line. - **C** and **R** are interpreted relative to the **center of the circle** representing a rotationally symmetric feature. ## 2.2 Engineering Application Matrix — Why Each Lay Direction is Chosen | Lay Direction | Functional Purpose | Real-World Application Examples | |---|---|---| | **=** (Parallel) | Reduces fluid drag in axial flow; aids sliding wear; breaks up hydrodynamic film for grip | Piston skirts, journal bearings (sometimes), cylinder bores for piston rings | | **⊥** (Perpendicular) | Traps lubricant reservoirs between peaks; ideal for dynamic loading | Shaft journals, bearing surfaces, wear plates, cam followers | | **X** (Crossed) | Provides isotropic (uniform-in-all-directions) lubrication behavior | Gear tooth flanks, high-precision sliding interfaces, bearing raceways | | **M** (Multidirectional) | Uniform friction in all directions; aesthetic | Decorative panels, optical components, mould surfaces | | **C** (Circular) | Continuous run-out of fluid; rotational symmetry maintained | Shaft bearing seats, rotating seal surfaces, optical mirrors | | **R** (Radial) | Allows oil to be thrown outward by centrifugal force; balanced wear | Bearing raceways, thrust washers, pump impeller hubs | | **P** (Particulate) | High adhesion; mechanical keying for coatings or bonded joints | Bonded rubber-to-metal joints, paint adhesion surfaces, brazed joints | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Industrial Case:** In **automotive cylinder bores**, the lay is intentionally specified as **X (crossed) at 45° angles** through a process called *honing*. This creates intersecting micro-grooves that retain oil film during the piston stroke, reducing friction and wear by up to **40%** compared to parallel ground surfaces. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet While lay is **primarily qualitative**, the specification system involves a few numerical relationships and rules that KTU may test in calculations or multiple-choice questions. | Concept | Notation | Value / Rule | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | Surface roughness (Arithmetic mean) | $Ra$ | Micrometres ($\mu m$) | Quantifies height of irregularities | | Ten-point height roughness | $Rz$ | Micrometres ($\mu m$) | Mean of 5 highest peaks + 5 lowest valleys | | Sampling length | $lr$ | $0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 \text{ mm}$ | Standard ISO 4288 values | | Evaluation length | $ln$ | $ln = 5 \times lr$ | Average of 5 sampling lengths | | Lay direction symbols | $L$ | $\{=, \perp, X, M, C, R, P\}$ | Qualitative direction of pattern | | Default cut-off (sampling length) for $Ra$ | $lr$ | e.g., $Ra = 0.4$ to $3.2 \mu m$ → $lr = 0.8$ mm | Industry-standard pairing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Note:** There is **no direct formula** that converts lay (qualitative) to $Ra$ (quantitative). The two are **independent parameters** and must be specified together on a complete surface finish callout. ## 2.4 Anatomy of a Complete ISO 1302 Surface Finish Callout A full surface texture symbol consists of **two distinct regions**: ``` ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ a b c │ │ ┌────────┐ ┌────┴────┐ ┌───┴───┐ │ │ │ Ra │ │ Lay │ │ Rz │ │ │ │ value │ │ Symbol │ │ opt. │ │ │ └────────┘ └─────────┘ └───────┘ │ │ │ │ d │ │ ┌──────────┐ │ │ │ Sampling │ │ │ │ length │ │ │ └──────────┘ │ └────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` - **Position `a`**: The roughness parameter name (e.g., $Ra$) and its numerical value. - **Position `b`**: The **lay symbol** — *this is the focus of our study*. - **Position `c`**: A second optional roughness parameter (e.g., $Rz$). - **Position `d`**: The sampling length or cut-off value. ## 2.5 Step-by-Step Logic of Lay Specification The engineer follows this structured decision tree when specifying lay: 1. **Identify the function** of the surface (sliding, sealing, bearing, decorative). 2. **Determine the desired friction and wear behavior** (isotropic, directional, hydrodynamic). 3. **Map the lay direction to the reference geometry** on the drawing (axis of rotation, line of projection). 4. **Select the appropriate lay symbol** from the ISO 1302 library. 5. **Combine** the lay symbol with the $Ra$ value inside the surface finish symbol. 6. **Annotate** the engineering drawing using the long arm pointing to the relevant surface. > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked to *interpret* a lay symbol on a drawing, always begin your answer by **identifying the reference line or surface** that the symbol relates to. Many students lose marks because they describe the symbol in isolation rather than *relating it to the part's geometry*. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Problem: Reading a Surface Finish Callout **Problem Statement:** A drawing shows the following ISO 1302 surface finish specification on a **cylindrical journal of a rotating shaft**: - $Ra$ value = $0.8 \, \mu m$ - Lay symbol = **=** (parallel) - Sampling length = $0.8$ mm - Evaluation length = $5 \times lr = 4.0$ mm **Required:** 1. Interpret the lay direction in plain engineering language. 2. Determine the most likely production process. 3. Evaluate whether this specification is *appropriate* for a high-speed rotating journal. 4. Recommend a corrected specification if inappropriate. ### Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation #### Step 1: Interpret the Lay Symbol The symbol **=** indicates that the lay is **parallel to the reference line** on the drawing. For a cylindrical shaft viewed in a standard orthographic projection, the reference line is the **longitudinal axis of the shaft**. Therefore, the lay pattern on the journal surface consists of **fine longitudinal striations running parallel to the shaft axis** — like the grain of a wooden dowel. #### Step 2: Identify the Production Process A longitudinal lay on a cylindrical surface is typically produced by one of the following processes: $$ \text{Longitudinal Lay} \implies \begin{cases} \text{Cylindrical grinding with axial traverse} \\ \text{Boring} \\ \text{Reaming} \\ \text{Honing (if horizontal)} \\ \text{Planing/Shaping} \end{cases} $$ For a $Ra$ of $0.8 \, \mu m$ (a moderately smooth finish), the most likely process is **cylrical grinding with fine cross-feed**. #### Step 3: Functional Evaluation A rotating journal typically operates in one of the following loading scenarios: - **Pure radial load** (e.g., in a journal bearing) - **Combined radial + axial load** (e.g., tapered roller bearing) For a **hydrodynamic journal bearing**, the optimum lay direction is **perpendicular (⊥)** to the axis of rotation. The perpendicular grooves act as **micro-reservoirs** that retain lubricant and feed it into the load zone. This is the principle exploited in **oil-retaining bearings** used in electric motors and turbomachinery. A **parallel ( = ) lay** on a journal bearing is **functionally inferior** because: - The longitudinal grooves do **not retain oil** effectively. - Hydrodynamic pressure can collapse between peaks. - Wear tends to be **non-uniform**, leading to ovality. #### Step 4: Quantitative Wear Estimate (Simplified Archard Analysis) The **Archard wear equation** gives the volumetric wear rate as: $$ Q = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H} $$ Where: - $Q$ = volumetric wear (m³) - $K$ = wear coefficient (dimensionless, typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$) - $W$ = normal load (N) - $L$ = sliding distance (m) - $H$ = hardness of the softer material (Pa) For **lay parallel to the load direction**, the wear coefficient $K$ is approximately: $$ K_{\parallel} \approx 3 \times K_{\perp} $$ This means the parallel lay will cause roughly **3× higher volumetric wear** than the perpendicular lay for identical operating conditions. #### Step 5: Recommendation The corrected specification should be: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Ra} = 0.8 \, \mu m \\ &\text{Lay} = \perp \text{ (perpendicular)} \\ &\text{Sampling length} = 0.8 \text{ mm} \\ &\text{Evaluation length} = 4.0 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ This corrected specification directs the manufacturer to **grind the journal with a fine cross-feed** (no longitudinal traverse or minimal traverse), producing lay perpendicular to the shaft axis — the functionally correct orientation for hydrodynamic lubrication. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Lay Direction vs. Functional Application | Engineering Application | Load Direction | Recommended Lay Symbol | Rationale | |---|---|---|---| | Journal bearing | Radial (perpendicular to axis) | ⊥ | Grooves retain oil film | | Cylinder bore (piston) | Reciprocating axial | X (45°) | Cross-hatched honing retains oil | | Sliding guideway | Perpendicular to slide | ⊥ | Oil reservoirs along edge | | Rotary seal face | Axial thrust | C | Concentric grooves aid sealing | | Shaft under torsion | Tangential | = | Lay aligned with principal stress | | Optical reflector | None (decorative) | M | Isotropic scattering | | Paint adhesion surface | Bonding (chemical) | P | Micro-roughness increases area | | Gear tooth flank | Combined rolling-sliding | X | Even stress distribution | ## 3.3 Quantitative Relationship — Lay Direction and Surface Bearing Area The **surface bearing ratio (tp)** is the percentage of the surface that actually supports the load, defined as: $$ tp = \frac{\text{Bearing area (load-supporting peaks)}}{\text{Total sampling area}} \times 100 \% $$ The lay direction influences $tp$ as follows: $$ \begin{aligned} tp_{\parallel} &= tp_0 \cdot \cos(\theta) \\ tp_{\perp} &= tp_0 \cdot \sin(\theta) \\ tp_{X} &= tp_0 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $tp_0$ = nominal bearing ratio for an isotropic surface - $\theta$ = angle between the lay direction and the load direction - $tp_X$ = bearing ratio for a 45° cross-hatched surface (factor of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ accounts for the area-projection effect) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Numerical Insight:** The maximum surface bearing area for a journal bearing occurs when the lay is **perpendicular to the load**, i.e., $\theta = 90°$, giving $\sin(\theta) = 1$ and thus $tp_{\perp} = tp_0$. This is why the **⊥ symbol is the standard specification for hydrodynamic bearings**. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Sampling Length Selection A grinding process produces a surface with $Ra = 1.6 \, \mu m$. According to ISO 4288, the appropriate sampling length must be selected from a standard set. The ISO 4288 cut-off selection table states: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{If } 0.4 \, \mu m \leq Ra < 4.0 \, \mu m \\ &\text{then } lr = 0.8 \text{ mm} \\ &\text{and } ln = 5 \times lr = 4.0 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ So for our $Ra = 1.6 \, \mu m$: $$ \boxed{lr = 0.8 \text{ mm}, \quad ln = 4.0 \text{ mm}} $$ This value is **independent of the lay direction** — it depends only on the $Ra$ magnitude. However, it is a **mandatory component of the complete surface finish callout** alongside the lay symbol. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Surface Texture Parameters The diagram below illustrates the relationship between the four main surface texture parameters and how lay fits into the overall framework. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Surface Texture

All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** **Q: Define the term "Lay" in the context of surface texture. List any four ISO 1302 lay symbols with their geometric interpretations.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Lay is the **direction of the predominant surface pattern** produced on a workpiece by the manufacturing process employed. It is a **qualitative** surface texture parameter that describes *orientation* rather than *magnitude*. **Four Lay Symbols:** | Symbol | Geometric Interpretation | |---|---| | **=** | Parallel to the reference line on the drawing | | **⊥** | Perpendicular to the reference line on the drawing | | **X** | Crossed in two directions at approximately 45° | | **C** | Circular, concentric about a center | *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for symbols — 0.5 each)* ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** **Q: A journal bearing surface requires hydrodynamic lubrication. Specify the appropriate lay direction. Justify your answer using the concept of lubricant film formation.** **Model Answer:** **Specified Lay:** **⊥ (Perpendicular)** — i.e., lay perpendicular to the axis of rotation of the journal. **Justification:** The perpendicular lay creates **micro-grooves running across the direction of shaft rotation**. These grooves act as **reservoirs for lubricant**, continuously feeding oil into the load zone as the shaft rotates. This: - Maintains a stable **hydrodynamic oil film** between the journal and the bearing. - Prevents direct **metal-to-metal contact** during start-up, run-up, and shut-down. - Reduces **frictional heating** and prolongs **fatigue life** of the bearing. A parallel lay ( = ) would not retain oil effectively, leading to film collapse and accelerated wear. *(3 marks: 1 for specification, 2 for justification)* ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]** **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the ISO 1302 standard for the graphical specification of lay direction on engineering drawings. With the help of sketches, describe **at least six** lay symbols and the corresponding manufacturing processes that produce them. **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A rotating shaft of diameter 50 mm runs at 3000 rpm in a hydrodynamic journal bearing. The bearing housing material is bronze, and the load is purely radial. The original specification called for a lay parallel to the shaft axis, but observations after 500 hours of operation show heavy wear. Analyze the failure and recommend a corrected lay specification, including the production process, sampling length, and evaluation length for a target $Ra = 0.8 \, \mu m$. --- ### Model Solution for Question Choice A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **ISO 1302 Standard — Overview:** The **ISO 1302** international standard provides a unified graphical vocabulary for communicating surface texture requirements. The lay symbol is one of the four components of the complete surface finish callout, and it occupies a dedicated position inside the basic surface finish symbol. The standard defines **eight principal lay symbols** that cover virtually every possible directionality of surface irregularities encountered in manufacturing. **Six Lay Symbols with Descriptions and Production Processes:** | Symbol | Description | Production Process | |---|---|---| | **=** | Lay parallel to the reference line | Lathe turning (longitudinal feed), shaping, broaching, cylindrical grinding (with axial traverse) | | **⊥** | Lay perpendicular to the reference line | Surface grinding (with cross-feed), end milling, facing | | **X** | Crossed at approximately 45° in two directions | Honing, lapping, two-pass grinding | | **M** | Multidirectional with no predominant pattern | Electrical discharge machining (EDM), electropolishing | | **C** | Circular, concentric about a center | Cylindrical turning (no longitudinal feed), spin finishing | | **R** | Radial, radiating outward from a center | Centrifugal barrel finishing, certain polishing techniques | **Drawing Placement:** The lay symbol is always placed inside the **long arm** of the basic surface finish symbol (an "inverted V" or "tick"). The **long arm points to the surface** to which the specification applies. The reference line for interpreting the lay symbol is the **line of the drawing on which the surface appears** — typically the projection line for the surface edge. **Sketches (described in text form):** - A shaft with **=** would show longitudinal lines running along the length. - A shaft with **⊥** would show circumferential rings around the shaft. - A shaft with **C** would show concentric circles. - A shaft with **R** would show lines radiating from a central point on the end face. - A flat plate with **M** would show a chaotic, non-directional pattern. - A flat plate with **X** would show two sets of lines intersecting at ~90° to each other. **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for ISO 1302 introduction, 4 marks for 6 symbols × 0.5 each with process, 2 marks for drawing placement and sketch descriptions. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Failure Analysis:** The given conditions are: - Shaft diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm}$ - Speed $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$ - Load: Purely radial - Application: Hydrodynamic journal bearing - Original lay: **=** (parallel to shaft axis) - Failure observed: Heavy wear after 500 hours **Diagnosis:** A parallel lay (=) on a radial-loaded journal bearing is **functionally incorrect**. The longitudinal striations run *along* the direction of relative motion between the journal and the bearing. This configuration has two critical disadvantages: 1. **No Oil Reservoirs:** The longitudinal grooves do not retain oil effectively because centrifugal force throws the oil outward *parallel* to the lay direction. In a perpendicular lay (⊥), the circumferential grooves act as reservoirs. 2. **Ploughing Effect:** The peaks of the longitudinal lay act as micro-ploughs that score the softer bronze bearing material, accelerating adhesive wear. **Mathematical Estimation of Wear:** The Archard wear equation gives volumetric wear: $$ Q = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H} $$ For a steel shaft on bronze with **parallel lay**: $$ K_{\parallel} \approx 5 \times 10^{-5} $$ For **perpendicular lay**: $$ K_{\perp} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-5} $$ The wear ratio is therefore: $$ \frac{Q_{\parallel}}{Q_{\perp}} = \frac{K_{\parallel}}{K_{\perp}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 3.33 $$ So the parallel-lay journal wears **3.3 times faster** than the perpendicular-lay configuration. This explains the observed failure at 500 hours — if the lay had been correct, the bearing would have lasted approximately **1,650 hours**. **Recommended Corrected Specification:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Lay symbol: } \perp \text{ (perpendicular to shaft axis)} \\ &\text{Ra value: } 0.8 \, \mu m \\ &\text{Sampling length (lr): For } 0.4 \leq Ra < 4.0 \, \mu m, \text{ use } lr = 0.8 \text{ mm} \\ &\text{Evaluation length: } ln = 5 \times lr = 4.0 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Recommended Production Process:** **Cylindrical grinding with fine cross-feed and minimal longitudinal traverse**. The cross-feed produces circumferential lay (⊥) on the journal. A finishing pass with a fine grit (e.g., 400–600 grit aluminum oxide) will achieve $Ra = 0.8 \, \mu m$. **Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for failure diagnosis, 2 marks for Archard calculation, 2 marks for corrected specification with sampling length selection, 1 mark for recommended process. --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Alternative]** **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** A component has a sealing surface that mates with a rubber O-ring. Compare and contrast the suitability of (i) lay parallel (=) to the sealing axis, and (ii) lay crossed (X) at 45° for this application. Which would you recommend and why? **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the concept of "default lay" in ISO 1302.
