The AASHO standard defines a horizontal band of width $\pm 5$ mm around the centreline of the recorded profilogram (in the original road-elevation scale, *not* the amplified paper scale). Any portion of the trace that stays within this band for a horizontal distance $\ge \Delta x$ (typically 1.5 m in the US practice, or 50 mm in the SI version) is **"blanked out"** — it is considered noise and is not counted as roughness.

**Step 2 — Identify the "Counts":**
The remaining portions of the trace that *cross* the blanking band are called **"counts"**. For each count, a **peak-to-valley height** $h_i$ is measured as the algebraic difference between the highest point above the centreline and the lowest point below the centreline in that count.

**Step 3 — Sum the Peak-to-Valley Heights:**
Let there be $N$ counts in a section of length $L$. Then the Profile Index is:

$$\boxed{\,PI = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{i=1}^{N} h_i\,}$$

**Worked KTU-Style Numerical Example:**

> A 200 m stretch of newly laid bituminous road is profiled with a profilograph. The recorded counts gave the following peak-to-valley heights (already converted to road-scale, in mm): 8, 12, 6, 10, 14, 5, 9, 11, 7, 8.
> **Find the Profile Index (PI) in mm/km.**

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Sum of peak-to-valley heights:

$$\sum h_i = 8 + 12 + 6 + 10 + 14 + 5 + 9 + 11 + 7 + 8 = 90 \; \text{mm}$$

Step 2 — Length of test section:

$$L = 200 \; \text{m} = 0.200 \; \text{km}$$

Step 3 — Apply the formula:

$$PI = \frac{90 \; \text{mm}}{0.200 \; \text{km}}$$

$$\boxed{\,PI = 450 \; \text{mm/km}\,}$$

Step 4 — Compare with typical AASHTO acceptance limits:
- Excellent: PI < 250 mm/km
- Good: 250–500 mm/km
- Fair: 500–750 mm/km
- Poor: > 750 mm/km

**Conclusion:** The road section, with PI = 450 mm/km, falls in the **"Good"** category and is acceptable.

## 3.4 Conversion Between Half-Car PI and International Roughness Index (IRI)

The IRI is the modern, vehicle-independent roughness measure (m/km). For a half-car simulation at the standard 80 km/h, an empirical linear relation is used:

$$IRI = a \cdot PI_{\text{half-car}} + b$$

where $a$ and $b$ are regression constants from ASTM E1926 (typical values: $a \approx 1.40$, $b \approx -0.10$ for PI in mm/km, IRI in m/km).

A simpler often-quoted rough conversion is:

$$PI \; (\text{in/mile}) \;\approx\; 1.27 \times IRI \; (\text{m/km})$$

> [!NOTE]
> Always quote the **units explicitly** in KTU answers; examiners allot marks for unit discipline.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mechanical Profilograph

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **signal flow** through the major sub-systems of a mechanical profilograph, and is the figure students should reproduce in their answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Road Surface Input] --> B1[Reference Wheel L]
    A --> B2[Reference Wheel R]
    A --> C[Sensing Wheel Center]
    B1 --> D[Rigid Truss Frame Datum]
    B2 --> D
    C --> E[Vertical Displacement delta]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Lever Amplifier A=b over a]
    F --> G[Cam Follower Linkage]
    G --> H[Recording Stylus and Pen]
    I[Odometer Wheel 5th Wheel] --> J[Paper Drive Drum]
    J --> H
    H --> K[Profilogram Paper Strip]
    K --> L[Profile Index PI Calculation via Blanking Band]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of an Electronic / Modern Profilograph

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Sensing Wheel Contact] --> S2[LVDT or Laser Sensor]
    S2 --> S3[Signal Conditioner and Amplifier]
    S3 --> S4[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    S4 --> S5[Microprocessor and DSP Unit]
    S5 --> S6[Onboard LCD Display and Keyboard]
    S5 --> S7[Solid State Memory Storage]
    S5 --> S8[PC Interface RS232 or USB]
    S9[Odometer / Encoder Wheel] --> S5
    S10[Inertial Reference Accelerometer] --> S5
    S5 --> S11[Software Post Processing IRI and PI]
```

## 4.3 Functional Roles of Each Block (KTU Exam Explanation)

| Block Symbol | Component | Function in Plain English |
|---|---|---|
| A | Road Surface | Source of physical roughness signal |
| B1, B2 | Reference Wheels | Define a slowly-varying, averaged datum line |
| C | Sensing Wheel | Faithfully follows local surface irregularities |
| D | Rigid Truss | Provides stiffness so that frame does not flex |
| E | Displacement $\delta$ | Raw signal = sensing – datum |
| F | Lever Amplifier | Magnifies millimetre-level signal to centimetre-level for recording |
| G | Cam–follower | Converts vertical displacement to horizontal pen motion (with additional gain) |
| H | Pen and Stylus | Writes the wavy trace on the moving paper |
| I, J | Odometer + Drum | Synchronises paper feed with road distance |
| K | Profilogram | The final graphical record |
| L | PI Analysis | Manual or digital blanking-band computation |
| S2 | LVDT/Laser | Modern, non-contact or precision-contact transducer |
| S3 | Signal Conditioner | Filtering, amplification, linearisation |
| S4 | ADC | Converts analogue to digital samples |
| S5 | DSP/μP | Computes PI, IRI in real time |
| S10 | Accelerometer | Provides inertial reference (for inertial profilometers) |

> [!NOTE]
> The two diagrams above are drawn at **block-level architecture** as required by the system rule on physical drawings. They are sufficient for full KTU exam marks when accompanied by a labelled free-body sketch of the truss.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 (3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define the term "Profilograph" and state its primary engineering application. Also define the term "Profile Index".**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Profilograph** – A precision contact-type mechanical instrument used to record the longitudinal surface profile (roughness/unevenness) of a road, runway, or floor, producing a graphical trace called a *profilogram*. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Primary Application** – Acceptance testing and routine monitoring of pavement surface roughness in highway and airport engineering; the recorded trace is used to decide whether a pavement requires resurfacing. **(1 mark)**
3. **Profile Index (PI)** – A single quantitative roughness parameter derived from the profilogram, defined as the sum of peak-to-valley heights of all counts in a section, divided by the length of the section. Units: **mm/km** (SI) or **inches/mile** (US). **(0.5 marks)**

> Total = 3 marks.

### Question A.2 (3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Distinguish between the "Reference Wheel" and the "Sensing Wheel" of a profilograph.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Reference Wheel | Sensing Wheel |
|---|---|---|
| Position | Outer (L and R ends) | Centre of frame |
| Diameter | Larger (≈ 380 mm) | Smaller (≈ 250 mm) |
| Function | Establishes a smoothed datum line by averaging short-wavelength roughness | Tracks every local bump/depression faithfully |
| Sensitivity | Low (averaging) | High (responsive) |

> Award 0.75 mark per correct distinguishing row. 4 rows × 0.75 = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule Reminder:** Answer **ONE** full question (either Q-A or Q-B). Each question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B

#### Q-A.(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a mechanical profilograph. Show the role of the reference wheels, sensing wheel, and lever amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(1) Set-up (1.5 marks):** A mechanical profilograph consists of a rigid **trussed beam frame** supported on **two reference wheels** at the ends and a **sensing (test) wheel** at the centre. A **fifth wheel (odometer)** is provided to drive a paper roll for recording. The frame is towed at a constant walking speed of 3–5 km/h along the road.

**(2) Datum establishment (1.5 marks):** The two outer reference wheels, being larger in diameter and connected by a rigid frame, average out short-wavelength roughness and define a slowly-varying straight datum line given by $y_D = (y_L + y_R)/2$.

**(3) Signal generation (1.5 marks):** The smaller centre sensing wheel deflects vertically by an amount $\delta = y_S - y_D$ as it rolls over each local bump or depression. This $\delta$ is of the order of 1–25 mm for a 3 m wheelbase.

**(4) Amplification (1 mark):** The displacement $\delta$ is transmitted via a **Class-1 lever** of ratio $A = b/a \approx 50$ and a **cam-follower** linkage giving an additional gain, so total vertical magnification reaches 75×–100×.

**(5) Recording (1 mark):** The amplified displacement drives a **stylus** that draws a continuous trace on a moving paper strip. The paper feed is synchronised to the odometer wheel so the horizontal axis is true distance. The resulting curve is the **profilogram**.

**(6) Block diagram (0.5 marks):** A clean Mermaid / sketch showing: Road → Sensing Wheel → Lever Amp → Pen → Paper ← Odometer. (Examiner's note: even a hand-drawn block diagram is acceptable.)

---

#### Q-A.(b) Explain the procedure for calculating the Profile Index (PI) from a profilogram using the blanking-band method. A 250 m section of road yields the following peak-to-valley heights (in mm) at successive counts: 7, 11, 5, 9, 13, 6, 8, 12, 10, 9. Calculate the PI in mm/km and comment on the pavement condition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Concept of the Blanking Band (1 mark):**
A horizontal band of width $\pm 5$ mm (in road scale) is drawn about the centreline of the recorded profilogram. Portions of the trace remaining within this band for a horizontal length $\ge 1.5$ m are blanked out (ignored as noise). Remaining crossings are called *counts*.

**Step 2 — Measurement of $h_i$ (1 mark):**
For each count, the peak-to-valley height $h_i$ is the algebraic sum of the maximum elevation above the centreline and the maximum depression below the centreline within that count.

**Step 3 — Data tabulation (1 mark):**
Ten counts are observed, with $h_i$ values: 7, 11, 5, 9, 13, 6, 8, 12, 10, 9 mm.

**Step 4 — Sum the peak-to-valley heights (1 mark):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{10} h_i = 7+11+5+9+13+6+8+12+10+9 = 90 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Convert length to km (1 mark):**

$$L = 250 \;\text{m} = 0.250 \;\text{km}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the formula (1 mark):**

$$PI = \frac{90 \;\text{mm}}{0.250 \;\text{km}} = 360 \;\text{mm/km}$$

**Step 7 — Comment (1 mark):**
Standard AASHTO categories: Excellent < 250; Good 250–500; Fair 500–750; Poor > 750 mm/km.
A PI of **360 mm/km** places the pavement in the **"Good"** category. The road is acceptable and no immediate resurfacing is required, though routine monitoring should continue.

> **[Examiner's note]:** Many students forget to convert the length to km. Always write units explicitly. Marks are deducted for unit mismatch.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Q-B.(a) With the aid of a labelled sketch, describe the main components of a mechanical profilograph. Explain the role of the odometer (fifth) wheel and the cam-follower linkage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(1) Truss frame (1.5 marks):** A light but rigid aluminium-alloy truss that holds the reference and sensing wheels in a fixed geometry. It must be torsionally stiff so that the only vertical relative motion between wheels is due to surface roughness.

**(2) Reference wheels (1 mark):** Two large-diameter (≈ 380 mm) pneumatic tyres at the ends; they average out short-wavelength roughness and define the datum.

**(3) Sensing wheel (1 mark):** A smaller (≈ 250 mm) wheel at the mid-span; its vertical motion faithfully tracks local surface undulations.

**(4) Odometer (fifth) wheel (1.5 marks):** Mounted off the truss in line with the road wheel, it rotates purely with the longitudinal travel. Each revolution corresponds to a known arc-length ($\pi d$, with $d$ = effective diameter). Via a gear train it drives the **paper-feed drum** so the horizontal axis of the profilogram is calibrated to true road distance. Without it, horizontal scale is meaningless.

**(5) Cam-follower linkage (1.5 marks):** Converts the small vertical motion of the sensing wheel into a larger horizontal motion of the pen. The cam profile is designed to give a non-linear gain that compensates for the lever's pivot geometry, achieving an overall vertical exaggeration of 75×–100×. It also reverses the sense of motion so that an "up" bump on the road plots as an "up" deflection on paper.

**(6) Recording drum, pen, paper, and damping dashpot (0.5 marks):** These complete the system. The dashpot critically damps high-frequency chatter so the stylus does not scribble.

---

#### Q-B.(b) Compare the mechanical profilograph with the modern electronic (inertial) profilograph. List at least four advantages of the electronic system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | Mechanical Profilograph | Electronic / Inertial Profilograph |
|---|---|---|
| Transducer | Mechanical lever + stylus | LVDT, laser, or accelerometer |
| Recording medium | Paper strip (manual analysis) | Digital memory; on-the-fly analysis |
| Test speed | Walking, 3–5 km/h | High speed, 50–90 km/h |
| Operator skill | High (manual paper handling, blanking) | Low (computerised) |
| Vertical resolution | ≈ 1 mm | ≈ 0.1 mm or better |
| Data storage | Single paper roll | Hundreds of km in solid-state memory |
| Cost | Low initial; high labour | High initial; low operating cost |
| Output | Profilogram (paper) | Profilogram, IRI, PI, RN, plotted on screen |

**Advantages of the Electronic System (3.5 marks — list any four, 0.9 mark each):**
1. **Higher test speed** (50–90 km/h) – traffic does not need to be stopped, so safety and convenience improve.
2. **Real-time computation** of multiple roughness indices (PI, IRI, RN, half-car) – no tedious manual blanking-band analysis.
3. **Non-contact sensing** (laser) – eliminates stylus wear and reduces calibration drift.
4. **Inertial reference** (accelerometer) – removes the influence of the host vehicle's body motion, giving a true profile even on rough terrain.
5. **Digital data storage & GIS integration** – facilitates network-level pavement management systems (PMS).
6. **Repeatability** – no operator-induced variability; suitable for legal acceptance tests.
7. **Lower long-term operating cost** despite higher initial outlay.

> **[Examiner's note]:** Do NOT write "electronic is *always* better". Always qualify the comparison. Mechanical profilographs are still used on small rural projects and for academic demonstration.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Profilograph questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert m to km** when applying the PI formula. The unit of PI is **mm/km**, not mm/m. One mark is routinely deducted.
> 2. **Confusing reference wheel with sensing wheel** – remember: **reference = ends, larger; sensing = centre, smaller**.
> 3. **Plotting the profilogram in the wrong sense** – an "up" bump on the road must be an "up" deflection on the paper (depending on the lever arrangement). Show the cam-follower sense explicitly.
> 4. **Omitting units** in the final numerical answer.
> 5. **Writing "profilometer" and "profilograph" interchangeably** – in strict KTU terminology, the **profilograph** records a *continuous trace*; the **profilometer** is often a *point-reading* instrument. (Many texts are sloppy here.)
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram** – even if the question does not explicitly ask for a diagram, a clean Mermaid / sketch of the signal path earns 1–2 easy marks.
> 7. **Not stating the test speed** when discussing electronic profilographs – examiners expect 50–90 km/h.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **profilograph** is a contact-type on-site surface-roughness recorder producing a **profilogram**.
- ✅ Major parts: **truss frame, two reference wheels, one sensing wheel, odometer wheel, lever amplifier, cam-follower, recording stylus, paper drum**.
- ✅ **Reference wheel diameter > sensing wheel diameter** (always).
- ✅ **Wheelbase B** (≈ 3 m) determines the longest wavelength that is *included* in the measured roughness; longer waves are rejected by the datum-averaging.
- ✅ **Vertical exaggeration** = 75× to 100× (paper scale vs road scale).
- ✅ **Profile Index (PI)** is the quantitative output, with units **mm/km** (SI) or **inches/mile** (US).
- ✅ The **AASHO blanking band** is $\pm 5$ mm in road-elevation units; counts are the crossings of this band; $h_i$ are peak-to-valley heights.
- ✅ **Formula:** $PI = \dfrac{\sum h_i}{L}$ with $L$ in **km** (for SI).
- ✅ **IRI** is the modern, vehicle-independent roughness index (m/km); relates to PI through an empirical linear regression.
- ✅ **Mechanical** = cheap, slow, manual; **Electronic / Inertial** = expensive, fast, automated, real-time.
- ✅ Applications: highways, airport runways, industrial floors, bridge decks, racetracks.
- ✅ **ASTM E867** is the standard roughness-terminology reference; **AASHTO PP 37** is the modern test method.
- ✅ Always mention **units** and **test speed** in KTU answers.
- ✅ In numerical problems, write the full summation step-by-step; do not skip intermediate algebra.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tomlinson Surface Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tomlinson Surface Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — TOMLINSON SURFACE METER
## SECTION 1 · Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)**
> The **Tomlinson Surface Meter** is a *mechanical longitudinal profilometer* used to measure the **unevenness (irregularity) of road and runway surfaces** in terms of the vertical deviation of the surface profile from a straight-edge reference. It is a beam-type instrument, **3 metres long**, pivoted at its centre, and traverses the road surface on three supports to record cumulative vertical undulations per unit length of pavement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic matters in Mechanical Instrumentation (PEICT416):**
> The Tomlinson Surface Meter is a textbook example of how **purely mechanical, displacement-amplifying instruments** can be used for quantitative surface profile measurement without any electronics. It falls under the broader category of *surface texture / roughness measurement instruments* taught in Module 4 (Surface Metrology and Field Profilometers).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Walking Seesaw"
Imagine a child sitting on the exact centre of a long plank (a seesaw) that is supported on three points: the centre and both ends. Now, ask the child to slide the plank along a *bumpy* road. As the plank moves:

1. If the **left end** of the plank hits a bump, that end goes **up**, the **right end** goes **down**, and the centre remains fixed.
2. The plank **rotates (tilts)** about the central pivot.
3. The **amount of tilt** is directly proportional to the **height of the bump** relative to the central reference.

The Tomlinson Surface Meter works on **exactly this principle**, except that it measures the tilt mechanically using a sensitive dial gauge and converts it into a numerical "unevenness" value. In essence, it tells the engineer: *"How much is this road deviating from a perfectly flat 3-metre straight edge?"*

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Standard beam length, L** = **3.0 m** (some variants use 3.66 m / 12 ft)
- **Standard speed of traverse** = Walking speed (≈ 1.0 to 1.5 km/h); motorised versions may run faster
- **Number of road wheels/supports** = **3** (two end wheels + one central pivot wheel)
- **Dial gauge least count** = Typically **0.01 mm** or **0.05 mm**
- **Standard reference for unevenness in India** = **IRC:SP:16 / IS:14673** — Unevenness reported in **mm per 3 m**
- **Surface Irregularity Index (SII)** expressed in **mm/km** for long stretches of pavement

### Typical Applications
- Highway pavement roughness surveys
- Airport runway and taxiway profile evaluation
- Acceptance testing of newly constructed bituminous/concrete surfaces
- Periodic maintenance evaluation of in-service roads
- Comparative rating of ride quality

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 · Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Features (Component Breakdown)
The instrument is fabricated as a rigid, light-alloy beam assembly, with the following key components:

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|------:|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Main beam (3 m long)** | Acts as the reference straight edge; aluminium alloy for light weight and high stiffness |
| 2 | **Central pivot wheel (middle support)** | The fixed point of rotation; the beam tilts about this point |
| 3 | **Two end wheels (left & right supports)** | Trail along the road surface; their vertical positions follow the pavement profile |
| 4 | **Dial gauge (mechanical indicator)** | Mounted near one end; its plunger is spring-loaded against a fixed reference on the beam body |
| 5 | **Counterweight / balancing mass** | Keeps the beam stable in horizontal position when on a level surface |
| 6 | **Handle** | For the operator to push the instrument manually |
| 7 | **Recording drum / chart (optional)** | For continuous recording of profile on a moving paper strip |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Pivot-at-Centre" Geometry is the key insight.** Because the pivot lies exactly at the mid-span, the two end wheels move in **opposite directions** (one up, one down) by **equal magnitude** when the surface is locally tilted. This symmetric arrangement **doubles the effective sensitivity** of the instrument.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic
1. The instrument is placed on the road with all three wheels in contact with the surface.
2. On a **perfectly level stretch**, the beam is horizontal, and the dial gauge reads **zero**.
3. As the operator pushes the instrument forward, the end wheels trace the longitudinal profile.
4. Whenever a **bump** is encountered, the front wheel rises; the beam rotates about the central pivot, and the rear wheel drops.
5. The **vertical displacement of the beam end (relative to the central pivot)** is transmitted to the **plunger of the dial gauge**.
6. The dial gauge converts this displacement into a magnified mechanical reading.
7. The operator (or an automatic integrator) **sums up the absolute vertical movements** over the surveyed length.
8. The **Surface Irregularity Index (SII)** is calculated as:
$$ \text{SII} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Unevenness (sum of absolute vertical deviations)}}{\text{Length of road surveyed (km)}} $$
   The unit is **mm / km**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Standard Unit |
|--:|----------|:------:|----------------------|:-------------:|
| 1 | Beam length (between end supports) | $L$ | $L = 3.0$ m (standard) | m |
| 2 | Distance from pivot to each end | $L/2$ | $1.5$ m for a 3 m beam | m |
| 3 | Vertical displacement of one end (dial reading) | $d$ | Direct dial gauge reading | mm |
| 4 | Vertical displacement of the other end | $d$ | Equal and opposite (pivot-at-centre geometry) | mm |
| 5 | **Unevenness between the two end points (per reading)** | $U$ | $U = 2d$ | mm |
| 6 | Angular tilt of the beam | $\theta$ | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{2d}{L}$ | radians / degrees |
| 7 | Slope at the surface (per 3 m) | $s$ | $s = \dfrac{U}{L} = \dfrac{2d}{L}$ | mm/m |
| 8 | Surface Irregularity Index | $\text{SII}$ | $\text{SII} = \dfrac{\sum \vert U_{i} \vert}{L_{\text{total}}(\text{in km})}$ | mm/km |
| 9 | Per-cycle summation (over $N$ readings) | $\sum U$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert 2d_{i} \vert$ | mm |
| 10 | Roughness Quality Threshold (good pavement) | — | $\text{SII} \le 2000 \text{ mm/km}$ | mm/km |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** A freshly constructed bituminous surface should ideally have **SII ≤ 1500 mm/km** for high-speed corridors and **≤ 2000 mm/km** for ordinary highways. Higher values indicate poor ride quality, structural weakness, or construction defect. The Tomlinson Meter, despite being purely mechanical, is still the **reference field instrument** in many developing-economy pavement evaluation programs.

### 2.4 Why the Centre-Pivot Design?
- A single, single-span straight edge would only measure **one end** at a time, requiring a perfectly flat reference.
- The **3-point support with central pivot** makes the instrument **self-referencing**: the centre of the road profile is taken as the local datum, so absolute level of the road is irrelevant.
- Sensitivity is **doubled** because both ends contribute to the dial reading (in opposite phase).
- The instrument becomes **insensitive to the absolute slope** of the road (gradients do not affect the reading), which is critical when surveying hilly terrain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 · Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Relationship Between Dial Reading and Unevenness

**Statement to be proved:** *The unevenness between the two end supports of a 3 m Tomlinson beam is exactly twice the dial gauge reading.*

**Geometric Setup (textual description):**
Consider the beam ABC with:
- A = left end wheel
- B = central pivot (mid-point)
- C = right end wheel
- Length AB = Length BC = $L/2$
- Total length AC = $L$

Let the left end A rise by a vertical distance $d$ above the pivot B. Because the beam is rigid and pivots about B, the right end C will drop by the *same* distance $d$ below B (provided $L/2$ is the same on both sides).

**Mathematical Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vertical rise of point A relative to pivot B} & \;=\; d \\[4pt]
\text{Vertical drop of point C relative to pivot B} & \;=\; d \quad \text{(by symmetry of pivot-at-centre)} \\[4pt]
\text{Total relative vertical separation between A and C} & \;=\; (\text{rise of A}) + (\text{depression of C}) \\[4pt]
& \;=\; d + d \;=\; 2d \\[4pt]
\therefore U_{\text{per reading}} & \;=\; 2d \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Angular Tilt from Dial Reading

The angle $\theta$ made by the beam with the horizontal is given by elementary trigonometry on the half-beam triangle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \theta & \;=\; \frac{\text{Opposite side (vertical rise of A)}}{\text{Adjacent side (horizontal distance AB)}} \\[4pt]
\tan \theta & \;=\; \frac{d}{L/2} \;=\; \frac{2d}{L} \\[4pt]
\theta & \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2d}{L}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For the standard 3 m beam, $L = 3000$ mm, so:

$$
\tan \theta = \frac{2d}{3000} = \frac{d}{1500}
$$

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Typical Style)

**Problem:** A Tomlinson Surface Meter (beam length = 3 m) is traversed over a 1 km stretch of newly laid bituminous road. The operator records the following dial readings (in mm) at every 3 m interval:

$$
+4,\; -3,\; +5,\; -2,\; +6,\; -4,\; +3,\; -5,\; +2,\; -1
$$

Assume the values repeat identically for the full 1 km (i.e., 1000 m / 3 m ≈ 333 complete cycles, treated as 333 identical cycles for this problem).

**Find:**
- (a) The unevenness per reading.
- (b) The angular tilt for the +6 mm reading.
- (c) The Surface Irregularity Index (SII) in mm/km.

**Solution:**

**(a) Unevenness per reading**

$$
U_i = 2 d_i
$$

Absolute values of the dial readings:
$$
4,\; 3,\; 5,\; 2,\; 6,\; 4,\; 3,\; 5,\; 2,\; 1 \quad \text{(mm)}
$$

Sum of absolute dial readings for one cycle (10 readings):
$$
\sum \vert d_i \vert = 4+3+5+2+6+4+3+5+2+1 = 35 \text{ mm}
$$

Sum of unevenness values per cycle:
$$
\sum \vert U_i \vert = 2 \times 35 = 70 \text{ mm per 30 m}
$$

**(b) Angular tilt for $d = +6$ mm**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \theta & = \frac{2d}{L} = \frac{2 \times 6}{3000} = \frac{12}{3000} = 0.004 \\[4pt]
\theta & = \tan^{-1}(0.004) \approx 0.229^\circ \approx 13.16 \text{ minutes of arc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Surface Irregularity Index**

For the full 1 km, we extrapolate the 70 mm per 30 m pattern:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SII} & = \frac{\sum \vert U_i \vert_{\text{per km}}}{1 \text{ km}} \\[4pt]
\sum \vert U_i \vert_{\text{per 30 m}} & = 70 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\sum \vert U_i \vert_{\text{per 1000 m}} & = 70 \times \frac{1000}{30} = 2333.33 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{SII} & = \frac{2333.33 \text{ mm}}{1 \text{ km}} = 2333.33 \text{ mm/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result interpretation:** SII = 2333 mm/km is **marginally above the 2000 mm/km threshold** for a "good" pavement. This indicates the road has minor surface defects and the construction quality is **borderline acceptable** for a low-volume rural road, but **unacceptable** for a national highway.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Tomlinson vs. Straight Edge vs. Profilometer

| Parameter | Tomlinson Surface Meter | 3 m Straight Edge | Modern Profilometer (Bump Integrator) |
|-----------|:-----------------------:|:-----------------:|:------------------------------------:|
| Output type | Cumulative unevenness (mm/km) | Local depression (mm) | Continuous profile (mm vs. distance) |
| Speed | Walking pace | Stationary | 20–80 km/h (vehicle-mounted) |
| Electronics required | **None (purely mechanical)** | None | Ultrasonic / Laser / Accelerometer |
| Sensitivity | High (centre-pivot doubled) | Low | Very high |
| Cost | Low | Very low | Very high |
| Operator skill | Moderate | Low | High |
| Best use | Routine field surveys, KTU lab | Spot checks | High-speed corridor audits |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 · Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **functional architecture** of the Tomlinson Surface Meter — i.e., how the mechanical energy and motion of the road surface are transformed, stage by stage, into a numerical unevenness reading.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Road Surface Profile] -->|contact| B[End Wheel Left A]
    A -->|contact| C[Central Pivot B]
    A -->|contact| D[End Wheel Right C]

    B -->|vertical rise d| E[Rigid Beam Tilts about Pivot B]
    D -->|vertical drop d| E
    C -->|fixed reference| E

    E -->|angular rotation theta| F[Dial Gauge Plunger Displaces]

    F -->|mechanical magnification| G[Dial Gauge Indicator Scale]

    G -->|read by operator| H[Dial Reading d in mm]

    H -->|doubled| I[Unevenness U = 2d per reading]

    I -->|summed over distance| J[Cumulative Unevenness mm]

    J -->|divided by length km| K[Surface Irregularity Index SII mm per km]

    style A fill:#ffe7c2,stroke:#cc6600
    style E fill:#c2e6ff,stroke:#003366
    style G fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#1f6f1f
    style K fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Physical Layout of the Beam

A simplified top-view and side-view layout of the instrument (rendered as a text schematic since complex 3D geometry is impractical in plain Mermaid):

```
   SIDE VIEW (looking along the road):

        d (rise)                                     d (drop)
          ↑                                              ↓
   ___A___/__________________________B__________________________\___C___
  (wheel)                          (pivot)                       (wheel)
   |                                |                              |
   |  ←——— L/2 = 1.5 m ———→|        |←——— L/2 = 1.5 m ———→|       |
   |←——————————————— L = 3.0 m total ————————————————→|              |
                                                                       ___
   |                                            [Dial Gauge]————|   __|
   |                                            [Operator reads d]   |__|
```

**Key to schematic:**
- **A** = Left end wheel — follows the surface profile from the left side
- **B** = Central pivot — fixed rotation axis (the local datum)
- **C** = Right end wheel — follows the surface profile from the right side
- **L = 3 m** = distance AC between the two end supports
- **d** = vertical displacement of either end relative to pivot B
- **Dial Gauge** = mounted near one end, plunger resting on a fixed reference

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Working Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Place instrument on road] --> S2[Step 2: Zero the dial gauge on level surface]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Push instrument forward at walking speed]
    S3 --> S4{End wheel encounters bump or dip?}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Step 4a: Beam tilts about central pivot]
    S5 --> S6[Step 4b: Dial plunger deflects by d]
    S6 --> S7[Step 4c: Operator records dial reading]
    S4 -->|No| S8[Step 4: Beam remains horizontal, reading = 0]
    S7 --> S9[Step 5: Sum absolute readings over 3 m intervals]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Step 6: Compute U = 2d for each interval]
    S10 --> S11[Step 7: Compute SII = total U divided by length in km]
    S11 --> S12[Step 8: Compare SII with IRC threshold for road quality]

    style S5 fill:#cfe8ff
    style S11 fill:#ffe5b4
    style S12 fill:#d4f4d4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 · KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a Tomlinson Surface Meter and state its primary use.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Tomlinson Surface Meter is a **mechanical, 3-metre long straight-edge type instrument** with a beam pivoted at its centre and resting on the road at three points (two end wheels and a central pivot wheel). It is used to **measure the longitudinal surface unevenness (roughness) of road and runway pavements**. The reading is summed over a surveyed length and reported as the **Surface Irregularity Index (SII) in mm/km**, which is an indicator of pavement ride quality and construction quality.

> **Marking key (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition · 1 mark for geometry (3 m pivoted beam) · 1 mark for use + unit (SII in mm/km).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Why is the pivot of a Tomlinson Surface Meter placed exactly at the centre of the beam? Explain in two sentences.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The pivot is placed at the centre so that the beam becomes **self-referencing** (the local road profile datum is the mid-point, removing any effect of absolute road gradient). Also, due to this symmetric placement, **both end wheels contribute equally and in opposite phase**, effectively **doubling the sensitivity** of the dial gauge reading.

> **Marking key:** 1 mark for self-referencing/datum removal · 1 mark for doubled sensitivity · 1 mark for opposite-phase motion.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

(a) *With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Tomlinson Surface Meter.* (7 marks)

(b) *A Tomlinson Surface Meter with a 3 m beam is run over a 500 m stretch of road. The recorded absolute dial readings (in mm) at every 3 m interval are: 5, 4, 6, 3, 5, 4, 2, 5, 3, 4. The pattern repeats 16 times to cover the 500 m. Calculate the Surface Irregularity Index. Comment on the pavement quality.* (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
- **Beam:** Light-alloy rigid beam, 3 m long, of high flexural stiffness to prevent sagging (1 mark).
- **Three supports:** Two end wheels (rubber-tyred to prevent road damage) and one central pivot wheel (1 mark).
- **Pivot:** Located exactly at the mid-span of the beam, allowing free rotation in the vertical plane (1 mark).
- **Dial gauge:** Mechanical type, least count 0.01 mm, mounted near one end with its plunger resting on a fixed reference on the beam body (0.5 mark).

**Working (3.5 marks):**
- The instrument is placed on the road and zeroed on a flat stretch (0.5 mark).
- It is pushed forward at walking speed along the longitudinal direction (0.5 mark).
- When the surface has a local bump, one end wheel rises, the beam tilts about the central pivot, and the other end wheel drops by the same amount (1 mark).
- The vertical displacement $d$ of the beam end is picked up by the dial gauge plunger and indicated on the dial (1 mark).
- The operator records the reading and continues; the cumulative sum of $2d$ over the surveyed length gives the SII in mm/km (1 mark).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

Step 1 — Sum of absolute dial readings for ONE cycle of 10 readings:

$$
\sum \vert d_i \vert = 5+4+6+3+5+4+2+5+3+4 = 41 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 2 — Sum of unevenness per cycle:

$$
\sum \vert U_i \vert_{\text{per 30 m}} = 2 \times 41 = 82 \text{ mm per 30 m}
$$

Step 3 — Total length surveyed = 500 m. Number of cycles = 500 / 30 ≈ 16.67 (treat as 16 complete cycles per problem statement, ignoring fractional remainder, since 30 m × 16 = 480 m; the remaining 20 m is also surveyed):

$$
\sum \vert U_i \vert_{\text{per 500 m}} = 82 \times 16 + \text{partial cycle contribution}
$$

For simplicity, as stated in the problem, the pattern repeats **exactly 16 times** for 500 m:

$$
\sum \vert U_i \vert_{\text{per 500 m}} = 82 \times 16 = 1312 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 4 — Surface Irregularity Index:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SII} & = \frac{1312 \text{ mm}}{0.5 \text{ km}} \\[4pt]
& = 2624 \text{ mm/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — **Comment on pavement quality:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since SII = 2624 mm/km **exceeds the 2000 mm/km threshold** for a "good" pavement (IRC standards), this road is **not acceptable for a high-speed corridor** and requires **corrective surface treatment** (e.g., milling and re-laying of bituminous wearing course) before being opened to heavy traffic.

> **Marking key (7 marks):** 1 mark for cycle summation · 1 mark for doubling · 1 mark for multiplication by 16 · 1 mark for correct unit conversion (per km) · 1 mark for final SII value · 1 mark for comparison with threshold · 1 mark for engineering comment.

---

#### **Question B — Internal Choice (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary Exam 2023]**

(a) *Explain the principle of operation of a Tomlinson Surface Meter. Derive the expression relating dial gauge reading to the surface unevenness.* (7 marks)

(b) *Compare the Tomlinson Surface Meter with a modern Bump Integrator / Profilometer. List two advantages and two limitations of the Tomlinson Meter.* (7 marks)

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Part (a) — Principle and Derivation (7 marks)**
- Principle: The beam pivots about its central point; vertical displacements at the two ends are equal and opposite, doubling the sensitivity. (2 marks)
- Diagram and identification of L, d, U. (2 marks)
- Derivation $U = 2d$ with the symmetric pivot geometry. (2 marks)
- Statement of the final formula and its units. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Comparison and Limitations (7 marks)**

| Tomlinson Surface Meter | Bump Integrator / Profilometer |
|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Purely mechanical, no power | Electronic, vehicle-mounted |
| Walking speed, low throughput | 30–80 km/h, high throughput |
| Discrete 3 m sampling | Continuous, 100 mm or finer sampling |
| Low cost, robust | High cost, sensitive to vibration/calibration |

**Advantages of Tomlinson Meter (2 marks):**
1. No external power or electronics required — ideal for **rural surveys**.
2. **Low cost** and easy to maintain / repair in the field.

**Limitations of Tomlinson Meter (2 marks):**
1. **Slow and labour-intensive** — only practical for short stretches or spot checks.
2. Cannot capture **high-frequency short-wavelength roughness** (e.g., texture-level irregularities) — limited to wavelengths ≥ 3 m.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Tomlinson remains a **reference instrument** for calibration and field audits, but for high-speed network-level surveys, modern profilometers are preferred.

> **Marking key (7 marks):** 2 marks for derivation · 2 marks for comparison table · 1 mark for advantages · 1 mark for limitations · 1 mark for conclusion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to double the dial reading:** Many students write $U = d$ instead of $U = 2d$. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in any KTU numerical. Always remember the centre-pivot symmetry.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Reporting SII in "mm" instead of "mm/km" is a **1-mark deduction**. The divisor must always convert the surveyed length into **kilometres**.
> 3. **Confusing Tomlinson with a straight edge:** A straight edge has NO pivot and NO doubling. Mixing the two will cost the construction-diagram marks.
> 4. **Skipping the sign convention:** Unevenness is the **sum of absolute values**, not algebraic sum. Writing $\sum U_i$ (signed) instead of $\sum \vert U_i \vert$ gives a wrong SII.
> 5. **Not commenting on the engineering meaning:** KTU explicitly awards marks for **interpretation** of the SII value with respect to the IRC threshold. A bare number without a comment loses **1 mark**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Tomlinson Surface Meter = 3 m long beam, pivoted at the centre, 3-point road contact.**
- [x] **Primary use:** Measure **longitudinal unevenness of road/runway surfaces**, reported as **SII in mm/km**.
- [x] **Core formula (memorise):** $U = 2d$ where $d$ is the dial gauge reading.
- [x] **Angular tilt:** $\tan \theta = \dfrac{2d}{L}$; for $L = 3$ m, $\tan \theta = d/1500$.
- [x] **SII formula:** $\text{SII} = \dfrac{\sum \vert 2d_i \vert \text{ (in mm)}}{L_{\text{road}} \text{ (in km)}}$
- [x] **Threshold:** Good pavement ⇒ SII ≤ 2000 mm/km; Excellent ⇒ SII ≤ 1500 mm/km.
- [x] **Why centre pivot?** Self-referencing (gradient-immune) + **doubled sensitivity** (opposite-phase end motions).
- [x] **Sign convention:** Always use **absolute values** of dial readings when summing unevenness.
- [x] **Advantages:** No electronics, low cost, robust, ideal for field audits.
- [x] **Limitations:** Slow, manual, misses short-wavelength roughness, operator-dependent.
- [x] **Comparison:** Tomlinson (3 m, walking pace) ≪ Bump Integrator (vehicle-mounted, electronic) ≪ Laser Profilometer (high-speed, mm-resolution).
- [x] **Standard reference (India):** IRC:SP:16 / IS:14673 guidelines for pavement unevenness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Centre pivot doubles the reading — never forget the factor of 2, and always report unevenness in mm/km, not mm."* — A line worth 2 marks in every KTU board exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tracer Type Profilograph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tracer Type Profilograph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tracer Type Profilograph (Definition per ISO 3274 / ISO 4287 Standards)**
> A **Tracer Type Profilograph** — also widely known as a *Stylus Profilometer* or *Surface Profilograph* — is a **contact-type, non-optical instrument** used for the quantitative measurement of **surface texture and roughness**. It operates by drawing a sharp, diamond-tipped **stylus (tracer)** at a constant traversing speed across the workpiece surface. The vertical displacement of the stylus, which faithfully follows the micro-geometry of the surface (peaks and valleys), is transduced into a proportional electrical signal, filtered, amplified, and recorded to produce a **magnified 2-D profile trace** from which standardized roughness parameters (Ra, Rz, Rq, Rt, Rsk, Rku) are computed.

The instrument conforms to international standards:
- **ISO 3274** – Instrumentation and calibration
- **ISO 4287** – Terminology, definitions and parameters
- **ISO 4288** – Rules and procedures for measurement
- **ISO 13565** – For parameters related to bearing area
- **ANSI/ASME B46.1** – American standard for surface texture

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Vinyl Record Player" Concept**
> Imagine the diamond needle of a vintage **gramophone (vinyl record player)**. As the needle rides along the spiraling groove of a vinyl record, it bobs up and down thousands of times per second, faithfully tracing every microscopic undulation. A tracer type profilograph works on the *exact opposite principle*: instead of a needle **reading** grooves, a diamond stylus **writes** the surface texture as it traces across a metal (or any solid) workpiece. The up-and-down motion of the stylus is converted into an electrical signal that "draws" the surface profile on a chart or screen, much like a seismograph needle records an earthquake.

**Another everyday analogy:** Run your fingertip across an unpolished cast-iron pan. Your nerve endings detect peaks and valleys. The profilograph does the same job with **a diamond tip** (radius 2–10 μm) and **electronic nerve endings** (the transducer), giving you a numeric, repeatable, and objective measurement.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Specification | Typical Value | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Stylus tip radius | **2 μm, 5 μm, or 10 μm** | ISO 3274 |
| Stylus tip material | **Diamond (industrial grade)** | ISO 3274 |
| Static measuring force | **0.7 mN – 1.0 mN** | ISO 3274 |
| Traverse speed | **0.5 mm/s or 1.0 mm/s** | ISO 3274 |
| Cut-off wavelength (λc) | **0.08 / 0.25 / 0.8 / 2.5 / 8.0 mm** | ISO 4288 |
| Sampling length | **5 × λc** | ISO 4288 |
| Evaluation length | **≥ 7 × λc** | ISO 4288 |
| Vertical magnification | **×100 to ×100,000** | Manufacturer spec |
| Horizontal magnification | **×10 to ×1000** | Manufacturer spec |
| Vertical resolution | **0.001 μm (1 nm)** | Modern digital units |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Tracer Type Profilograph

A tracer type profilograph is a **multi-stage signal conditioning system**. The following bulleted logic breaks down each stage's role and the "why" behind it:

**Stage 1 — Tracer / Stylus Assembly**
- **Component:** A sharp diamond stylus (tip radius = 2 μm) attached to a cantilever arm, with a **skid** (reference shoe) sliding on the surface to provide a datum.
- **Why?** The skid filters out long-wavelength waviness, allowing the stylus to measure only short-wavelength roughness.
- **The "How":** As the skid glides, the stylus rises and falls according to the surface micro-geometry within the band of the skid.

**Stage 2 — Transducer (Displacement-to-Electrical Conversion)**
- **Component:** Commonly an **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)**, **Piezoelectric crystal**, or **Inductive pick-off**.
- **Why?** The mechanical displacement (μm or sub-μm scale) is too small to be measured directly; it must be transduced into a voltage.
- **The "How":** LVDT: the stylus drives a ferrite core inside a differential transformer; movement generates a differential voltage.

**Stage 3 — Amplifier**
- **Why?** The raw transducer signal is in the millivolt range — too weak for recording.
- **The "How":** A linear AC amplifier with high gain (60–100 dB) raises the signal to a recordable level.

**Stage 4 — Filter (Gaussian Filter per ISO 16610)**
- **Why?** Surface profiles contain roughness, waviness, and form error. Filters separate these into meaningful bands.
- **The "How":** A 2CR (2-pole Butterworth) or Gaussian filter with **cut-off wavelength λc** transmits the roughness band only.

**Stage 5 — Recorder / Display**
- **Why?** Provides a permanent, magnified visual profile.
- **The "How":** An analog **strip-chart recorder** plots stylus height (y) vs. traversing distance (x). Modern units use **digital displays** and PC interfaces.

**Stage 6 — Digital Processing (Modern Units)**
- **Why?** Direct numeric computation of Ra, Rz, Rq, Rt, Rsk, Rku, Rp, Rv, Rc, Rsm.
- **The "How":** ADC samples the filtered signal at high rate, integrates digitally per ISO 4287 definitions.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 ESE question on this topic.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ra** (Arithmetic Mean Roughness) | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | Average absolute deviation of profile from mean line | μm |
| 2 | **Rq** (Root-Mean-Square Roughness) | $R_q = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} y^2(x) \, dx}$ | RMS deviation from mean line | μm |
| 3 | **Rz** (Mean Peak-to-Valley Height) | $R_z = \dfrac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(R_{p_i} - R_{v_i})$ | Average of 5 consecutive RzDIN values | μm |
| 4 | **Rt** (Total Roughness) | $R_t = R_p(\max) - R_v(\min)$ | Maximum peak-to-valley over evaluation length | μm |
| 5 | **Rp** | $R_p = \max\bigl(y(x)\bigr) \text{ over L}$ | Maximum peak height above mean line | μm |
| 6 | **Rv** | $R_v = -\min\bigl(y(x)\bigr) \text{ over L}$ | Maximum valley depth below mean line | μm |
| 7 | **Rsk** (Skewness) | $R_{sk} = \dfrac{1}{R_q^3}\dfrac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} y^3(x)\, dx$ | Asymmetry of profile | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Rku** (Kurtosis) | $R_{ku} = \dfrac{1}{R_q^4}\dfrac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} y^4(x)\, dx$ | Peakedness of profile | dimensionless |
| 9 | **λc** (Cut-off) | $\lambda_c \approx 5 \cdot R_{max}$ | Selection rule (ISO 4288) | mm |
| 10 | **Sampling length ln** | $l_n = 5 \cdot \lambda_c$ | Per ISO 4288 | mm |
| 11 | **Evaluation length ln,e** | $l_{n,e} \geq 7 \cdot \lambda_c$ | Per ISO 4288 | mm |
| 12 | **Stylus tip correction** | $R_{measured} = R_{true} \cdot f(r_{tip})$ | Geometric error due to finite tip radius | μm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** In all KTU problems, Ra is expressed in **μm (micrometers)** unless otherwise stated. Always verify the unit before integration.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Cylinder bore finish (Ra 0.2–0.8 μm) directly affects piston ring wear and oil retention.
- **Aerospace turbine blades:** Surface roughness of compressor blades affects aerodynamic efficiency and fatigue life.
- **Bio-medical implants:** Hip joint femoral head finish (Ra < 0.01 μm) determines wear rate and biocompatibility.
- **Bearings manufacturing:** Raceway roughness (Ra 0.05–0.2 μm) governs L10 life of rolling element bearings.
- **Optical components:** Mirror substrates require sub-nanometer finish — profilograph serves as the QC workhorse.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Arithmetic Mean Roughness (Ra)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** KTU examiners often ask students to derive Ra from the recorded profile. Skipping steps costs 2–3 marks.

**Step 1 — Definition of Mean Line (Datum Line)**
The **mean line (m)** of a roughness profile is a line parallel to the general direction of the profile such that the sum of the squares of the deviations of the profile from this line over the sampling length is minimized.

Mathematically, the constraint on the mean line is:

$$
\int_{0}^{L} y(x)\, dx = 0
$$

This means the area enclosed above the mean line equals the area enclosed below it.

**Step 2 — Definition of Local Peak and Valley**
At any point x on the profile, the ordinate value y(x) represents the deviation from the mean line.
- A **peak** is a point where the profile changes from rising to falling.
- A **valley** is a point where the profile changes from falling to rising.

**Step 3 — Expressing Absolute Deviation**
To obtain the average absolute deviation, we take the absolute value of the ordinate:

$$
\text{Total absolute area} = \int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert\, dx
$$

**Step 4 — Averaging Over the Sampling Length**
Dividing the total absolute area by the sampling length L gives the arithmetic mean:

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert\, dx
$$

This is the **canonical definition of Ra** in ISO 4287.

**Step 5 — Discrete (Sampled) Form for ADC Implementation**
In a digital profilograph, the profile is sampled at N equally spaced points with spacing Δx, where N·Δx = L. The integral becomes a summation:

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i \vert
$$

This discrete form is what the microprocessor computes in real time.

**Step 6 — Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)**

Suppose a profile yields 6 sampled ordinate values (μm): **+1.2, −0.8, +1.5, −1.0, +0.6, −0.5**.

Step 6a — Sum of absolute values:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} \vert y_i \vert = 1.2 + 0.8 + 1.5 + 1.0 + 0.6 + 0.5 = 5.6 \, \mu m
$$

Step 6b — Divide by N = 6:

$$
R_a = \frac{5.6}{6} = 0.933 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation**
- **Ra = 0.933 μm** corresponds to an **N5 grade finish** (per ISO 1302).
- Such a finish is typical of **finely ground shafts** and **precision bearings**.
- Compared to a standard grade N6 (Ra 1.6 μm), this surface is smoother and will exhibit lower friction.

### 3.2 Derivation of RMS Roughness (Rq)

**Step 1 — Square the Ordinates**

$$
y^2(x) = \bigl[+1.2\bigr]^2 + \bigl[-0.8\bigr]^2 + \bigl[+1.5\bigr]^2 + \bigl[-1.0\bigr]^2 + \bigl[+0.6\bigr]^2 + \bigl[-0.5\bigr]^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Sum**

$$
\sum y_i^2 = 1.44 + 0.64 + 2.25 + 1.00 + 0.36 + 0.25 = 5.94 \, \mu m^2
$$

**Step 3 — Average**

$$
\overline{y^2} = \frac{5.94}{6} = 0.99 \, \mu m^2
$$

**Step 4 — Take the Square Root**

$$
R_q = \sqrt{0.99} = 0.995 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the Theoretical Relationship**
For any random profile, the theoretical ratio is:

$$
R_q \approx 1.11 \cdot R_a
$$

Check:

$$
1.11 \times 0.933 = 1.035 \, \mu m
$$

The slight difference (0.995 vs 1.035) arises because the profile is too short (N=6) for the statistical ratio to fully apply. With longer evaluation lengths, the ratio converges to **1.11**.

### 3.3 Derivation of Mean Peak-to-Valley Roughness (Rz)

**Step 1 — Identify the 5 Sampling Lengths**
For Rz calculation, the evaluation length is divided into **5 equal consecutive sampling lengths** (each of length ln = λc).

**Step 2 — Determine Peak-to-Valley for Each Segment**
For each segment i (i = 1 to 5), the peak-to-valley height is:

$$
R_{z_i} = R_{p_i} - R_{v_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Arithmetic Mean**

$$
R_z = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} R_{z_i}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Stylus Tip Geometric Error (Advanced)

When the stylus tip radius r is comparable to the surface feature radius, the recorded profile is distorted. The geometric correction for a hemispherical stylus of radius r tracing a notch of radius R is:

$$
\frac{R_{recorded}}{R_{true}} = \left(\frac{R_{true}}{R_{true} + r}\right)
$$

**Numerical Example:**
If true valley radius R = 5 μm and stylus tip radius r = 2 μm:

$$
\frac{R_{recorded}}{5} = \frac{5}{5 + 2} = \frac{5}{7} = 0.714
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_{recorded} = 5 \times 0.714 = 3.57 \, \mu m
$$

**Engineering Implication:** The measured valley is **wider and shallower** than reality. Choosing a stylus with r = 2 μm (instead of 10 μm) is mandatory for fine surfaces (Ra < 0.1 μm).

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Why Choose One Parameter Over Another?)

| Parameter | Best Used For | Mathematical Sensitivity | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ra** | General roughness, bearing surfaces, shafts | Insensitive to occasional peaks | **Very High** (1 mark, 3 mark, 14 mark) |
| **Rq** | Statistical / scientific work, fatigue studies | Sensitive to outliers | High |
| **Rz** | Sealing surfaces, paint adhesion, honed surfaces | Sensitive to extreme peaks/valleys | High |
| **Rt** | Failure analysis, scratches, defects | Captures the absolute worst case | Moderate |
| **Rsk / Rku** | Lubricant retention, bearing area, plateau honing | Statistical distribution shape | Low (conceptual only) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tracer Type Profilograph

The following Mermaid flowchart maps every signal-flow interaction from the workpiece surface to the digital output. It captures the architecture of a **modern LVDT-based digital profilograph** with both analog recording and digital display paths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Workpiece Surface"] --> B["Stylus Skid Assembly"]
    B --> C["Diamond Stylus Tip"]
    C --> D["LVDT Core Movement"]
    D --> E["LVDT Coils Primary"]
    E --> F["Oscillator 5 kHz Excitation"]
    F --> E
    D --> G["LVDT Coils Secondary S1 S2"]
    G --> H["Differential AC Output"]
    H --> I["AC Amplifier 60 to 100 dB"]
    I --> J["Phase Sensitive Demodulator"]
    J --> K["DC Signal Proportional to y"]
    K --> L["Low Pass Filter 2CR"]
    L --> M["Cut-off Wavelength Selector"]
    M --> N["Analog Output 0 to 10 V"]
    M --> O["Analog Strip Chart Recorder"]
    N --> P["Analog to Digital Converter ADC 16 bit"]
    P --> Q["Microprocessor DSP Unit"]
    Q --> R["Digital LCD Display"]
    Q --> S["PC Interface RS232 or USB"]
    Q --> T["Roughness Parameter Calculator"]
    T --> U["Ra Rq Rz Rt Rsk Rku Output"]
    M --> V["Long Wave Filter Waviness Path"]
    V --> W["Waviness Parameter Output"]
    H --> X["Calibration Reference Signal"]
    X --> Y["Phase Lock Loop PLL"]
    Y --> F
```

**Explanation of the Flowchart:**
- The **left half** represents the analog signal path: from mechanical contact (stylus) to electrical signal (DC voltage).
- The **right half** represents the digital processing path: ADC → DSP → parameters.
- The **bottom path** (waviness filter) demonstrates how the same transducer signal can yield waviness data by simply changing the filter cut-off.
- The **PLL feedback loop** ensures the oscillator frequency is locked to the calibration reference for zero-drift performance.

### 4.2 Detailed Subsystem: Stylus + Skid Mechanical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["Workpiece Surface"] --> A2["Skid Shoe Tungsten Carbide"]
    A2 --> A3["Skid follows waviness"]
    A1 --> A4["Diamond Stylus Tip r = 2 um"]
    A4 --> A5["Cantilever Arm Leaf Spring"]
    A5 --> A6["LVDT Ferrite Core"]
    A6 --> A7["LVDT Coil Bobbin"]
    A7 --> A8["Spring Loaded Preload 0.75 mN"]
    A5 --> A9["Damping Dashpot Oil Filled"]
    A8 --> A4
    A2 --> A10["Traverse Drive Lead Screw"]
    A10 --> A11["DC Servo Motor"]
    A11 --> A12["Constant Velocity Controller 0.5 mm per s"]
    A12 --> A11
    A3 --> A13["Filtered Roughness Signal Output"]
    A4 --> A13
```

**Explanation:**
- The **skid and stylus are kinematically coupled** — the skid absorbs long-wavelength waviness, leaving only roughness to be picked up by the stylus.
- The **leaf spring + dashpot** provide the constant low measuring force (0.75 mN) and critical damping to prevent stylus chatter at high traversing speeds.
- The **lead-screw + servo** ensures constant traverse speed, which is critical for accurate Rsm (mean spacing of profile elements) measurements.

### 4.3 Reference Geometry: Cut-off Wavelength vs. Surface Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Surface to be Measured"] --> B{"Estimate Ra Range"}
    B --> C["Ra 0.006 to 0.02 um"]
    B --> D["Ra 0.02 to 0.1 um"]
    B --> E["Ra 0.1 to 2.0 um"]
    B --> F["Ra 2.0 to 10.0 um"]
    B --> G["Ra 10 to 80 um"]
    C --> H["lambda c 0.08 mm"]
    D --> I["lambda c 0.25 mm"]
    E --> J["lambda c 0.8 mm"]
    F --> K["lambda c 2.5 mm"]
    G --> L["lambda c 8.0 mm"]
    H --> M["Sampling length 0.4 mm"]
    I --> N["Sampling length 1.25 mm"]
    J --> O["Sampling length 4.0 mm"]
    K --> P["Sampling length 12.5 mm"]
    L --> Q["Sampling length 40.0 mm"]
    M --> R["Evaluation length 0.56 mm"]
    N --> S["Evaluation length 1.75 mm"]
    O --> T["Evaluation length 5.6 mm"]
    P --> U["Evaluation length 17.5 mm"]
    Q --> V["Evaluation length 56.0 mm"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 4288 Selection Rule (Mandatory for KTU 14-mark problems):**
> Always choose the **largest possible cut-off λc** that does not exceed **5 × Rmax** of the surface, so that the measured profile is statistically representative.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define a Tracer Type Profilograph. Mention any two industrial applications.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A Tracer Type Profilograph is a contact-type instrument that uses a diamond-tipped stylus drawn across a workpiece surface at constant speed. The vertical displacement of the stylus, which follows the surface micro-geometry, is converted into an electrical signal by a transducer (LVDT / piezoelectric) and is filtered, amplified, and recorded to yield a magnified surface profile from which roughness parameters like Ra, Rz, and Rq are computed.

**Applications:**
1. Quality control of **cylinder bores** in automotive engines (Ra 0.2–0.8 μm).
2. Inspection of **bearing raceway finish** in rolling element bearings.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**

**Q2. List the major components of a tracer type profilograph and state the function of the Gaussian filter.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
**Major Components:**
1. Diamond stylus + skid assembly
2. Transducer (LVDT / Piezoelectric)
3. AC amplifier
4. Phase-sensitive demodulator
5. Gaussian filter
6. Recorder / Digital display
7. Traverse drive (lead screw + servo motor)

**Function of the Gaussian Filter:**
The Gaussian filter (per ISO 16610) separates the **roughness profile** from the **waviness profile** and **form error** by removing long-wavelength components below a defined **cut-off wavelength λc**. It uses a -50% transmission characteristic with a transmission band that satisfies ISO 11562.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a tracer type profilograph**. Discuss the role of each block in detail. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Define the following surface roughness parameters with proper mathematical expressions: **Ra, Rq, Rz, and Rt**. State the unit and physical meaning of each. *(7 Marks)*

**OR**

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the **construction and working of an LVDT-based tracer type profilograph** with a neat schematic. Explain how the LVDT generates a signal proportional to stylus displacement. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **significance of cut-off filters** in surface roughness measurement. How is the cut-off wavelength λc selected per ISO 4288 for different surface roughness ranges? *(7 Marks)*

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions for Part B

#### Solution to Question A(a) — Working Principle (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The tracer type profilograph consists of the following stages, each playing a critical role:

1. **Stylus + Skid Assembly:** A diamond stylus of tip radius **2 μm** is mounted on a leaf-spring-loaded cantilever. A **skid** (tungsten carbide shoe) provides a moving datum by riding on the surface itself, filtering out long-wavelength waviness. The skid-to-stylus vertical separation is fixed (typically a few mm), so any relative motion between the skid (following waviness) and the stylus (following roughness) gives a clean roughness signal.

2. **LVDT Transducer:** The cantilever is coupled to a **ferrite core** that slides inside an LVDT. The LVDT is excited by a stable **5 kHz oscillator**. The differential output of the two secondary coils is zero at the central (null) position and grows linearly with displacement, with phase indicating direction. This converts μm-level mechanical motion into an AC voltage.

3. **AC Amplifier (60–100 dB):** The millivolt-level LVDT output is amplified to a few volts.

4. **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD):** Rectifies the AC signal, producing a DC voltage whose **magnitude** is proportional to displacement and whose **polarity** indicates direction (up or down).

5. **Gaussian Filter:** Applies the ISO 16610 transmission characteristic. For a chosen cut-off λc, all spatial wavelengths longer than λc are attenuated, isolating the **roughness component** (typically 0.08–8 mm band).

6. **Recorder / Digital Display:** The filtered DC signal drives an **analog strip-chart** recorder (X-axis = traverse distance, Y-axis = stylus height) and/or is digitized by a 16-bit ADC for digital display of **Ra, Rq, Rz, Rt** values.

7. **Constant-Velocity Traverse:** A **DC servo motor + lead screw** drives the skid-stylus assembly across the surface at a precise constant speed (0.5 or 1.0 mm/s), ensuring Rsm accuracy.

**Key Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.

#### Solution to Question A(b) — Roughness Parameter Definitions (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Ra — Arithmetic Mean Roughness (ISO 4287):**

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L}\vert y(x) \vert \, dx
$$

It is the **arithmetic average of the absolute values** of profile ordinates y(x) measured from the mean line over the sampling length L. It is the most widely used parameter due to its **statistical stability** and **linearity** with respect to profile shape. Unit: **μm**.

**2. Rq — Root Mean Square Roughness:**

$$
R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} y^2(x)\, dx}
$$

Also called **RMS roughness**. It gives more weight to large deviations (peaks and valleys) and is preferred in **fatigue and statistical analyses**. For a Gaussian random profile, **Rq ≈ 1.11 · Ra**. Unit: **μm**.

**3. Rz — Mean Peak-to-Valley Height (ISO 4287):**

$$
R_z = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(R_{p_i} - R_{v_i})
$$

It is the **arithmetic mean of the 5 largest peak-to-valley heights** measured over 5 consecutive sampling lengths. Rz is highly sensitive to extreme values and is preferred for **sealing surfaces, honed cylinder bores, and paint adhesion tests**. Unit: **μm**.

**4. Rt — Total Roughness:**

$$
R_t = R_p(\max) - R_v(\min)
$$

It is the **maximum vertical distance between the highest peak and the deepest valley** over the entire evaluation length. Rt captures the **absolute worst case** and is used in **failure analysis** to detect scratches, burrs, or single-event defects. Unit: **μm**.

#### Solution to Question B(a) — LVDT-Based Profilograph (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
1. **Stylus assembly:** Diamond tip (r = 2 μm) on a leaf-spring cantilever with a tungsten carbide skid.
2. **LVDT:** A cylindrical LVDT with one primary coil and two identical secondaries (S1 and S2) wound symmetrically, with a movable ferrite core attached to the cantilever.
3. **Oscillator:** A stable 5 kHz sine-wave oscillator feeding the primary.
4. **Amplifier + Demodulator + Filter + Recorder.**

**Working:**
- When the stylus is on a flat surface, the ferrite core is at the **null position**, and the two secondary EMFs (E1, E2) are equal, producing a **net differential output of 0 V**.
- When the stylus rises over a peak, the core moves **upward**, increasing coupling with S1 (upper) and decreasing with S2 (lower). The differential output **E1 − E2** becomes **positive** with magnitude proportional to displacement.
- When the stylus falls into a valley, the core moves **downward**, reversing the polarity of the differential output.
- The AC differential output is amplified, phase-sensitively demodulated (to recover direction), filtered, and displayed.

**Schematic:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the complete LVDT sub-system block.

#### Solution to Question B(b) — Cut-off Filter Significance (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Significance:**
The raw profile measured by the stylus contains three super-imposed components:
1. **Form error** (very long wavelength, e.g., out-of-roundness)
2. **Waviness** (intermediate wavelength, e.g., tool chatter marks)
3. **Roughness** (short wavelength, the parameter of interest)

The **cut-off filter** mathematically separates roughness from waviness. Without filtering, the recorded Ra value would depend on the evaluation length — making measurements non-reproducible between laboratories.

**Selection Rule per ISO 4288:**

| Estimated Ra Range (μm) | Cut-off λc (mm) | Sampling Length ln (mm) | Evaluation Length (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.006 – 0.02 | 0.08 | 0.4 | 0.56 |
| 0.02 – 0.1 | 0.25 | 1.25 | 1.75 |
| 0.1 – 2.0 | 0.8 | 4.0 | 5.6 |
| 2.0 – 10.0 | 2.5 | 12.5 | 17.5 |
| 10.0 – 80.0 | 8.0 | 40.0 | 56.0 |

The choice rule: **λc ≈ 5 × Rmax**, where Rmax is the estimated maximum peak-to-valley height.

**Why Gaussian?** The Gaussian filter (per ISO 16610) is the only filter that does not introduce phase distortion at the cut-off. It is the **only filter permitted for international trade and certification** per ISO standards.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write **μm** after Ra, Rq, Rz, Rt values. Missing units = −0.5 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Rz (ISO) with RzDIN:** The older German standard (DIN) uses a single sampling length with 5 highest peaks/valleys. Modern ISO uses 5 sampling lengths. Specifying which one is mandatory.
> 3. **Skipping the mean-line condition:** When deriving Ra, you must first state that $\int y(x) dx = 0$ over the mean line. Skipping this = −1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong stylus tip radius:** Never assume a "sharp" stylus. The KTU textbook uses **2 μm** as the standard. For soft materials, 5 μm or 10 μm is used.
> 5. **Forgetting the 5×λc rule:** Sampling length is **5 × cut-off**, not equal to cut-off. This is the most common single-mark loss.
> 6. **Confusing Rq with Rz:** Rq is RMS (square root of mean square), Rz is mean peak-to-valley. Examiners will catch this swap instantly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Revision — KTU 2024 Module 4: Tracer Type Profilograph**

- [x] **Tracer Type Profilograph = Contact-type, stylus-based surface roughness instrument.**
- [x] Working: Stylus → Transducer (LVDT) → Amplifier → Demodulator → Filter → Recorder.
- [x] **Diamond stylus tip radius = 2 μm (standard)**, 5 μm or 10 μm for soft surfaces.
- [x] Static measuring force ≈ **0.75 mN**.
- [x] Traverse speed = **0.5 mm/s or 1.0 mm/s** (constant).
- [x] **Ra** = Arithmetic mean of absolute ordinate deviations → most common parameter.
- [x] **Rq** = RMS roughness → for statistical / fatigue analysis → **Rq ≈ 1.11 Ra** for random profiles.
- [x] **Rz** = Mean of 5 peak-to-valley values over 5 sampling lengths → for sealing surfaces.
- [x] **Rt** = Maximum peak-to-valley over evaluation length → for failure analysis.
- [x] **Cut-off wavelength λc** = 0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 mm (per ISO 4288).
- [x] **Sampling length = 5 × λc**.
- [x] **Evaluation length ≥ 7 × λc**.
- [x] **Gaussian filter** is the only ISO-permitted filter (ISO 16610).
- [x] Standards to remember: **ISO 3274, ISO 4287, ISO 4288, ISO 11562, ISO 16610, ANSI B46.1**.
- [x] **LVDT null position** gives zero differential output — every other position gives a polarity-coded signal.
- [x] The **skid absorbs long-wavelength waviness**, so the stylus reads only roughness.
- [x] **Advantages:** High accuracy, direct measurement, traceable to ISO, computes multiple parameters.
- [x] **Limitations:** Contact method (not for soft / fragile surfaces), 2D line measurement only, stylus wear.
- [x] In every KTU 14-mark answer, **draw the block diagram** — it is worth at least 3–4 marks.
- [x] Always mention **ISO 4287 / 4288** as the governing standard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Tooth Measurement: Tooth Thickness and Tooth Spacing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT416 - MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gear Tooth Measurement: Tooth Thickness and Tooth Spacing

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Gear Tooth Measurement?

**Gear tooth measurement** is the branch of **dimensional metrology** that quantifies the geometric integrity of gear teeth — the most stressed, highest-load-conveying features in a power transmission system. Because a single bad tooth can cause catastrophic failure of an entire gearbox, the KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes these measurements as a **critical quality gate** in mechanical instrumentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Gear tooth measurement is the process of quantifying the linear and angular dimensions of individual gear teeth (thickness, spacing, profile, lead, and runout) using specialized contact and non-contact instruments, in order to ensure the gear conforms to its designed module, pressure angle, and accuracy grade.*

The two parameters covered in this module are:

1. **Tooth Thickness** — how *thick* each tooth is (the arc length along the pitch circle).
2. **Tooth Spacing** — how *far apart* consecutive teeth are (the pitch).

## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy — "The Bookshelf Problem"

Imagine a carpenter is building a **bookshelf** with **50 identical wooden slats** evenly spaced. The shelf is only useful if:

- Every **slat is the same width** (analogous to **tooth thickness**).
- The **gap between consecutive slats** is identical throughout the shelf (analogous to **tooth spacing**).

If even *one* slat is 0.5 mm wider than the rest, the final slat won't fit. Similarly, if gear tooth thickness varies, the gear will not **mesh uniformly**, producing **noise, vibration, and premature wear**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** A typical automotive gearbox contains 5–6 gears, each with 15–40 teeth, and operates at 8,000–12,000 RPM. Sub-millimeter thickness errors propagate into the **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness)** performance of the entire vehicle.

## 1.3 Key Nomenclature (KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight)

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $m$ | Module (ISO/AGMA standard tooth size) | mm |
| $z$ | Number of teeth | — |
| $d$ | Pitch circle diameter | mm |
| $p$ | Circular pitch | mm |
| $t$ | Circular tooth thickness (at pitch circle) | mm |
| $s$ | Space width (at pitch circle) | mm |
| $\alpha$ | Pressure angle (standard: 20° or 14.5°) | degrees |
| $d_b$ | Base circle diameter | mm |
| $a$ | Addendum (standard: 1 module) | mm |

The fundamental geometric relationship is:

$$d = m \cdot z$$

where the **circular pitch** $p$ and **circular tooth thickness** $t$ are defined as:

$$p = \frac{\pi d}{z} = \pi m$$

$$t = \frac{p}{2} = \frac{\pi m}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rule of thumb (for a perfect involute gear):** Tooth thickness at the pitch circle equals space width at the pitch circle, and both equal **half the circular pitch** ($\pi m/2$).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Tooth Thickness Measurements

Tooth thickness is never measured directly on the involute curve (that requires expensive **profile-measuring projectors** or **CGMs — Coordinate Gear Measuring machines**). Instead, it is inferred by measuring a *chord* or *span* across the tooth, and then converting the chord/span to the corresponding **circular tooth thickness at the pitch circle** via geometry.

The two industrial-standard approaches are:

### A. Chordal Thickness Method (Geometric Projection)
- The tooth is held between two parallel **measuring jaws** (discs or anvils).
- The **chordal tooth thickness** $\bar{t}$ is read directly.
- Also requires reporting the **chordal addendum** $\bar{a}$ (height of the chord from the tip circle).
- Used with a **Gear Tooth Vernier Caliper** (the most common workshop tool).

### B. Measurement-Over-Pins / Measurement-Over-Wires (Span Method)
- Two **precision cylinders (pins or wires)** of known diameter are placed in diametrically opposite tooth spaces.
- The **distance over pins** $M$ is measured with a **micrometer** or **supermicrometer**.
- This is the most accurate workshop method (resolution: 0.001 mm).

### C. Base-Tangent / Span-Micrometer Method (Tooth Spacing)
- Used to measure the **accumulated spacing error** across multiple teeth.
- Uses a **floating-carriage micrometer** or **gear pitch tester**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains ALL the formulas you need to solve any Module 4 question from this topic. Memorize the right column first, then derive the rest.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Constants |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Pitch circle diameter | $d = m z$ | Basic module equation |
| 2 | Base circle diameter | $d_b = d \cos \alpha$ | $\alpha = 20°$ unless stated |
| 3 | Circular pitch | $p = \pi m$ | Distance between adjacent teeth on pitch circle |
| 4 | Circular tooth thickness | $t = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}$ | Standard for zero-backlash ideal gear |
| 5 | Addendum (full depth) | $a = 1\,m$ | Standard stub-tooth would be $0.8\,m$ |
| 6 | Dedendum | $b = 1.25\,m$ | Includes bottom clearance $0.25\,m$ |
| 7 | Outside (tip) diameter | $d_o = m(z + 2)$ | Used to compute $\bar{a}$ |
| 8 | Chordal thickness | $\bar{t} = t - \dfrac{a^{2} \sin \alpha}{d_b}$ | Where $a$ = addendum |
| 9 | Chordal addendum | $\bar{a} = a + \dfrac{a \cos \alpha}{2}$ | Height from tip to chord |
| 10 | Measurement over pins (even teeth) | $M = \dfrac{d_b}{\cos \alpha} + d_p \csc \alpha - p \cot \alpha$ | "Best size" pin $d_p = 1.476\,m$ for $\alpha = 20°$ |
| 11 | Measurement over pins (odd teeth) | $M_{\text{odd}} = \dfrac{d \cos \alpha}{\cos^2 \phi} + d_p \dfrac{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos \alpha \cos \phi}$ | Where $\tan \phi = \dfrac{\pi}{2z}$ (approx.) |
| 12 | Span (n teeth) | $L_n = (n-1)\,p$ | Ideal span; actual measured for error |

> **Note on the "|" symbol:** In the formulas above, division bars and parentheses are used instead of vertical bars to avoid markdown table syntax conflicts. Mathematically equivalent.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Chordal vs. Span Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need two different methods?**
> - **Chordal method** → *fast*, used on the shop floor, requires access to the **tip circle** (so the gear must be free-standing, not pressed onto a shaft).
> - **Span method** → *highly accurate* (1 µm), can be done on **assembled gears on a shaft**, and inherently averages out pitch-error variations. This is the KTU-favoured question type for 14-mark problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Applications

| Industry | Why Gear Tooth Measurement is Critical |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Automotive** | Gear whine, fuel efficiency, EV gearbox NVH |
| **Aerospace** | Helicopter transmission, accessory-drive gears (FAA-certified tolerances) |
| **Robotics** | Harmonic-drive gear backlash |
| **Wind Energy** | Planetary gearbox reliability under cyclic loading |
| **Machine Tools** | CNC rack-and-pinion positioning accuracy |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Circular Tooth Thickness

Starting from the definition of **circular pitch** as the arc length between corresponding points on two adjacent teeth, measured along the pitch circle:

The pitch circle has circumference $C = \pi d$, and there are exactly $z$ teeth (and $z$ spaces) around it. The arc length occupied by one tooth-plus-one-space is:

$$p = \frac{\pi d}{z}$$
