### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the **construction and working** of the Parkinson Gear Tester.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a Parkinson Gear Test, the test gear has **module = 5 mm, 18 teeth**, the lever ratio is **8:1**, the applied dead weight is **6 kgf**, the test speed is **180 rpm**, and the coefficient of friction is **0.05**. Calculate the **tangential mesh force**, the **pitch-line sliding velocity**, and the **frictional power loss**.

##### **Model Solution (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)**

*(Expected answer outline — any equivalent wording is acceptable.)*

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Two identical spur gears (one driving, one driven) are mounted on parallel shafts in a sealed housing.
- A 1-HP single-phase induction motor drives the system through a **worm and worm-wheel reducer** for speed reduction.
- A **loading lever** pivoted on a knife-edge transmits a dead-weight to the driven gear shaft.
- A **revolution counter** records the total number of rotations.
- An **oil drip feed** provides boundary lubrication to the mesh.

**Working (4 marks):**
- Both gears are cleaned, weighed, and meshed.
- A known weight is applied via the lever, producing a tangential mesh force $F_t$ on the tooth flank.
- The motor is switched ON; the gears rotate under combined rolling and sliding contact.
- After a fixed number of revolutions (e.g., 50,000 to 100,000), the gears are removed, cleaned, and re-weighed.
- The **weight loss** is the wear. Tests are repeated at progressively higher loads to determine the **load-carrying capacity**.

##### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $m = 5$ mm, $z = 18$, $L_1/L_2 = 8$, $W = 6$ kgf, $N = 180$ rpm, $\mu = 0.05$

**Step 1 — Pitch diameter:**
$$
d = m \cdot z = 5 \times 18 = 90 \text{ mm} = 0.09 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Tangential mesh force:**
$$
F_t = W \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_2} = 6 \times 8 = 48 \text{ kgf}
$$

Converting to SI: $F_t = 48 \times 9.81 = 470.88 \text{ N}$.

**Step 3 — Pitch-line velocity:**
$$
v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.09 \times 180}{60}
$$

$$
v = \dfrac{50.894}{60} = 0.8482 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Frictional force:**
$$
F_f = \mu \cdot F_t = 0.05 \times 470.88 = 23.544 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Frictional power loss:**
$$
P_f = F_f \cdot v = 23.544 \times 0.8482 = 19.97 \text{ W}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $F_t = 470.88$ N  ≈  **471 N**
- $v = 0.848$ m/s  ≈  **0.85 m/s**
- $P_f = 19.97$ W  ≈  **20 W**

*(1 mark for given data + 1 for d + 1 for F_t + 1 for v + 1 for F_f + 1 for P_f + 1 for unit consistency = 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set C]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **various stages of wear** observed on gear teeth tested in a Parkinson Gear Tester, with the help of a load-vs-wear-rate sketch.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A test gear of initial mass **120.4521 g** lost **28.6 mg** after running for **80,000 revolutions** under a tangential load of **300 N** and pitch-line velocity of **0.9 m/s**. Compute (i) the **wear rate in mg/10³ rev**, (ii) the **volume loss in mm³**, and (iii) the **specific wear rate in mm³/(N·m)**. Take $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³.

##### **Model Solution (a) — Stages of Wear (7 marks)**

*(Expected answer outline:)*

As load is progressively increased, the gear tooth surface goes through the following wear stages:

1. **Mild Adhesive Wear / Polishing** — A thin, smooth, polished layer forms on the flank. Wear rate is low and nearly constant. *(1 mark)*
2. **Abrasive Wear / Scratching** — Micro-plowing of the surface by asperities or debris. Wear rate begins to rise. *(1 mark)*
3. **Plastic Deformation / Rippling** — Surface yields plastically; ripples appear near the dedendum. Wear rate rises sharply. *(1 mark)*
4. **Scuffing** — Localized welding and tearing due to adhesive seizure. Severe surface damage. *(1 mark)*
5. **Pitting / Spalling** — Subsurface fatigue cracks propagate; small craters break out from the surface. *(1 mark)*
6. **Tooth Fracture / Test Termination** — The load-carrying capacity is exceeded. The wear-rate curve shoots up vertically. *(1 mark)*

**Sketch (1 mark):** A graph of *Wear rate $W_r$ on Y-axis* vs. *Tangential load $F_t$ on X-axis* — initially linear, then bending upward sharply at the critical load $P_c$.

##### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $m_i = 120.4521$ g, $\Delta m = 28.6$ mg, $N = 80{,}000$ rev, $F_t = 300$ N, $v = 0.9$ m/s, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Wear rate per 1000 revolutions:**
$$
W_r = \dfrac{\Delta m}{N} \times 10^3 = \dfrac{28.6}{80{,}000} \times 10^3 = 0.3575 \text{ mg/10}^3 \text{ rev}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_r \approx 0.358 \text{ mg/10}^3 \text{ rev}}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume loss:**
$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho} = \dfrac{28.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg}}{7850 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 3.643 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3
$$

Converting to mm³: $1$ m³ $= 10^9$ mm³, so:
$$
\Delta V = 3.643 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Sliding distance:**
$$
s = \pi \cdot d \cdot N \;\; \text{(but the standard form, since v is given directly:)}
$$

Using $s = v \cdot t$ where $t$ is the total test time:

First, total test time:
$$
t = \dfrac{N}{N_{\text{rpm}}} \times 60 = \dfrac{80{,}000}{N_{\text{rpm}}} \times 60 \text{ s}
$$

But the **standard KTU convention** is to use the **pitch circumference × total revolutions**:
$$
s = \pi d N \;\; \text{(requires } d\text{)}
$$

Since $v = 0.9$ m/s and $N = 80{,}000$ rev at 180 rpm (assume $N_{\text{rpm}}$ given implicitly):

A **simpler equivalent** that KTU accepts is:
$$
s = \pi d N
$$

But since $v$ is given, we can extract $d$ from $v = \pi d N_{\text{rpm}} / 60$ — without $N_{\text{rpm}}$ given, we use the **direct sliding distance = pitch circumference × revolutions** and assume a **standard pitch diameter of 95.5 mm** (typical Parkinson test gear) or state $d = v \cdot 60 / (\pi \cdot N_{\text{rpm}})$.

**Simpler KTU-Accepted Approach:** If the examiner does not give $N_{\text{rpm}}$, use:
$$
s_{\text{per rev}} = \pi d \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; s_{\text{total}} = \pi d N
$$

But we can rewrite in terms of $v$ using the test time. With $N_{\text{rpm}}$ typically 180 rpm in Parkinson test:
$$
t = \dfrac{80{,}000}{180} \times 60 = 26{,}667 \text{ s}
$$
$$
s = v \cdot t = 0.9 \times 26{,}667 = 24{,}000 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Specific wear rate:**
$$
K = \dfrac{\Delta V}{F_t \cdot s} = \dfrac{3.643}{300 \times 24{,}000} = 5.06 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mm}^3/(\text{N} \cdot \text{m})
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $W_r = 0.358$ mg/10³ rev
- $\Delta V = 3.643$ mm³
- $K = 5.06 \times 10^{-7}$ mm³/(N·m)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap (–2 marks typical):** Many students write $F_t$ in kgf and $v$ in m/s, then compute $P_f$ in nonsensical units. Always convert $W$ to **N** first via $W \times 9.81$.
> 2. **Sliding distance oversight (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often confuse **sliding distance $s$** with **rolling distance**. For gear teeth, use $s = \pi d N$ — not the contact length.
> 3. **Density confusion (–1 mark):** The density $\rho$ must be in **kg/m³** when $\Delta m$ is in **kg**. Mixing CGS and SI leads to answers off by $10^3$ or $10^6$.
> 4. **Forgetting lever ratio direction (–1 mark):** $L_1/L_2 = 10$ means a *10× mechanical advantage* — a 5 kgf weight gives 50 kgf at the gear. Reversing the ratio silently under-loads the test.
> 5. **Skipping the schematic (–2 marks):** In construction questions, **a labeled diagram is mandatory**. Even a rough sketch with the motor, lever, gears, and weight pan earns 2 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Bookmark this section. Read it 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ Parkinson's Gear Tester is a **back-to-back gear tribometer** — used for **load-carrying capacity and wear rate** determination.
- ✅ Two **identical** spur gears, module 4–5 mm, ~18–20 teeth are the standard specimens.
- ✅ Loading is via a **dead-weight and lever** — tangential force $F_t = W \cdot L_1/L_2$.
- ✅ Drive is via **motor + worm-worm wheel reducer** at constant speed (≈150–200 rpm).
- ✅ The **wear metric** is **weight loss $\Delta m$** in mg, measured on an **analytical balance** to 0.1 mg precision.
- ✅ **Wear rate** is reported in **mg/1000 revolutions**.
- ✅ Pitch-line velocity $v = \pi d N / 60$ in m/s.
- ✅ Frictional power loss $P_f = \mu F_t v$ in Watts.
- ✅ **Specific wear rate** $K = \Delta V / (F_t \cdot s)$ is a material property — independent of test rig.
- ✅ Wear progresses in **6 stages**: Polishing → Scratching → Rippling → Scuffing → Pitting → Spalling/Fracture.
- ✅ The **load-carrying capacity** is the **critical load** where the wear-rate curve turns sharply upward on a $W_r$ vs. $F_t$ plot.
- ✅ Always state **units** in the final answer. KTU deducts 1 mark per missing unit.
- ✅ Convert **all weights to Newtons** using $g = 9.81$ m/s² before substituting into SI formulas.
- ✅ For numerical problems: write **Given → Formula → Substitution → Final Answer with units** in four clearly separated steps.
- ✅ Common errors to avoid: confusing sliding vs. rolling distance, mixing CGS and SI density, omitting the lever ratio in $F_t$ calculation.
